syllabus_desc: Define professionalism in aviation.
concept: Professionalism in aviation goes well beyond turning up in a pressed uniform. At CPL level, you're being paid to fly--and that changes the game. Professionalism means consistently meeting high standards of conduct, knowledge, and skill, even when nobody's watching. It's about thorough preparation, honest self-assessment, and never cutting corners because you're running behind schedule or the boss wants the flight to go. A professional pilot maintains currency, stays fit to fly, keeps their knowledge sharp, and treats every flight--whether it's a scenic over Milford Sound or a freight run to Chatham Islands--with the same level of care. It also means ethical behaviour: not falsifying logbooks, not flying when medically unfit, and speaking up when something's not right. Professionalism is what separates someone who holds a CPL from someone who deserves to hold one.
exam_tip: Define professionalism as consistent adherence to high standards of conduct, knowledge, and skill in all aspects of aviation operations. Examiners want more than 'being professional'--give specifics.
key_facts: Professionalism encompasses conduct, knowledge, skill, and ethical behaviour
Includes thorough preparation, honest self-assessment, and maintaining currency
Applies equally to all flights regardless of type or commercial pressure
Ethical obligations include honest record-keeping and reporting fitness to fly
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between piloting for personal reasons and for hire or reward.
concept: When you're flying for personal reasons--a weekend trip to Tauranga, a bit of sightseeing--you're operating under your own risk tolerance and your own schedule. Nobody's paying you, so the only pressure is self-imposed. Flying for hire or reward is fundamentally different. You've got a duty of care to your passengers or cargo operator, commercial pressures from employers or clients, regulatory obligations under Part 135 or Part 119, and the legal implications of being a professional. The distinction matters because commercial operations demand higher standards: more rigorous maintenance schedules, stricter weather minima, proper risk assessments, and formal SOPs. You can't just 'give it a go' when someone's paying you to get them somewhere. The CAA recognises this by requiring a CPL and a medical to a higher standard. The trap is when commercial pressure erodes your personal minimums--that's where accidents happen.
exam_tip: The key distinction is duty of care and commercial pressure. Personal flying = self-regulated risk. Hire or reward = regulated duty of care with higher standards and external pressures.
key_facts: Personal flying involves self-imposed risk tolerance and schedule flexibility
Hire or reward operations carry a legal duty of care to passengers and cargo
Commercial operations require higher maintenance, weather, and operational standards
Commercial pressure can erode personal safety minimums--a major accident factor
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, NZ CARs Part 1 definitions, Part 135 Air Operations

syllabus_desc: List the people to whom a pilot is responsible in carrying out his or her duties.
concept: As a commercial pilot, your responsibility web is wider than you might think. You're responsible to your passengers first and foremost--their safety is the non-negotiable priority. You're responsible to your employer or operator, who trusts you to conduct flights safely and efficiently within the company's procedures. You're responsible to the CAA as the regulator--they issued your licence on the condition you operate within the rules. You're responsible to other airspace users--every pilot sharing the sky relies on everyone else following procedures. You're responsible to the general public on the ground. And you're responsible to your crew, if you have one. In a broader sense, you're also responsible to the aviation industry itself--one cowboy pilot tarnishes the profession for everyone. When you're making decisions in the cockpit, remember that long list of people counting on you to get it right.
exam_tip: List at least five groups: passengers, employer/operator, CAA, other airspace users, general public, crew members. Some syllabi also include 'the aviation industry' as a broader responsibility.
key_facts: Passengers -- primary duty of care for their safety and wellbeing
Employer/operator -- safe and efficient conduct within SOPs
CAA -- compliance with regulations and conditions of licence
Other airspace users, crew members, and the general public on the ground
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe key features of good and safe airmanship.
concept: Good airmanship at CPL level builds on everything you learnt for your PPL, but the stakes are higher because you're operating commercially. The key features include: thorough pre-flight planning that accounts for weather, NOTAMs, fuel reserves, and alternates; disciplined adherence to SOPs and checklists even when you've done the same flight a hundred times; continuous risk assessment throughout every phase of flight; maintaining situational awareness--knowing where you are, what the weather's doing, how much fuel you've got, and what your options are if things go sideways. Good airmanship also means knowing your personal limits and the aircraft's limits, and never letting commercial pressure push you past either. It's staying current, staying humble, and staying alert. The best commercial pilots are the ones who make it look routine precisely because they've prepared for every contingency.
exam_tip: Frame airmanship as a combination of knowledge, skill, and attitude. Mention specific habits: thorough planning, SOP adherence, continuous risk assessment, knowing personal limits, and resisting commercial pressure.
key_facts: Thorough pre-flight planning covering weather, NOTAMs, fuel, alternates
Disciplined adherence to SOPs and checklists on every flight
Continuous risk assessment and situational awareness throughout all phases
Knowing personal and aircraft limits; resisting commercial pressure to exceed them
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight

syllabus_desc: Define human factors as used in a professional aviation context.
concept: Human factors in a professional aviation context is the applied science of understanding how people interact with aircraft systems, procedures, other people, and the environment--and designing operations to account for normal human limitations. At CPL level, this goes beyond the PPL definition because you're now operating in a professional system with crews, schedules, SOPs, and organisational pressures. Human factors encompasses everything from cockpit design and checklist layout to crew communication, fatigue management, and organisational safety culture. The goal isn't to eliminate human error--that's impossible--but to design systems that catch errors before they cause harm, and to train people to manage threats and errors effectively. When you're flying commercially out of Christchurch in winter, human factors is what helps you recognise that your fatigue from an early start, combined with deteriorating weather and a tight schedule, creates a risk picture that needs managing.
exam_tip: At CPL level, define human factors as the science of optimising human performance and managing human limitations within professional aviation systems. Include the system perspective--people, machines, procedures, environment, and organisation.
key_facts: The applied science of human performance and limitations in aviation systems
Encompasses people, machines, procedures, environment, and organisational factors
Aims to manage human error through system design, not elimination
Professional context adds crew dynamics, schedules, SOPs, and organisational pressures
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, ICAO Doc 9683 Human Factors Training Manual

syllabus_desc: Describe the fundamentals of the SHELL Model in relation to the interaction of humans with other humans, hardware, information sources, and the environment.
concept: The SHELL Model (sometimes written SHEL) was developed by Edwards and refined by Hawkins. It puts the human--Liveware--at the centre, surrounded by four interfaces: Software (procedures, checklists, regulations, symbology), Hardware (aircraft controls, displays, seats, cockpit layout), Environment (weather, terrain, noise, temperature, time of day), and Liveware (other people--crew, ATC, engineers, passengers). The model's power is in the interfaces between L and each other component. A mismatch at any interface creates a potential failure point. For example, L-H mismatch: a throttle quadrant designed for large hands that a smaller pilot struggles with. L-S mismatch: an SOP that's written ambiguously, causing two crew members to interpret it differently. L-E mismatch: flying into Queenstown at dusk with turbulence and rain degrading your performance. L-L mismatch: a captain and first officer who don't communicate effectively due to a steep authority gradient. The edges of the L block in the centre are jagged, representing the fact that humans are variable and imperfect--unlike hardware, we don't perform the same way every time.
exam_tip: Know all four interfaces and give a concrete aviation example for each. Remember: the central Liveware block has irregular edges because human performance varies--that's the whole point of the model.
key_facts: S = Software (procedures, rules, checklists), H = Hardware (physical aircraft systems)
E = Environment (weather, terrain, noise), L = Liveware (humans in the system)
Liveware is central; mismatches at any L-S, L-H, L-E, or L-L interface create risk
The irregular edges of the L block represent human variability and imperfection
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Edwards SHEL Model 1972

syllabus_desc: Explain the role of human factors programmes in promoting aviation safety in flight operations requiring a commercial pilot licence.
concept: Human factors programmes in commercial operations exist because understanding human limitations only helps if that knowledge is systematically applied. A good HF programme includes initial and recurrent CRM training for all crew, fatigue risk management systems (FRMS), incident and hazard reporting systems that encourage open reporting without blame, safety management systems (SMS) with HF components, and regular safety audits that look at human performance issues. For CPL operations, this means your employer should have formal processes for managing crew fatigue, assessing fitness to fly, standardising procedures, and learning from incidents. The CAA requires operators to incorporate HF principles into their training and operations under Part 119 and associated rules. Without a structured programme, human factors knowledge stays theoretical--the programme is what turns understanding into action. Think of it as the difference between knowing fatigue is dangerous and actually having roster rules that prevent it.
exam_tip: Link HF programmes to specific elements: CRM training, FRMS, SMS, incident reporting, and safety audits. Show how they translate knowledge into operational practice.
key_facts: HF programmes include CRM training, FRMS, SMS, incident reporting, and safety audits
Required under NZ CARs for commercial operators (Part 119, Part 135)
Turn theoretical HF knowledge into operational practice and procedures
Focus on systemic safety improvements rather than individual blame
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, ICAO Doc 9683, NZ CARs Part 119

syllabus_desc: Describe the importance of an effective human factors programme in operations requiring a commercial pilot licence.
concept: An effective HF programme isn't just a regulatory box to tick--it's what keeps your operation safe when the pressure's on. Commercial operations face pressures that private flying doesn't: tight schedules, revenue targets, customer expectations, and the fatigue of repetitive flying. Without an effective HF programme, these pressures erode safety margins quietly, one small compromise at a time. The importance shows up in the statistics: the vast majority of aviation accidents involve human factors as a contributing cause. An effective programme reduces incidents by: training crews to communicate effectively and manage workload, establishing reporting cultures where people flag problems early, managing fatigue through science-based rostering, standardising procedures so everyone does things the same way, and continuously learning from incidents and near-misses. For a small NZ operator running scenic flights out of Queenstown or freight into remote strips, an HF programme is what prevents the gradual normalisation of deviance--where unsafe practices slowly become 'just how we do things.'
exam_tip: Emphasise that HF programmes address the systemic causes of accidents in commercial operations. Mention normalisation of deviance, just culture, and continuous improvement.
key_facts: Human factors are a contributing cause in the vast majority of aviation accidents
Commercial pressures (schedules, revenue, fatigue) erode safety without structured management
Effective programmes prevent normalisation of deviance through continuous monitoring
Key elements: CRM training, fatigue management, reporting culture, standardised SOPs, learning from incidents
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Reason -- Managing the Risks of Organisational Accidents, ICAO Doc 9683

syllabus_desc: State the percentage of the two main gases in the atmosphere.
concept: You covered this at PPL level but here's the refresher: nitrogen makes up about 78% and oxygen about 21% of the atmosphere. The remaining 1% is argon, carbon dioxide, and trace gases. At CPL level, the reason this matters is the partial pressure concept--your body doesn't care about the percentage of oxygen (that stays constant up to about 80 km), it cares about the partial pressure. At sea level, 21% of 1013 hPa gives you about 213 hPa of oxygen partial pressure, which is plenty. But climb to 18,000 feet where ambient pressure is roughly half, and that 21% now delivers only about 106 hPa of oxygen--not enough for effective cognitive function. This is why understanding the gas composition leads directly into understanding hypoxia, pressurisation, and oxygen requirements.
exam_tip: Nitrogen 78%, oxygen 21%. At CPL level, examiners expect you to link this to partial pressure and its implications for flight at altitude.
key_facts: Nitrogen: approximately 78% of the atmosphere
Oxygen: approximately 21% of the atmosphere
Remaining 1%: argon (0.93%), CO2 (0.04%), and trace gases
The percentage remains constant; it is the partial pressure that decreases with altitude
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the variation of pressure as altitude increases.
concept: Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, but not in a linear fashion--it follows an exponential decay. Near sea level, pressure drops roughly 1 hPa per 30 feet (or about 1 inch of mercury per 1,000 feet). The standard atmosphere gives you 1013.25 hPa at sea level, about 700 hPa at 10,000 feet, roughly 500 hPa at 18,000 feet (half sea-level pressure), and about 250 hPa at 34,000 feet. The rate of decrease slows as you go higher because there's less air above you to compress the air below. For commercial operations, this matters in two big ways: first, your body receives less oxygen as pressure drops, which is the basis of all the hypoxia and pressurisation rules. Second, pressure changes affect trapped gases in your body--sinuses, ears, gut--which is why barotrauma is a real concern during rapid climbs and descents.
exam_tip: Know the key pressure milestones: half sea-level pressure at approximately 18,000 ft. Describe the decrease as non-linear (exponential decay), not a straight line.
key_facts: Pressure decreases exponentially with altitude, not linearly
Approximately 1 hPa per 30 ft near sea level
Half sea-level pressure (~500 hPa) at approximately 18,000 ft
Rate of decrease slows with increasing altitude
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain how the partial pressure of oxygen changes as altitude increases.
concept: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures tells you that the total pressure of a gas mixture equals the sum of each individual gas's partial pressure. Since oxygen is 21% of the atmosphere, its partial pressure is always 21% of the total ambient pressure. At sea level: 0.21 x 1013 hPa = approximately 213 hPa of oxygen. At 10,000 ft: 0.21 x 700 hPa = approximately 147 hPa. At 18,000 ft: 0.21 x 500 hPa = approximately 105 hPa. Your body needs a minimum alveolar oxygen partial pressure of about 60-65 mmHg (roughly 80-87 hPa) to function adequately. Below that threshold, hypoxia becomes serious. This is why NZ CARs require supplemental oxygen above 10,000 ft for extended periods--even though the percentage of oxygen hasn't changed, the partial pressure has dropped to the point where your cognitive and physical performance deteriorates significantly.
exam_tip: Apply Dalton's Law: partial pressure of O2 = 0.21 x total atmospheric pressure. Know that the percentage stays at 21% but the partial pressure drops, which is what affects your body.
key_facts: Partial pressure of O2 = 0.21 x total atmospheric pressure (Dalton's Law)
Sea level: ~213 hPa O2; 10,000 ft: ~147 hPa; 18,000 ft: ~105 hPa
The percentage of O2 remains constant at 21%; only the partial pressure drops
Minimum alveolar O2 partial pressure for adequate function: ~60-65 mmHg
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic anatomy of the respiratory system.
concept: Your respiratory system is the pipeline that gets oxygen from the outside air into your blood. It starts at your nose and mouth, passes through the pharynx and larynx, down the trachea (windpipe), which splits into two bronchi--one for each lung. The bronchi subdivide into smaller and smaller bronchioles, like branches on a tree, until they end in tiny air sacs called alveoli. You've got about 300 million alveoli in your lungs, giving you a gas exchange surface area roughly the size of a tennis court. The alveoli are where the magic happens--oxygen crosses the thin alveolar membrane into the pulmonary capillaries, and carbon dioxide crosses the other way to be exhaled. The whole system is driven by the diaphragm and intercostal muscles, which create negative pressure to draw air in. At altitude, the system works exactly the same way mechanically--but the reduced partial pressure of oxygen in the inhaled air means less oxygen crosses into your blood per breath.
exam_tip: Trace the path: nose/mouth, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, alveoli. Know that gas exchange occurs at the alveoli by diffusion across a thin membrane.
key_facts: Air path: nose/mouth > pharynx > larynx > trachea > bronchi > bronchioles > alveoli
Approximately 300 million alveoli provide a huge surface area for gas exchange
Gas exchange occurs by diffusion across the thin alveolar-capillary membrane
Breathing is driven by the diaphragm and intercostal muscles creating negative pressure
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the physiology of the respiratory system.
concept: Respiration is a two-part process: ventilation (the mechanical act of breathing) and gas exchange (getting oxygen into the blood and CO2 out). Ventilation is controlled by the respiratory centre in the brainstem, primarily driven by CO2 levels in the blood--when CO2 rises, you breathe faster and deeper. Oxygen levels play a secondary role as a backup driver. During inhalation, the diaphragm contracts and flattens, intercostal muscles lift the ribs, and the chest cavity expands, drawing air in by negative pressure. Exhalation is largely passive--the elastic recoil of the lungs pushes air out. At the alveoli, gas exchange occurs by diffusion: oxygen moves from high partial pressure in the alveoli to lower partial pressure in the venous blood, while CO2 moves the opposite way. The efficiency of this exchange depends on the partial pressure gradient--which is why altitude reduces oxygen uptake even though the breathing mechanics work fine. Normal resting respiratory rate is 12-20 breaths per minute, moving about 500 ml of air per breath (tidal volume).
exam_tip: Key concept: breathing is primarily driven by CO2 levels, not oxygen. Gas exchange depends on partial pressure gradients. Know tidal volume (~500 ml) and normal respiratory rate (12-20/min).
key_facts: Breathing is primarily driven by CO2 levels detected by the brainstem respiratory centre
Oxygen levels serve as a secondary (hypoxic) drive for respiration
Gas exchange at the alveoli occurs by diffusion down partial pressure gradients
Normal tidal volume ~500 ml; normal respiratory rate 12-20 breaths/min at rest
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic anatomy of the circulatory system.
concept: Your circulatory system is the delivery network that carries oxygenated blood from the lungs to every cell in your body and returns deoxygenated blood for reprocessing. The heart is a four-chambered pump: the right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from the body via the vena cavae, passes it to the right ventricle, which pumps it to the lungs via the pulmonary arteries. In the lungs, blood picks up oxygen and dumps CO2 (pulmonary circulation). Oxygenated blood returns to the left atrium via the pulmonary veins, passes to the left ventricle--the most muscular chamber--which pumps it out through the aorta to the rest of the body (systemic circulation). Arteries carry blood away from the heart (generally oxygenated, except pulmonary arteries), veins carry it back (generally deoxygenated, except pulmonary veins). Capillaries are the tiny vessels where actual gas, nutrient, and waste exchange occurs at the tissue level. A healthy resting heart rate is 60-100 bpm, pumping about 5 litres per minute.
exam_tip: Know the two circuits: pulmonary (heart to lungs and back) and systemic (heart to body and back). Remember: pulmonary arteries carry deoxygenated blood--the exception to the general rule.
key_facts: Four chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle
Pulmonary circuit: right ventricle > lungs > left atrium (picks up O2)
Systemic circuit: left ventricle > body > right atrium (delivers O2)
Cardiac output at rest: ~5 litres/min; resting heart rate: 60-100 bpm
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the physiology of the circulatory system.
concept: The circulatory system's physiology centres on delivering oxygen and nutrients to tissues and removing waste products. Blood pressure is generated by the left ventricle contracting (systole) and measured as systolic/diastolic pressure--typically around 120/80 mmHg for a healthy adult. The autonomic nervous system regulates heart rate and blood pressure: the sympathetic system speeds things up during stress or exertion (fight or flight), while the parasympathetic system slows things down at rest. Oxygen is transported in two ways: about 98.5% bound to haemoglobin in red blood cells (forming oxyhaemoglobin), and about 1.5% dissolved directly in plasma. The haemoglobin-oxygen relationship follows an S-shaped (sigmoid) dissociation curve--at high partial pressures (lungs), haemoglobin readily loads up with oxygen; at low partial pressures (tissues), it readily releases it. This is elegant design for oxygen delivery. Carbon monoxide disrupts this by binding to haemoglobin 200-250 times more readily than oxygen, which is why CO poisoning is so dangerous in the cockpit.
exam_tip: Know the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve shape (sigmoid/S-shaped) and why it matters. Remember CO binds haemoglobin 200-250x more readily than O2.
key_facts: 98.5% of oxygen transported bound to haemoglobin; 1.5% dissolved in plasma
Oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve is S-shaped (sigmoid)
Normal blood pressure: ~120/80 mmHg; regulated by autonomic nervous system
Carbon monoxide binds haemoglobin 200-250 times more readily than oxygen
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the role of the lungs in oxygen and carbon dioxide transfer.
concept: The lungs are where the respiratory and circulatory systems meet--the interface between outside air and your bloodstream. In the alveoli, oxygen from inhaled air diffuses across the alveolar-capillary membrane (which is only about 0.5 micrometres thick) into the pulmonary capillary blood. Simultaneously, CO2 diffuses the other way--from the blood into the alveoli to be exhaled. This exchange is driven entirely by partial pressure gradients: oxygen partial pressure is higher in the alveoli than in the venous blood arriving from the body, so O2 moves into the blood. CO2 partial pressure is higher in the venous blood than in the alveoli, so it moves out. The efficiency of this transfer depends on several factors: the partial pressure gradient (reduced at altitude), the surface area available (reduced by lung disease), the thickness of the membrane (increased by fluid or inflammation), and the time blood spends in contact with the alveoli (reduced when cardiac output is very high during exercise). Understanding this helps you appreciate why altitude, smoking, respiratory illness, and exertion all compound to create hypoxia risk.
exam_tip: Gas exchange is by diffusion down partial pressure gradients. Four factors affect efficiency: pressure gradient, surface area, membrane thickness, and contact time.
key_facts: Gas exchange occurs by diffusion across the alveolar-capillary membrane (~0.5 um thick)
O2 moves from alveoli (high pO2) to blood (low pO2); CO2 moves the opposite way
Efficiency depends on: pressure gradient, surface area, membrane thickness, contact time
Altitude reduces the pressure gradient, directly reducing oxygen transfer efficiency
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: State the partial pressure of oxygen both inside and outside the lungs at sea level.
concept: At sea level, the ambient (outside) partial pressure of oxygen is about 213 hPa (or 160 mmHg)--that's 21% of 1013 hPa. But by the time air reaches your alveoli, the oxygen partial pressure has dropped to about 103 mmHg (roughly 137 hPa). Why the reduction? Because the air in your alveoli is mixed with water vapour (which takes up partial pressure at body temperature--about 47 mmHg worth) and residual CO2 from the blood (about 40 mmHg). So the alveolar gas equation accounts for these diluting effects. The venous blood arriving at the lungs has an oxygen partial pressure of about 40 mmHg, creating a healthy gradient of about 60-63 mmHg that drives oxygen into the blood. After gas exchange, arterial blood leaves with an oxygen partial pressure of about 95-100 mmHg. These numbers are worth knowing because they set the baseline against which altitude effects are measured.
exam_tip: Outside lungs at sea level: ~160 mmHg (213 hPa). Inside alveoli at sea level: ~103 mmHg (~137 hPa). The difference is due to water vapour and CO2 dilution.
key_facts: Ambient pO2 at sea level: ~160 mmHg (213 hPa) -- 21% of atmospheric pressure
Alveolar pO2 at sea level: ~103 mmHg -- reduced by water vapour and CO2
Water vapour pressure at body temperature: ~47 mmHg (constant regardless of altitude)
Arterial blood pO2 after gas exchange: ~95-100 mmHg
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the mechanical effect of the partial pressure of oxygen on oxygen transfer in the lungs.
concept: Oxygen transfer in the lungs is driven by the partial pressure gradient between the alveolar air and the pulmonary capillary blood. At sea level, alveolar pO2 is about 103 mmHg and incoming venous blood pO2 is about 40 mmHg--a gradient of about 63 mmHg that efficiently drives oxygen across the membrane. As altitude increases, the alveolar pO2 drops (because ambient pressure drops), but the water vapour pressure stays constant at 47 mmHg and CO2 in the blood doesn't change much initially. This means the alveolar pO2 falls faster than you might expect. At 10,000 ft, alveolar pO2 drops to about 61 mmHg. At 18,000 ft, it's around 40 mmHg--roughly equal to venous blood pO2, meaning there's almost no gradient left to drive oxygen across. The mechanical effect is simple: less pressure pushing oxygen across the membrane means less oxygen gets into your blood per unit time. Your body can partially compensate by breathing faster (increasing ventilation), but this has limits and side effects (hyperventilation). This is the fundamental mechanism of hypoxic hypoxia.
exam_tip: The gradient between alveolar pO2 and venous blood pO2 drives transfer. As altitude increases, this gradient shrinks, reducing oxygen transfer. At ~18,000 ft, the gradient approaches zero.
key_facts: O2 transfer driven by partial pressure gradient: alveolar pO2 minus venous blood pO2
At sea level: gradient ~63 mmHg (103 - 40); at 10,000 ft: gradient shrinks significantly
At 18,000 ft: alveolar pO2 ~40 mmHg, approaching venous blood pO2 -- minimal transfer
Water vapour pressure (47 mmHg) is constant, taking an increasing proportion at altitude
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of hypoxia.
concept: Hypoxia is insufficient oxygen reaching your body tissues, and there are four types based on cause. Hypoxic hypoxia (the most relevant to pilots) occurs when there's insufficient oxygen in the inhaled air--altitude is the classic cause, but it also includes breathing in a smoke-filled cockpit. Anaemic hypoxia occurs when the blood can't carry enough oxygen even though it's available--carbon monoxide poisoning is the textbook example, as CO hogs the haemoglobin. Heavy smoking, blood loss, and anaemia also cause this. Stagnant (or circulatory) hypoxia occurs when blood flow is restricted, so oxygen-rich blood doesn't reach the tissues--positive G-forces pooling blood in the extremities, heart failure, or even prolonged sitting restricting circulation. Histotoxic hypoxia occurs when tissues can't use the oxygen that's delivered to them--alcohol and certain drugs interfere with cellular oxygen metabolism. In practice, these types can combine: a pilot who smokes (anaemic), drinks the night before (histotoxic), and flies unpressurised above 10,000 ft (hypoxic) is stacking the deck against themselves.
exam_tip: Know all four types: hypoxic, anaemic, stagnant, histotoxic. Give the classic cause for each. Examiners love the combined scenario question--multiple types acting together.
key_facts: Hypoxic: insufficient O2 in inhaled air (altitude, smoke inhalation)
Anaemic: blood cannot carry enough O2 (CO poisoning, smoking, blood loss, anaemia)
Stagnant/circulatory: blood flow restricted (G-forces, heart failure, prolonged sitting)
Histotoxic: tissues cannot use delivered O2 (alcohol, certain drugs)
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight

syllabus_desc: Describe the primary physiological and behavioural consequences of hypoxia for flight crew and passengers.
concept: Hypoxia attacks your brain first because the brain consumes about 20% of your body's oxygen despite being only 2% of your body weight. For flight crew, the consequences are devastating: impaired judgement and decision-making (you make bad choices but feel confident about them), reduced coordination and motor skills, slowed reaction times, degraded vision (especially night vision, which is affected from as low as 5,000 ft), euphoria or a false sense of wellbeing, personality changes (some become aggressive, others passive), tunnel vision, and eventually unconsciousness and death. The insidious part is that hypoxia removes your ability to recognise you're hypoxic--it's like being drunk but not knowing you're drunk. For passengers, the effects are similar but the consequences differ: they may become anxious, confused, or unresponsive. In a depressurisation scenario, incapacitated passengers create additional workload for crew. Children, elderly passengers, and those with cardiac or respiratory conditions are more susceptible. A smoker's haemoglobin is already partly occupied by CO, so their effective altitude is higher than the cabin altitude.
exam_tip: Emphasise that hypoxia impairs judgement first, making self-diagnosis nearly impossible. Night vision degrades from as low as 5,000 ft. Mention euphoria as a dangerous symptom--pilots feel fine while performing badly.
key_facts: Brain affected first: impaired judgement, reduced coordination, slowed reactions
Night vision degrades from as low as 5,000 ft due to rod cell oxygen sensitivity
Euphoria and false confidence make self-diagnosis extremely difficult
Passengers with pre-existing conditions, smokers, children, and elderly are more susceptible
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the common symptoms of hypoxia.
concept: The symptoms of hypoxia follow a general progression, though individual variation is significant. Early symptoms (from about 10,000-14,000 ft without supplemental oxygen) include: impaired night vision, reduced mental performance, increased breathing rate, slight headache, and a sense of warmth or tingling. Moderate hypoxia (14,000-18,000 ft) brings: poor judgement, impaired memory, reduced coordination, delayed reactions, cyanosis (blue lips and fingernails), drowsiness or alternatively euphoria, difficulty with calculations or multi-tasking. Severe hypoxia (above 18,000 ft) produces: marked confusion, inability to perform simple tasks, loss of peripheral vision, tunnel vision, loss of consciousness. The really dangerous thing is that the order and severity of symptoms vary between individuals and even between exposures for the same person. Some pilots get a headache first; others go straight to euphoria. You can't rely on recognising a specific symptom--which is why altitude awareness and oxygen equipment are your primary defences.
exam_tip: List symptoms in order: impaired night vision, headache, increased breathing rate, poor judgement, euphoria, impaired coordination, cyanosis, tunnel vision, unconsciousness. Stress that symptoms vary between individuals.
key_facts: Early: impaired night vision, increased breathing rate, headache, tingling
Moderate: poor judgement, euphoria, impaired coordination, cyanosis, drowsiness
Severe: marked confusion, tunnel vision, loss of consciousness
Symptom order and severity vary significantly between individuals and exposures
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons hypoxia symptoms are difficult to detect.
concept: Hypoxia symptoms are difficult to detect for several interconnected reasons. First, the onset is insidious and gradual--there's no dramatic warning like pain or discomfort in the early stages. Second, one of the first faculties affected is your higher cognitive function, which is precisely the faculty you need to recognise something's wrong--it's a vicious feedback loop. Third, the euphoria effect means you may actually feel better than normal while your performance is degrading. Fourth, the symptoms mimic other conditions: a headache could be dehydration, fatigue could be a long day, and tingling could be from sitting in one position. Fifth, individual variation means your personal symptoms may not match the textbook list, so even with training, your experience of hypoxia might not be what you expect. Sixth, gradual altitude changes (as in an unpressurised climb) produce gradual symptom onset, unlike the sudden onset in a rapid depressurisation that's more obviously abnormal. This is precisely why hypobaric chamber training (or equivalent normobaric hypoxia training) is so valuable--it lets you experience your personal symptoms in a controlled environment.
exam_tip: The key reasons: insidious onset, cognitive impairment prevents self-recognition, euphoria masks the problem, symptoms mimic other conditions, and individual variation. Recommend hypobaric chamber experience for personal symptom recognition.
key_facts: Insidious gradual onset with no obvious warning pain or discomfort
Higher cognitive function impaired first -- the faculty needed to detect the problem
Euphoria creates a false sense of wellbeing, masking performance degradation
Symptoms mimic fatigue, dehydration, and other common conditions
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between hypoxic onset and both vision and cognitive performance.
concept: Vision and cognitive performance are both early casualties of hypoxia, but they degrade at different rates and in different ways. Night vision is the most sensitive--rod cells in the retina have a very high metabolic rate and are affected from as low as 5,000 ft cabin altitude. You may lose up to 25% of your night vision sensitivity at 8,000 ft without being aware of it. Colour vision and visual acuity begin to degrade around 10,000-12,000 ft. Peripheral vision narrows progressively, leading to tunnel vision at higher altitudes. Cognitive performance follows a similar pattern: complex tasks degrade first (multi-tasking, mental arithmetic, decision-making), while simple overlearned tasks (basic aircraft handling) persist longer. Short-term memory is particularly vulnerable--you might read a clearance from ATC and forget it before you can write it down. The relationship is significant for commercial pilots because you need both visual acuity and cognitive sharpness simultaneously: reading instruments, interpreting weather, making approach decisions, and communicating with ATC all require both faculties working together.
exam_tip: Night vision affected from ~5,000 ft. Complex cognitive tasks degrade before simple overlearned tasks. Both vision and cognition are needed simultaneously for safe commercial operations.
key_facts: Night vision (rod cells) affected from as low as 5,000 ft; up to 25% loss at 8,000 ft
Colour vision and visual acuity degrade from ~10,000-12,000 ft
Peripheral vision narrows progressively, leading to tunnel vision
Complex cognitive tasks (decision-making, multi-tasking) degrade before simple tasks
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe how hypoxia can be prevented.
concept: Preventing hypoxia in commercial operations comes down to three strategies: pressurisation, supplemental oxygen, and altitude awareness. Aircraft pressurisation maintains cabin altitude below 8,000 ft (typically 6,000-8,000 ft in most pressurised aircraft), keeping oxygen partial pressure in a comfortable range. For unpressurised operations--which many CPL holders in NZ will encounter flying Cessna Caravans, Beechcraft, or similar--supplemental oxygen is the primary defence. NZ CARs require supplemental oxygen above 10,000 ft for flights exceeding 30 minutes, and at all times above 13,000 ft. For crew, oxygen is required above 10,000 ft cabin altitude. Additional prevention measures include: not smoking (which reduces haemoglobin's oxygen-carrying capacity), avoiding alcohol before flying (histotoxic hypoxia risk), maintaining physical fitness, being aware of personal susceptibility factors (fatigue, illness, altitude acclimatisation), and monitoring cabin altitude in pressurised aircraft. Night flights should factor in the 5,000 ft threshold for night vision degradation when planning cruise altitudes.
exam_tip: Know the NZ CAR oxygen requirements: crew supplemental O2 above 10,000 ft; all occupants above 13,000 ft. Prevention includes pressurisation, supplemental O2, not smoking, avoiding alcohol, fitness, and altitude awareness.
key_facts: Aircraft pressurisation maintains cabin altitude typically below 8,000 ft
NZ CARs: supplemental O2 required above 10,000 ft (>30 min) and at all times above 13,000 ft
Avoid smoking (reduces O2 carrying capacity) and alcohol (histotoxic effect)
For night flights, consider night vision degradation from 5,000 ft when selecting altitude
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, NZ CARs Part 91, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: List the main factors influencing variation in hypoxia onset (tolerance) between individuals.
concept: Hypoxia tolerance varies enormously between people, and even for the same person from day to day. The main factors include: physical fitness (fitter people generally tolerate altitude better due to more efficient cardiovascular systems), altitude acclimatisation (people who live or regularly operate at altitude develop physiological adaptations), metabolic rate (higher activity = higher oxygen demand = earlier onset), smoking (smokers effectively start at a higher altitude because CO occupies haemoglobin), anaemia or blood disorders (less haemoglobin = less oxygen-carrying capacity), time at altitude (longer exposure = cumulative effect), rate of ascent (rapid climbs don't allow time for physiological compensation), body temperature (fever increases metabolic rate and oxygen demand), recent alcohol consumption (impairs cellular oxygen use), fatigue and illness (reduce overall physiological resilience), age (older individuals generally have reduced cardiovascular efficiency), and individual genetic variation. For CPL operations, the practical takeaway is that you can't assume you'll react the same way every time you fly at altitude.
exam_tip: List at least six factors: fitness, acclimatisation, smoking, metabolic rate, alcohol, fatigue/illness, rate of ascent, anaemia. Emphasise that the same pilot may react differently on different days.
key_facts: Physical fitness, altitude acclimatisation, and metabolic rate affect tolerance
Smoking, anaemia, and blood loss reduce effective oxygen-carrying capacity
Alcohol, fatigue, illness, and fever increase susceptibility
Rate of ascent, time at altitude, age, and individual genetic variation also contribute
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: State the factors that affect the likelihood of suffering from hypoxia.
concept: The likelihood of suffering hypoxia depends on a combination of environmental and personal factors. Environmental: cabin/flight altitude (the dominant factor), rate of climb, duration at altitude, ambient temperature (cold increases oxygen demand), and cabin air quality. Personal: smoking status (a pack-a-day smoker may have 5-8% of their haemoglobin bound to CO, equivalent to starting at 3,000-5,000 ft higher), recent alcohol consumption, physical workload during flight, physical fitness, state of health (respiratory infections, anaemia), medications that affect breathing or circulation, fatigue level, and previous altitude experience. Operational factors also play in: workload in the cockpit (high workload increases oxygen demand), stress (increases metabolic rate), and whether you're flying day or night (night vision sensitivity means hypoxia effects are noticed earlier at night, though the actual onset is the same). For a CPL pilot planning a flight above 10,000 ft in an unpressurised aircraft, you need to consider all these factors in your pre-flight risk assessment.
exam_tip: Group factors into environmental (altitude, duration, rate of climb), personal (smoking, alcohol, fitness, health), and operational (workload, stress, day/night). Smoking effectively adds 3,000-5,000 ft to your physiological altitude.
key_facts: Primary factor: cabin/flight altitude and duration of exposure
Smoking adds 3,000-5,000 ft equivalent altitude due to CO binding haemoglobin
High workload, stress, and physical exertion increase oxygen demand
Night operations reveal hypoxia effects earlier through degraded night vision
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe how hypoxia can be treated.
concept: Treatment of hypoxia follows a clear priority: first, recognise the problem (which is the hardest part). Once recognised, the immediate action is to descend to a lower altitude--get below 10,000 ft as quickly as safely possible. If supplemental oxygen is available, apply it immediately at 100% (demand or continuous flow, depending on your system). In a pressurised aircraft experiencing depressurisation, don the oxygen mask, then descend. Recovery from mild to moderate hypoxia is rapid once oxygen supply is restored--usually within seconds to minutes. However, if hypoxia has been prolonged or severe, there may be a period of confusion during recovery, and full cognitive function may take longer to return. After a significant hypoxic event, don't assume you're fine just because you feel better--give yourself time to recover and consider diverting. For passengers, get them on supplemental oxygen if available and descend. Monitor for any signs of residual impairment. After landing, anyone who experienced significant hypoxia should seek medical assessment.
exam_tip: Treatment: recognise, descend, apply 100% O2. Recovery is rapid but may not be complete immediately--residual confusion can persist. Consider diverting after a significant hypoxic event.
key_facts: Immediate actions: recognise the problem, descend below 10,000 ft, apply 100% O2
Recovery from mild/moderate hypoxia is rapid once oxygen is restored (seconds to minutes)
Residual confusion may persist after recovery--don't assume immediate full function
Consider diversion after significant hypoxic event; seek medical assessment after landing
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Define the concept of 'time of useful consciousness'.
concept: Time of useful consciousness (TUC)--also called effective performance time--is the period from the onset of oxygen deprivation until the point where you can no longer take purposeful corrective action. This isn't the time until you pass out; it's the time until you can't effectively help yourself. The distinction matters: you might remain conscious for a while after your TUC expires, but you'll be too impaired to put on an oxygen mask, initiate a descent, or make a radio call. TUC assumes a sudden complete loss of oxygen supply (like a rapid depressurisation), not a gradual onset. Physical activity dramatically reduces TUC--if you're wrestling with controls during an emergency, your muscles are consuming oxygen faster, cutting your useful time. TUC is the reason pressurised aircraft have quick-donning oxygen masks within arm's reach of the crew, and why the immediate response to a depressurisation warning is 'mask on first, diagnose later.'
exam_tip: TUC is NOT time to unconsciousness--it's time until you can no longer take effective corrective action. Physical activity reduces it significantly. Always mask first, then troubleshoot.
key_facts: TUC = time from oxygen loss to inability to take purposeful corrective action
Not the same as time to unconsciousness--useful function is lost before consciousness
Physical activity (struggling, moving) significantly reduces TUC
Basis for quick-donning crew oxygen masks in pressurised aircraft
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: State the approximate time of useful consciousness at: (a) 18,000ft.
concept: At 18,000 ft, the time of useful consciousness following a sudden and complete loss of supplemental oxygen or pressurisation is approximately 20-30 minutes. This is assuming you're seated and not physically exerting yourself. With moderate physical activity, this drops to roughly half. At 18,000 ft, atmospheric pressure is about half sea-level, giving an alveolar pO2 that's marginal for useful function. You'll still be conscious, but your decision-making and motor skills will progressively deteriorate. The relatively long TUC at this altitude gives you time to recognise the problem and act--but only if you're monitoring for it. Many CPL operations in NZ unpressurised aircraft (like the Cessna Caravan) don't routinely operate at 18,000 ft, but a pressurised aircraft losing pressurisation at FL180 gives the crew a reasonable window to respond.
exam_tip: 18,000 ft TUC: approximately 20-30 minutes (at rest). This is a commonly examined figure. Physical activity halves it.
key_facts: TUC at 18,000 ft: approximately 20-30 minutes at rest
Physical activity reduces this to approximately 10-15 minutes
Atmospheric pressure at 18,000 ft is approximately half sea-level (~500 hPa)
Sufficient time to recognise and act if the pilot is monitoring for symptoms
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: State the approximate time of useful consciousness at: (b) 25,000ft.
concept: At 25,000 ft, the time of useful consciousness drops dramatically to approximately 3-5 minutes at rest. With physical activity, this shrinks to about 1.5-3 minutes. The atmospheric pressure at 25,000 ft is roughly 376 hPa--about 37% of sea-level pressure--giving an alveolar pO2 that's critically low. At this altitude, there's very little margin. If you're in a pressurised aircraft that loses cabin pressure at FL250, you've got minutes, not tens of minutes, to get your mask on and initiate an emergency descent. This is why crew oxygen masks must be within immediate reach and why the drill is drilled relentlessly: mask on first, establish communication, then begin emergency descent. Three to five minutes sounds like a lot, but in a stressful emergency situation with alarms going off and passengers panicking, it goes very fast.
exam_tip: 25,000 ft TUC: approximately 3-5 minutes at rest. This is a critical altitude for pressurised aircraft operations. Physical activity reduces it to 1.5-3 minutes.
key_facts: TUC at 25,000 ft: approximately 3-5 minutes at rest
Physical activity reduces this to approximately 1.5-3 minutes
Atmospheric pressure ~376 hPa (37% sea level)
Immediate mask donning essential--no time to troubleshoot first
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: State the approximate time of useful consciousness at: (c) 35,000ft.
concept: At 35,000 ft, the time of useful consciousness is a terrifyingly short 30-60 seconds at rest, and as little as 15-30 seconds with physical activity. Atmospheric pressure at FL350 is only about 238 hPa--roughly 23% of sea-level. The alveolar pO2 at this altitude is so low that oxygen simply can't diffuse into the blood fast enough to maintain any useful brain function. This is the cruise altitude for many jet transport aircraft, and it's why rapid depressurisation at these altitudes is considered a critical emergency. The crew's response must be immediate and instinctive: mask on, 100% oxygen, emergency descent. There's no time for analysis or discussion. The automation must be programmed to manage the aircraft during the initial seconds while the crew secures their oxygen supply. Training for this scenario emphasises muscle memory--reaching for the mask must be automatic, not a conscious decision.
exam_tip: 35,000 ft TUC: approximately 30-60 seconds at rest; 15-30 seconds with activity. This is the critical figure for jet operations. Response must be immediate and instinctive.
key_facts: TUC at 35,000 ft: approximately 30-60 seconds at rest
Physical activity reduces this to approximately 15-30 seconds
Atmospheric pressure ~238 hPa (23% sea level)
Response must be immediate and instinctive--no time for analysis
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain oxygen paradox.
concept: Oxygen paradox is a phenomenon where a person's condition temporarily worsens after they start breathing supplemental oxygen following a period of hypoxia. You'd expect that giving someone oxygen would immediately improve things, but paradoxically, symptoms may briefly intensify--dizziness may increase, vision may temporarily blur further, and there may be a brief period of confusion or even near-syncope. The mechanism isn't completely understood, but it's thought to relate to the sudden change in blood chemistry: the reintroduction of high oxygen levels may cause a transient drop in blood pressure, a temporary change in heart rate, or a shift in the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve. Some theories suggest it relates to the washout of CO2 that had been elevated during the hypoxic period. The practical importance for pilots is: don't panic if you feel briefly worse when you first go on oxygen. Continue breathing 100% oxygen and the symptoms will resolve within 30-60 seconds. It doesn't mean the oxygen system is faulty.
exam_tip: Oxygen paradox: temporary worsening of symptoms when first applying supplemental oxygen after hypoxia. It's transient (30-60 seconds). Continue breathing 100% O2--symptoms resolve quickly.
key_facts: Brief worsening of hypoxia symptoms when supplemental oxygen is first applied
May include increased dizziness, blurred vision, brief confusion, or near-syncope
Thought to relate to sudden changes in blood chemistry and pressure
Transient effect lasting 30-60 seconds; continue 100% O2 through the episode
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of hyperventilation.
concept: Hyperventilation is breathing too fast and/or too deep for your body's metabolic needs, blowing off carbon dioxide faster than your body produces it. The most common cause in the cockpit is anxiety or stress--a sudden emergency, turbulence, or an unexpected situation triggers rapid breathing as part of the fight-or-flight response. Pain can cause it. So can fear, particularly in passengers. Heat stress in a hot cockpit can increase respiratory rate. Hypoxia itself can trigger hyperventilation as the body's compensatory mechanism to get more oxygen--which is why differentiating between the two is critical. Even excitement or anger can trigger it. Motion sickness with nausea often leads to hyperventilation. At CPL level, the workload and responsibility of commercial operations can create chronic low-level stress that predisposes you to hyperventilation during acute stressors. The underlying mechanism is always the same: excessive loss of CO2 from the blood, causing respiratory alkalosis (blood pH rises above normal).
exam_tip: Main cause in pilots: stress/anxiety triggering rapid breathing. Key mechanism: excessive CO2 loss causing respiratory alkalosis. Remember that hypoxia can also trigger hyperventilation--confusing the diagnosis.
key_facts: Primary cause in pilots: anxiety, stress, fear, or panic during flight operations
Can also be caused by pain, heat, motion sickness, excitement, or hypoxia compensation
Mechanism: excessive CO2 blown off, causing respiratory alkalosis (raised blood pH)
Hypoxia can trigger hyperventilation, making differential diagnosis critical
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of hyperventilation.
concept: Hyperventilation symptoms result from the drop in blood CO2 and the resulting alkalosis, which causes blood vessels to constrict and affects nerve function. Common symptoms include: tingling or numbness in the fingers, toes, and around the lips (paraesthesia); dizziness and lightheadedness; a feeling of breathlessness despite breathing rapidly (which can create a vicious cycle--feeling breathless makes you breathe even faster); muscle spasms, particularly in the hands and feet (carpopedal spasm, where fingers curl inward); visual disturbances including blurring or tunnelling; chest tightness or pain; a sensation of unreality or detachment; anxiety (which feeds back into more hyperventilation); nausea; and in severe cases, loss of consciousness. The symptoms can be alarming, which worsens the anxiety that caused the hyperventilation in the first place. Many of these symptoms overlap with hypoxia, which is why it's so important to differentiate between the two--the treatment is very different.
exam_tip: Key symptoms: tingling (especially hands, feet, lips), dizziness, breathlessness, muscle spasms, visual disturbance, chest tightness. Note the overlap with hypoxia symptoms--this is a favourite exam topic.
key_facts: Tingling/numbness in fingers, toes, and lips (paraesthesia) -- a hallmark symptom
Dizziness, lightheadedness, visual disturbance, and chest tightness
Carpopedal spasm (hand/finger muscle spasms) in severe cases
Symptoms overlap with hypoxia, making differential diagnosis essential
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe how hyperventilation can be treated.
concept: Treatment of hyperventilation is straightforward once correctly diagnosed: you need to restore normal CO2 levels in the blood. The first step is conscious control of breathing--deliberately slow your breathing rate and reduce the depth of each breath. Talk yourself through it: breathe in slowly through the nose for 3-4 seconds, hold briefly, exhale slowly through the mouth for 4-6 seconds. If that's not working, you can breathe into a paper bag or cupped hands (rebreathing your own exhaled CO2). However, in the cockpit, this is impractical and potentially dangerous if the actual problem is hypoxia. The critical decision is: are you hyperventilating, or are you hypoxic? If there's any doubt, treat for hypoxia first (apply oxygen, descend) because untreated hypoxia kills, while hyperventilation is uncomfortable but rarely fatal. Once you're on oxygen and at a safe altitude, if symptoms persist or match the hyperventilation pattern, then address the breathing rate. Reassurance and reducing stress also help--if it's a passenger, calm communication is part of the treatment.
exam_tip: Treatment: slow and control breathing rate. If in doubt between hypoxia and hyperventilation, TREAT FOR HYPOXIA FIRST (oxygen + descend). Hyperventilation is uncomfortable but hypoxia kills.
key_facts: Primary treatment: consciously slow and control breathing rate
Rebreathing into a bag/cupped hands restores CO2 levels
If in doubt between hypoxia and hyperventilation, treat for hypoxia first
Reduce stress and provide reassurance to break the anxiety-hyperventilation cycle
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the differences between hyperventilation and hypoxia.
concept: Differentiating hyperventilation from hypoxia is one of the most important clinical skills for a pilot because the symptoms overlap significantly but the treatments are opposite. Both can cause dizziness, visual disturbance, tingling, and impaired performance. Key differences: hyperventilation typically causes tingling in the extremities and lips (paraesthesia) as a prominent early symptom, while hypoxia typically starts with night vision loss and euphoria. Hyperventilation causes muscle spasms (carpopedal spasm); hypoxia causes cyanosis (blue lips/nails). In hyperventilation, you're aware you're breathing fast; in hypoxia, you often don't notice the increased breathing rate. Hyperventilation usually occurs at lower altitudes during stressful situations; hypoxia occurs at altitude. The most reliable differentiator is altitude: if you're above 10,000 ft without supplemental oxygen, assume hypoxia first. Below 10,000 ft during a stressful event, hyperventilation is more likely. The golden rule in the cockpit: if in any doubt, apply 100% oxygen and descend--this treats hypoxia directly and gives you time to assess if the problem is actually hyperventilation.
exam_tip: This is a very common exam question. Key differentiators: altitude (above/below 10,000 ft), paraesthesia more prominent in hyperventilation, cyanosis in hypoxia, euphoria in hypoxia. When in doubt: treat for hypoxia first.
key_facts: Hyperventilation: prominent tingling/paraesthesia, muscle spasms, awareness of rapid breathing
Hypoxia: cyanosis, euphoria, night vision loss, unawareness of impairment
Most reliable differentiator: altitude -- above 10,000 ft suspect hypoxia first
Golden rule: when in doubt, treat for hypoxia (O2 + descend) then reassess
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of barotrauma.
concept: Barotrauma is tissue damage caused by the expansion or contraction of trapped gas as ambient pressure changes. The underlying physics is Boyle's Law: as pressure decreases (during climb), gas volume increases, and vice versa during descent. Your body has several gas-containing cavities: the middle ear (behind the eardrum), the sinuses (frontal, maxillary, ethmoid, sphenoid), the gastrointestinal tract, and any dental cavities with trapped air. Barotrauma occurs when these cavities can't equalise pressure with the environment. On ascent, expanding gas needs to escape--if passages are blocked (swollen eustachian tubes, blocked sinuses from a cold), the expanding gas presses against surrounding tissue, causing pain and potential damage. On descent, the reverse happens: ambient pressure increases, cavity volume tries to shrink, and if air can't enter to equalise, a relative vacuum forms, pulling tissue inward. Flying with a head cold, sinus infection, or congestion is the classic cause because the passages that normally allow pressure equalisation are blocked by swelling and mucus.
exam_tip: Boyle's Law: volume inversely proportional to pressure. Barotrauma occurs when trapped gas can't equalise. The classic cause: flying with a cold or sinus infection blocking equalisation passages.
key_facts: Caused by Boyle's Law: gas expands on ascent, contracts on descent
Affects middle ear, sinuses, GI tract, and dental cavities
Occurs when gas-containing cavities cannot equalise with ambient pressure
Most commonly caused by flying with upper respiratory infection or congestion
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of barotrauma.
concept: Barotrauma symptoms depend on which cavity is affected. Ear barotrauma (barotitis media): pain in the ear (usually during descent when the eustachian tube can't open to let air in), a feeling of fullness or pressure, reduced hearing, dizziness or vertigo if the inner ear is affected, and in severe cases, rupture of the eardrum with sudden relief of pain followed by bleeding. Sinus barotrauma (barosinusitis): pain over the affected sinus--sharp, localised pain above the eyes (frontal), below the eyes (maxillary), or deep behind the nose (ethmoid/sphenoid), often with referred pain to the teeth, and sometimes with epistaxis (nosebleed) as blood vessels rupture. GI barotrauma: abdominal pain, bloating, cramping, and discomfort as trapped gas expands during climb (particularly relevant after eating gas-producing foods or carbonated drinks). Dental barotrauma (barodontalgia): toothache, particularly in teeth with recent fillings, crowns, or decay where air pockets may be trapped. All of these are acutely distracting in the cockpit, potentially incapacitating during critical phases of flight.
exam_tip: Know symptoms by cavity: ear (pain during descent, hearing loss, vertigo), sinus (localised face pain, nosebleed), GI (bloating on climb), dental (toothache). All are distracting and potentially incapacitating.
key_facts: Ear: pain (especially during descent), hearing loss, vertigo, potential eardrum rupture
Sinus: localised facial pain, referred tooth pain, possible epistaxis (nosebleed)
GI tract: abdominal bloating, cramping, and discomfort during climb
Dental: toothache in teeth with fillings, crowns, or decay trapping air
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of barotrauma on the various parts of the body.
concept: The effects of barotrauma range from uncomfortable to incapacitating, depending on the severity and location. In the middle ear, the expanding or contracting gas can stretch or rupture the tympanic membrane (eardrum), causing acute pain, hearing loss, and potentially allowing infection. If pressure differentials affect the inner ear, you can get vestibular disturbance--vertigo, nausea, and spatial disorientation, which in flight is a genuine emergency. In the sinuses, expanding gas can rupture blood vessels in the sinus lining, causing bleeding and severe facial pain. Prolonged sinus barotrauma can lead to sinus infection as the inflamed lining becomes susceptible to bacteria. In the GI tract, expanding gas is mainly uncomfortable, but in extreme cases can cause significant distension and pain that makes concentrating on flying difficult. Dental effects include cracking of fillings, severe toothache, and in rare cases, fracture of weakened teeth. For a commercial pilot, any of these can degrade performance during critical phases. Ear barotrauma with vertigo is the most dangerous because it directly attacks spatial orientation.
exam_tip: The most dangerous effect is inner ear involvement causing vertigo and spatial disorientation. Ear and sinus barotrauma are the most common and the most operationally significant.
key_facts: Middle ear: eardrum rupture, hearing loss, potential vertigo from inner ear involvement
Sinuses: epistaxis, severe facial pain, secondary infection risk
GI tract: distension and discomfort; rarely incapacitating but distracting
Dental: cracked fillings, severe toothache, tooth fracture in extreme cases
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe how barotrauma can be prevented.
concept: Preventing barotrauma is straightforward in principle: don't fly when you can't equalise pressure, and minimise trapped gas. Specifically: don't fly with a cold, sinus infection, or upper respiratory congestion--the blocked passages prevent equalisation. Avoid flying with severe nasal allergies or hay fever unless symptoms are well controlled. Use decongestant sprays or tablets if absolutely necessary, but be aware of side effects and that they may wear off during a long flight, leaving you worse off mid-descent. Don't fly with a dental problem that might trap air--see your dentist before your next flight if you've had recent dental work. Avoid carbonated drinks and gas-producing foods before flight to minimise GI gas. During flight, equalise ear pressure frequently during climbs and descents by swallowing, yawning, or using the Valsalva manoeuvre (pinch nose, close mouth, gently blow). If you can't equalise, slow or halt the descent/climb if possible. For commercial operations, the practical rule is simple: if you have a head cold, you're not fit to fly.
exam_tip: Key prevention: don't fly with a cold or congestion. Equalise frequently during altitude changes. Valsalva manoeuvre for ear equalisation. Avoid carbonated drinks and gas-producing foods.
key_facts: Do not fly with a cold, sinus infection, or significant nasal congestion
Equalise ear pressure frequently during climbs and descents (swallow, yawn, Valsalva)
Avoid carbonated drinks and gas-producing foods before flight
Seek dental clearance before flying after recent dental work
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe how barotrauma can be treated.
concept: In-flight treatment of barotrauma depends on the affected area. For ear barotrauma during descent: try the Valsalva manoeuvre (pinch nose, close mouth, blow gently against closed passages to force air up the eustachian tubes). Swallowing or yawning may help. If Valsalva doesn't work, slow or halt the descent rate if operationally possible--giving the eustachian tubes more time to open. Chewing gum can help promote swallowing. If ear pain is severe and you can't equalise, consider climbing back up briefly to relieve the pressure, then descending more slowly. For sinus barotrauma: there's little you can do in flight beyond slowing altitude changes. A nasal decongestant spray may help open passages if you have one available. For GI discomfort: loosen clothing around the waist, change position if possible, and let gas pass naturally. After landing, anyone who experienced significant barotrauma should see a doctor--particularly ear barotrauma, which may need otoscopic examination to check for eardrum damage. A pilot should not fly again until symptoms have completely resolved and clearance has been obtained.
exam_tip: In-flight ear treatment: Valsalva, swallow, yawn; slow descent rate; climb back up if needed. Post-flight: medical assessment required for significant barotrauma. Don't fly again until symptoms resolve.
key_facts: Ear: Valsalva manoeuvre, swallow, yawn; slow/halt descent; climb briefly if severe
Sinus: slow altitude changes; nasal decongestant spray if available
GI: loosen clothing, change position, allow gas to pass naturally
Post-flight: medical assessment for significant barotrauma; don't fly until resolved
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of decompression sickness.
concept: Decompression sickness (DCS, also called 'the bends') occurs when dissolved nitrogen in your blood and tissues comes out of solution as bubbles when ambient pressure decreases. Think of it like opening a bottle of fizzy drink--the dissolved gas forms bubbles when pressure is released. Under normal sea-level pressure, nitrogen is dissolved harmlessly in your blood and tissues. When you ascend to altitude, the reduced pressure allows some of that nitrogen to form bubbles. For most aviation purposes, DCS becomes a risk above approximately 18,000 ft, though it can occur lower in certain circumstances. The risk increases significantly above 25,000 ft. The key predisposing factor that catches pilots out is recent scuba diving: diving saturates your tissues with extra nitrogen under the increased water pressure, and if you fly before that nitrogen has been adequately off-gassed, the reduced cabin pressure at altitude can cause DCS at much lower altitudes than would otherwise be a risk. Body fat stores more nitrogen than lean tissue, so individuals with higher body fat percentages are more susceptible.
exam_tip: Cause: dissolved nitrogen forming bubbles as ambient pressure decreases (Henry's Law). Risk altitude: above ~18,000 ft normally, but lower after diving. Scuba diving before flying is the classic risk scenario.
key_facts: Dissolved nitrogen forms bubbles when ambient pressure decreases (Henry's Law)
Risk becomes significant above approximately 18,000 ft, increasing markedly above 25,000 ft
Recent scuba diving dramatically increases risk at lower altitudes
Higher body fat percentage increases susceptibility (fat stores more nitrogen)
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of decompression sickness.
concept: DCS symptoms depend on where the nitrogen bubbles form. The 'bends' (Type I DCS): joint and limb pain, typically in the knees, elbows, shoulders, and wrists--dull, deep, throbbing pain that increases with movement. This accounts for about 60-70% of DCS cases. 'Creeps': skin itching, tingling, mottling, or a sensation of insects crawling on the skin, caused by bubbles in the skin's capillaries. 'Chokes' (Type II DCS): bubbles in the pulmonary circulation causing chest pain, coughing, difficulty breathing, and potentially circulatory collapse--this is a medical emergency. Neurological DCS: bubbles in the brain or spinal cord causing headache, visual disturbances, confusion, weakness, numbness, paralysis, or seizures--also a medical emergency. Symptoms typically appear within 30 minutes to several hours of exposure to reduced pressure. The onset may be gradual or sudden. Any symptom occurring after altitude exposure or after flying following a dive should be treated as potential DCS until proven otherwise.
exam_tip: Know the types: bends (joint pain, most common), creeps (skin symptoms), chokes (chest/breathing, emergency), neurological (brain/spinal, emergency). Symptoms appear within minutes to hours of exposure.
key_facts: Bends (Type I): joint pain in knees, elbows, shoulders, wrists -- most common (60-70%)
Creeps: skin itching, tingling, mottling from capillary bubbles
Chokes (Type II): chest pain, coughing, breathing difficulty -- medical emergency
Neurological: headache, confusion, weakness, paralysis, seizures -- medical emergency
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain how decompression sickness can be prevented.
concept: Preventing DCS in aviation centres on three strategies: limiting altitude exposure, pre-breathing oxygen, and managing the diving-flying interval. For unpressurised flight, stay below 18,000 ft when possible. Pressurised aircraft should maintain cabin altitude below the DCS threshold. Pre-breathing 100% oxygen for 30 minutes before ascending above 25,000 ft can significantly reduce risk by washing nitrogen out of the blood and tissues. For pilots who scuba dive, the diving-flying interval is critical. The general guidance is: after no-decompression dives, wait at least 12 hours before flying (24 hours is recommended for commercial operations). After decompression dives or multi-day repetitive dives, wait at least 24-48 hours. These are minimum times--longer is safer. Other prevention measures include: maintaining physical fitness, staying hydrated, avoiding alcohol before flying at altitude, and being aware that fatigue, cold, and exercise at altitude increase susceptibility. For NZ CPL pilots who enjoy recreational diving around the Poor Knights Islands or Bay of Islands, planning the diving-flying interval is a real operational consideration.
exam_tip: Know the diving-flying intervals: 12 hours minimum after no-decompression dives (24 hours recommended for commercial ops); 24-48 hours after decompression or multi-day dives. Pre-breathing 100% O2 for 30 min reduces risk.
key_facts: Limit altitude exposure; maintain cabin altitude below 18,000 ft where possible
Pre-breathe 100% O2 for 30 minutes before planned exposure above 25,000 ft
Diving-flying interval: minimum 12 hours (no-decompression), 24-48 hours (decompression dives)
Stay hydrated, avoid alcohol, maintain fitness; cold and exercise increase risk
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations, DAN Diving Guidelines

syllabus_desc: Describe how decompression sickness can be treated.
concept: In-flight treatment of suspected DCS: descend immediately to the lowest safe altitude to increase ambient pressure and drive bubbles back into solution. Apply 100% supplemental oxygen, which helps by increasing the pressure gradient for nitrogen to diffuse out of the bubbles. Keep the affected person warm, lying down if possible, and restrict physical activity (movement can dislodge bubbles and worsen symptoms). Don't attempt to pressurise the cabin beyond its normal limits. Land as soon as practicable and arrange for the affected person to be taken to a hyperbaric facility. Definitive treatment for DCS is hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT)--recompression in a pressure chamber breathing 100% oxygen. This physically shrinks the bubbles and accelerates nitrogen reabsorption. In New Zealand, hyperbaric chambers are available at the RNZN facility at Devonport, Auckland, and at Christchurch Hospital. Even if symptoms appear to resolve with descent and oxygen, medical assessment is still required because DCS can recur or have delayed complications.
exam_tip: In-flight: descend, 100% O2, keep warm, restrict activity, land ASAP. Definitive treatment: hyperbaric oxygen therapy (recompression chamber). NZ facilities: RNZN Devonport, Christchurch Hospital.
key_facts: In-flight: descend to lowest safe altitude, 100% O2, keep warm, restrict activity
Definitive treatment: hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT) in a recompression chamber
NZ hyperbaric facilities: RNZN Devonport (Auckland), Christchurch Hospital
Medical assessment required even if symptoms appear to resolve; DCS can recur
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of an explosive decompression on the body.
concept: Explosive decompression occurs when cabin pressure drops to ambient altitude pressure in less than about 0.5 seconds--typically from a structural failure, window blowout, or door seal failure at high altitude. The immediate effects on the body are dramatic: air in the lungs expands violently and must be exhaled forcefully (holding your breath during explosive decompression can rupture the lungs); trapped gas in the sinuses, ears, and GI tract expands rapidly, causing instant barotrauma; the sudden pressure drop causes immediate temperature drop and fog formation in the cabin as moisture condenses; loose objects become projectiles; there's a deafening noise from the rush of air; and the time of useful consciousness begins immediately at whatever altitude the cabin has decompressed to. If the aircraft was at FL350, you've got 30-60 seconds of useful consciousness from the moment of decompression. The physiological shock alone--noise, cold, disorientation, flying debris--can be paralysing for several critical seconds. This is why the donning of oxygen masks must be an instinctive, trained response.
exam_tip: Explosive decompression: <0.5 seconds. Immediate effects: lung expansion risk if breath held, instant barotrauma, temperature drop, fog, debris, noise. TUC begins immediately at ambient altitude.
key_facts: Occurs in less than 0.5 seconds from structural failure, window blowout, or seal failure
Lungs must exhale or risk rupture; never hold breath during decompression
Immediate barotrauma to ears, sinuses, GI tract from rapid gas expansion
TUC begins immediately at ambient flight altitude; fog, cold, noise, and debris
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the actions that must be taken to deal with an explosive decompression.
concept: The immediate actions for explosive decompression follow a rigid priority: first, oxygen masks on--this is the absolute first priority because your TUC is ticking from the moment of decompression. Don your own mask first (you can't help anyone if you're incapacitated). Confirm 100% oxygen flow. Second, communicate--advise ATC with a MAYDAY call: 'MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY, [callsign], explosive decompression, descending FL[xxx] to [lower altitude].' Third, initiate emergency descent to a safe altitude--typically 10,000 ft or the minimum safe altitude for the terrain, whichever is higher. Deploy speed brakes, reduce thrust, and descend at maximum rate. In NZ, the terrain consideration is critical--the Southern Alps, Kaimanawa Ranges, and volcanic plateau mean you can't always descend to 10,000 ft directly. Fourth, check the integrity of the aircraft--is the structure sound enough for continued flight?
