Fifth, manage passengers--cabin crew deploy oxygen masks (they should drop automatically), reassure passengers, treat injuries. Plan a diversion to the nearest suitable aerodrome.
exam_tip: Priority order: MASK ON first, communicate (MAYDAY), emergency descent, aircraft integrity check, passenger management, divert. In NZ, terrain constrains minimum descent altitude.
key_facts: First priority: don oxygen mask immediately (own mask before helping others)
MAYDAY call to ATC with decompression notification and descent intentions
Emergency descent to 10,000 ft or minimum safe altitude (terrain consideration in NZ)
Check aircraft structural integrity; manage passengers; divert to nearest suitable aerodrome
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the dangers of flying after diving.
concept: Flying after diving is dangerous because scuba diving saturates your body tissues with extra nitrogen under the increased pressure of being underwater. When you return to the surface, your body begins off-gassing this excess nitrogen gradually. If you then fly before adequate off-gassing has occurred, the reduced cabin pressure at altitude accelerates nitrogen bubble formation--effectively creating decompression sickness at altitudes that would normally be completely safe. A diver might experience DCS symptoms at a cabin altitude of just 5,000-8,000 ft, which is a normal pressurised cabin altitude for commercial flights. The deeper and longer the dive, and the more repetitive dives conducted, the more nitrogen is dissolved in tissues and the longer the required surface interval before flying. Even commercial airline flights at cabin altitudes of 6,000-8,000 ft can trigger DCS in a diver who hasn't waited long enough. For NZ pilots who dive recreationally, this means planning your diving and flying schedule carefully--no 'dive in the morning, fly in the afternoon' scenarios.
exam_tip: Flying after diving creates DCS risk at lower altitudes than normal. Even commercial cabin altitudes (6,000-8,000 ft) can trigger DCS. Minimum intervals: 12 hours (no-decompression), 24-48 hours (decompression dives).
key_facts: Diving saturates tissues with extra nitrogen under increased water pressure
Reduced cabin pressure at altitude accelerates nitrogen bubble formation
DCS can occur at normal cabin altitudes (5,000-8,000 ft) if off-gassing is incomplete
Deeper, longer, and repetitive dives require longer surface intervals before flying
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations, DAN Guidelines

syllabus_desc: State the approximate required times between diving at various depths and flying.
concept: The recommended waiting times between diving and flying depend on the type of diving conducted. After a single no-decompression dive to a depth of 30 metres or less: minimum 12 hours surface interval before flying, with 24 hours recommended for commercial operations. After repetitive no-decompression dives over multiple days: minimum 24 hours before flying. After decompression diving (dives requiring mandatory decompression stops): minimum 24-48 hours, with 48 hours recommended. After any dive where DCS symptoms occurred: do not fly until cleared by a diving medicine specialist and fully treated. These guidelines apply to all flying, including as a passenger on a commercial airline. The Divers Alert Network (DAN) and the Undersea and Hyperbaric Medical Society (UHMS) publish detailed guidelines. For NZ CPL pilots who also dive, the conservative approach is to always wait at least 24 hours after any diving before operating an aircraft. If you've been doing multi-day diving (like a liveaboard trip to the Poor Knights), wait at least 48 hours before your next flight duty.
exam_tip: Key intervals: single no-decompression dive: 12 hours minimum (24 recommended). Multi-day/repetitive: 24 hours. Decompression dives: 24-48 hours. After DCS symptoms: specialist clearance required.
key_facts: Single no-decompression dive: 12 hours minimum, 24 hours recommended for commercial ops
Multi-day repetitive no-decompression dives: minimum 24 hours before flying
Decompression diving: minimum 24-48 hours, 48 hours recommended
After any DCS symptoms: do not fly until cleared by a diving medicine specialist
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, DAN Flying After Diving Guidelines, Campbell & Bagshaw -- HPL

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between rod and cone cell functions and distribution in the retina.
concept: Your retina contains two types of photoreceptor cells, each optimised for different conditions. Cones are concentrated in the fovea (the central area of the retina) and are responsible for sharp, detailed, colour vision in good lighting conditions. You've got about 6-7 million cones, and they need relatively high light levels to function--they're your daytime, detail-reading cells. Rods are distributed across the peripheral retina (with none in the fovea) and are responsible for peripheral vision and low-light (scotopic) vision. You've got about 120 million rods--far more than cones--and they're incredibly sensitive to light but provide no colour information and poor detail. This distribution has direct implications for flying: during the day, you look directly at something to see it clearly (foveal/cone vision). At night, looking slightly off-centre--about 10-15 degrees to the side of what you're trying to see--puts the image on the rod-rich peripheral retina, where your dim-light sensitivity is best. Rods are also more sensitive to hypoxia than cones, which is why night vision degrades first at altitude.
exam_tip: Cones: central fovea, colour, detail, daylight, 6-7 million. Rods: peripheral retina, no colour, low-light, 120 million, more sensitive to hypoxia. Night technique: look 10-15 degrees off-centre.
key_facts: Cones: ~6-7 million, concentrated in fovea, colour vision, detail, require good light
Rods: ~120 million, peripheral retina (none in fovea), monochrome, sensitive to dim light
At night, use off-centre viewing (10-15 degrees) to use rod-rich peripheral retina
Rods are more sensitive to hypoxia, explaining early night vision degradation at altitude
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations of the eye in terms of: (a) the ability to discern objects at night.
concept: Night vision is fundamentally limited compared to daylight vision. Your rods take 20-30 minutes to fully dark-adapt, and even then they provide only monochrome (shades of grey), low-acuity images with no fine detail. The fovea has no rods, so anything you look directly at in darkness disappears--a phenomenon called the foveal blind spot in scotopic conditions. You need to use off-centre viewing to see objects at night, scanning slowly because rods respond more slowly than cones. Depth perception is severely reduced at night because many of the monocular depth cues (texture, colour, shadow) are absent. Distance estimation becomes unreliable. Small or unlit objects are effectively invisible until very close. The minimum light threshold for rod function is very low, but it still requires some ambient light--in truly dark conditions (overcast night, no moon, no ground lights), visual references can be essentially zero. For commercial operations, this means night approaches, especially to poorly-lit or remote NZ strips, require particular care and reliance on instruments rather than visual references.
exam_tip: At night: no colour vision, no foveal detail (use off-centre viewing), slow adaptation (20-30 min), poor depth perception, unreliable distance estimation. Night approaches to unlit strips are high-risk.
key_facts: Rods provide only monochrome, low-acuity vision at night
Fovea has no rods--direct gaze causes objects to 'disappear' (scotopic foveal blind spot)
Full dark adaptation takes 20-30 minutes; easily destroyed by bright light
Depth perception and distance estimation severely degraded at night
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations of the eye in terms of: (b) the ability to discern objects in daylight, including wires and other aircraft.
concept: Even in broad daylight, your eyes have significant limitations. Visual acuity--the ability to resolve fine detail--is only sharp within about 2 degrees of the foveal centre. Outside that narrow cone, detail drops off rapidly. This means you need to directly fixate on something to see it clearly. Wires are notoriously difficult to see because they're thin, they lack contrast against most backgrounds, and they don't move relative to the landscape. By the time you see wires visually, you may be too close to avoid them. Other aircraft are hard to spot for similar reasons: a small aircraft at distance subtends a tiny visual angle, and if it's on a collision course, it has no relative motion against the background--it just appears as a stationary dot that slowly gets bigger. Your brain's visual system is wired to detect motion, so stationary objects in the visual field are easier to miss. Haze, glare, dirty windscreens, and cockpit workload all further reduce your ability to spot objects. For commercial low-level operations in NZ (agricultural work, scenic flights through valleys), wire awareness is absolutely critical.
exam_tip: Sharp acuity only within ~2 degrees of fovea. Wires: thin, low contrast, no relative motion. Collision-course aircraft appear stationary. Scanning technique is essential for 'see and avoid.'
key_facts: Sharp visual acuity limited to ~2 degrees around the foveal centre
Wires are extremely difficult to see: thin, low contrast, no relative motion
Collision-course aircraft appear stationary with no relative motion cue
Haze, glare, dirty windscreens, and workload further reduce detection ability
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations of the eye in terms of: (c) poor lighting.
concept: Poor lighting conditions--dawn, dusk, overcast, heavy cloud shadow--create a challenging middle ground where neither your cone system nor your rod system works optimally. During mesopic conditions (twilight), both rods and cones are partially active but neither is at peak performance. Colour perception is unreliable, visual acuity is reduced, and depth perception suffers. Shadows can hide obstacles or create false impressions of terrain shape. In cockpit terms, poor lighting makes instrument reading more difficult if the panel lighting isn't adjusted properly. Outside the cockpit, runway identification from a distance is harder, terrain features are less distinct, and other aircraft are harder to spot against a grey or murky background. The transition periods--sunset and sunrise--are particularly tricky because your eyes are constantly readjusting between photopic and scotopic modes. For NZ operations, poor lighting conditions are common: overcast days on the Canterbury Plains, dawn departures from Ardmore in winter, or dusk approaches into poorly-lit rural strips.
exam_tip: Mesopic conditions (twilight) are worst: neither rods nor cones at full effectiveness. Colour unreliable, acuity reduced, depth perception degraded. Transition periods (dawn/dusk) require extra vigilance.
key_facts: Mesopic (twilight) conditions: neither rods nor cones operate at peak performance
Colour perception unreliable, visual acuity and depth perception reduced
Shadows hide obstacles and distort terrain perception
Dawn and dusk transitions require constant visual readjustment
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations of the eye in terms of: (d) glare.
concept: Glare occurs when excessive light reduces visual performance. There are two types relevant to pilots. Discomfort glare causes squinting and discomfort but doesn't necessarily reduce visual acuity significantly--like low sun in your eyes during a westerly approach at dusk. Disability glare actually reduces your ability to see by scattering light within the eye, washing out the retinal image--like trying to read instruments with sun reflecting off a scratched windscreen. The most dangerous glare scenario in aviation is low sun angle combined with a dirty or scratched windscreen, which scatters light across your entire visual field. After exposure to bright glare, your dark adaptation is impaired--stepping from bright sunlight into a shadowed cockpit on a clear day means you can't read instruments properly for several seconds. For commercial operations, glare management includes: keeping windscreens clean, using sun visors, wearing appropriate sunglasses (but not too dark for instrument reading), and being particularly cautious during approaches towards low sun.
exam_tip: Two types: discomfort glare (uncomfortable) and disability glare (reduces visual performance). Worst scenario: low sun + dirty/scratched windscreen. Glare impairs subsequent dark adaptation.
key_facts: Discomfort glare: uncomfortable but minimal visual performance reduction
Disability glare: scattered light reduces ability to see (scratched/dirty windscreen + sun)
Low sun angle combined with dirty windscreen is the worst scenario
Bright glare exposure impairs subsequent dark adaptation for several seconds
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations of the eye in terms of: (e) lack of contrast.
concept: Your visual system depends heavily on contrast to detect objects and define boundaries. When contrast is low--white aircraft against a light grey sky, terrain obscured by haze, runway markings on a faded surface--your ability to detect and identify objects drops dramatically. The minimum contrast threshold for detection varies with conditions, but as a rule, anything that reduces the brightness difference between an object and its background makes it harder to see. In NZ, common low-contrast scenarios include: flying over snow-covered terrain where horizon and surface blend together (whiteout conditions), haze over the Canterbury Plains reducing visibility to below-horizon, fog banks that blend with overcast sky making the horizon disappear, and grey aircraft against grey cloud. For object detection, contrast is actually more important than size--a high-contrast small object is easier to spot than a low-contrast large one. This is why aircraft conspicuity (bright colours, strobe lights, landing lights) matters so much for collision avoidance.
exam_tip: Contrast is more important than size for object detection. Common low-contrast hazards in NZ: whiteout over snow, haze on plains, grey aircraft against cloud. Aircraft conspicuity aids detection.
key_facts: Visual detection depends heavily on contrast between object and background
Low contrast significantly reduces ability to detect and identify objects
Contrast is more important than object size for initial detection
NZ hazards: whiteout over snow, haze, fog blending with overcast, grey-on-grey
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations of the eye in terms of: (f) the blind spot.
concept: Every eye has a physiological blind spot where the optic nerve exits the retina--an area with no photoreceptors at all. It's located about 15 degrees from the fovea on the nasal side of each eye (temporal side of the visual field). The blind spot is roughly 5-7 degrees in diameter. Normally, you don't notice it because your brain fills in the gap using information from the surrounding area and from the other eye. However, if an object falls within the blind spot of one eye and is outside the visual field of the other (or you're using only one eye), it genuinely disappears. In aviation, the blind spot means that an aircraft on a near-collision course could theoretically be invisible if it happens to sit right in your blind spot during a scan. This is one reason why effective scanning technique involves moving your eyes in segments rather than a smooth sweep--smooth eye movements allow objects in the blind spot to travel with it and remain hidden. By fixating briefly at each scan point, you give objects a chance to move out of the blind spot.
exam_tip: Blind spot: ~15 degrees from fovea, ~5-7 degrees diameter, where the optic nerve exits. Effective scanning uses segmented fixation, not smooth sweeps, to prevent objects hiding in the blind spot.
key_facts: Located about 15 degrees from the fovea on the nasal side of each retina
Approximately 5-7 degrees in diameter with no photoreceptors
Brain normally fills in the gap; dangerous when an object falls within it during scanning
Segmented scanning technique (fixate, move, fixate) helps prevent objects staying hidden
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations of the eye in terms of: (g) colour perception.
concept: Colour perception relies entirely on the three types of cone cells in your fovea, each sensitive to different wavelengths: short (blue), medium (green), and long (red). This trichromatic system gives you excellent colour discrimination in good light, but it has limitations. Colour vision degrades significantly in low light because cones need high light levels to function--at night, everything appears in shades of grey (rod vision). Colour deficiency (colour blindness) affects about 8% of males and 0.5% of females, most commonly as red-green confusion. This has direct aviation implications because many instruments, charts, and warning systems use colour coding: red for warnings, amber for cautions, green for safe. A colour-deficient pilot may not distinguish between a red warning light and an amber caution light. The CAA medical assessment tests for adequate colour perception. Coloured lenses (some sunglasses) can alter your colour perception, potentially making coloured lights and signals harder to interpret correctly. At altitude, hypoxia can subtly impair colour discrimination even in those with normal colour vision.
exam_tip: Colour vision requires cones (adequate light). Red-green deficiency affects ~8% of males. Aviation uses colour coding extensively (red/amber/green). Coloured sunglasses can alter colour perception.
key_facts: Three cone types: short (blue), medium (green), long (red) wavelength sensitivity
Colour vision degrades in low light as cones need high light levels to function
Red-green colour deficiency affects ~8% of males; tested in aviation medicals
Coloured lenses and hypoxia can impair colour discrimination
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the process of dark adaptation.
concept: Dark adaptation is the process by which your eyes adjust from bright light to darkness, primarily involving the regeneration of rhodopsin (visual purple) in the rod cells. When you move from a bright environment to a dark one, your vision initially seems very poor. Cones adapt relatively quickly (about 5-7 minutes) but reach a limited sensitivity level. Rods take much longer--about 20-30 minutes for full dark adaptation--but eventually become far more sensitive than cones. The process follows a characteristic two-phase curve: an initial quick improvement (cone adaptation) followed by a gradual continued improvement (rod adaptation). What's critical for pilots is that dark adaptation is easily destroyed: a single brief exposure to bright white light can reset the process, requiring another 20-30 minutes to recover. Red light preserves dark adaptation because rods are insensitive to long-wavelength (red) light--this is why cockpit instruments and torches for night flying use red lighting. Before a night flight, you should spend at least 20-30 minutes in a darkened environment or wearing dark adaptation goggles to achieve full rod sensitivity.
exam_tip: Full dark adaptation takes 20-30 minutes (rods). Easily destroyed by bright light. Red cockpit lighting preserves adaptation because rods are insensitive to red wavelengths. Plan 20-30 min before night flight.
key_facts: Cones adapt in ~5-7 minutes; rods require 20-30 minutes for full dark adaptation
Rhodopsin (visual purple) regeneration in rods drives the adaptation process
Brief exposure to bright white light resets adaptation, requiring 20-30 min to recover
Red light preserves dark adaptation; rods are insensitive to long (red) wavelengths
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: State the normal time for full night vision adaptation.
concept: Full dark adaptation of the rod cells takes approximately 20-30 minutes in darkness. The process follows a predictable curve: there's a noticeable improvement in the first 5-7 minutes as the cones reach their maximum dark sensitivity, then a continued gradual improvement as the rods regenerate rhodopsin and become increasingly sensitive to lower light levels. Maximum rod sensitivity is typically reached by about 30 minutes. However, achieving and maintaining full adaptation in a real cockpit environment is challenging. You've got instrument panel lighting, occasional torch use for charts, and potentially the bright flash of strobe lights from other traffic. Each bright light exposure partially resets the process. In practice, you'll rarely achieve textbook full adaptation during flight. The key is to protect what adaptation you have: use the lowest practical panel lighting, avoid looking directly at bright lights, and if you need to use a torch, make sure it's red-filtered. For NZ night operations, allow at least 30 minutes of dark adaptation before a night departure.
exam_tip: Full dark adaptation: 20-30 minutes. Cones plateau at ~5-7 minutes; rods continue improving to ~30 minutes. Protect adaptation with low panel lighting and red-filtered torches.
key_facts: Full dark adaptation takes approximately 20-30 minutes
Cone adaptation: ~5-7 minutes; rod adaptation continues to ~30 minutes
Maximum rod sensitivity at approximately 30 minutes in darkness
Allow at least 30 minutes before night flight; protect with red lighting
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Identify precautionary actions to protect night vision adaptation.
concept: Protecting night vision adaptation requires deliberate action both before and during night flight. Before flight: spend 20-30 minutes in a darkened environment or wear dark red adaptation goggles (which allow cone function for walking around while protecting rod adaptation). Avoid looking at bright screens (phones, tablets at full brightness) close to departure. During flight: use the lowest practical cockpit lighting--dim the panel as low as you can while still reading instruments. Use red-filtered torches for charts and paperwork (red light doesn't stimulate rods). Keep cabin lights dimmed. If you need to look at a bright light source (landing light, strobe), close one eye to preserve adaptation in the other. Avoid staring at bright ground lights or city lights during approach. Set electronic flight bag screens to 'night mode' with reduced brightness and red tinting. Keep the windscreen clean--dirt and scratches scatter light and increase internal glare. Supplemental oxygen above 5,000 ft at night can help maintain rod function by preventing the mild hypoxia that degrades night vision at moderate altitudes.
exam_tip: Actions: 20-30 min dark adaptation pre-flight, dim cockpit lighting, red-filtered torches, close one eye for bright lights, clean windscreen, night-mode screens, supplemental O2 above 5,000 ft at night.
key_facts: Pre-flight: 20-30 min dark adaptation or red goggles; avoid bright screens
Cockpit: lowest practical lighting, red-filtered torches, dimmed cabin lights
Close one eye when exposed to bright light to preserve adaptation in the other
Supplemental O2 above 5,000 ft at night helps maintain rod sensitivity
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe methods of cockpit/flight deck lighting and problems associated with each.
concept: Cockpit lighting must serve competing needs: enough light to read instruments and charts, but not so much that it destroys dark adaptation or creates glare and reflections. Integral instrument lighting uses lights built into the instruments themselves (edge-lit or post-lit)--advantage: each instrument evenly illuminated; problem: uniformity can make the panel look 'flat,' and dimming all instruments equally may not suit all conditions. Flood lighting illuminates the entire panel from above--advantage: simple and even coverage; problems: creates shadows in recessed instruments, can cause reflections on windscreen, and bright enough to degrade dark adaptation. Red flood lighting preserves dark adaptation but makes red markings on instruments invisible and can make some colours hard to distinguish. White flood lighting provides better colour discrimination but degrades dark adaptation. Thunderstorm lights (bright white) are used to eliminate blinding contrast from lightning but destroy night vision. Map lights need to be focused and adjustable to avoid illuminating the windscreen. Modern LED panels allow colour temperature adjustment, offering a compromise. Each method requires careful dimmer management during flight.
exam_tip: Know the trade-offs: red light preserves dark adaptation but hides red markings; white light gives better colour discrimination but degrades adaptation. Flood lighting can cause windscreen reflections.
key_facts: Integral lighting: even coverage per instrument, but can look flat; variable dimming
Flood lighting: simple even coverage, but shadows, reflections, and dark adaptation impact
Red lighting preserves dark adaptation but makes red markings invisible
Thunderstorm lights eliminate lightning contrast but destroy night vision
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements for using corrective lenses.
concept: If your aviation medical assessment determines that you need corrective lenses to meet the visual acuity standards, you must wear them at all times while exercising the privileges of your licence. This is a condition on your medical certificate. You must carry a spare pair of corrective lenses in the cockpit--if your primary glasses break or a contact lens falls out mid-flight, you need an immediate backup. Contact lenses are permitted for aviation use, but certain types are better suited: daily disposable or soft lenses are generally preferred because they're less likely to be dislodged by airflow or dry cockpit environments. Hard (gas permeable) lenses can be displaced more easily and may trap debris. In dry cockpit environments (pressurised cabins with low humidity), contact lenses can dry out and become uncomfortable. Carrying lubricating eye drops is sensible. Tinted contact lenses that alter colour perception are not acceptable. Sunglasses worn as corrective lenses must meet aviation colour perception requirements. For multifocal or progressive lenses, be aware of the limited clear zone for instrument scanning--head position becomes more critical.
exam_tip: Must carry spare corrective lenses in cockpit. Condition on medical certificate. Contact lenses: soft/daily preferred; dry cockpit environments can cause issues. Tinted contacts altering colour perception not acceptable.
key_facts: Corrective lenses must be worn when exercising licence privileges if required by medical
Spare pair must be carried in the cockpit at all times
Soft/daily disposable contact lenses generally preferred for aviation
Dry cockpit environments can cause contact lens discomfort; carry lubricating drops
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, NZ CARs Part 67 Medical Standards

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors associated with the selection of suitable sunglasses for flying.
concept: Choosing sunglasses for flying isn't just about comfort--wrong sunglasses can be genuinely dangerous. Key factors: lens colour should be neutral grey or green-grey, which provides true colour perception without distorting the colours of instruments, warning lights, or navigation aids. Avoid blue, amber, yellow, or rose-tinted lenses as they alter colour perception. The lens should block at least 70-85% of visible light transmission for general use (category 2-3 sunglasses). Polarised lenses are controversial in aviation: they reduce glare effectively but can make LCD instrument displays and some windscreen laminations appear to blank out or show rainbow patterns. They can also make it harder to see other aircraft. UV protection (UV400) is important at altitude where UV exposure is higher. Frame design should allow peripheral vision--wraparound styles restrict less than narrow frames. Gradient tints (darker at top, lighter at bottom) can help by reducing overhead glare while allowing clear view of instruments. Photochromic (auto-darkening) lenses are not ideal because they respond too slowly to rapid light changes and may not darken enough in a UV-blocking windscreen. Scratch-resistant coating helps prevent light scatter.
exam_tip: Best lens colour: neutral grey or green-grey for true colour perception. Avoid polarised (LCD issues). UV400 protection. Category 2-3 tint. Check compatibility with cockpit displays.
key_facts: Neutral grey or green-grey lenses maintain true colour perception
Polarised lenses can blank out LCD displays and reduce aircraft conspicuity
UV400 protection important due to increased UV exposure at altitude
Photochromic lenses respond too slowly and may not function behind UV-blocking windscreens
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the visual system resting state focus and its effects on object detection.
concept: When there are no distinct visual features to focus on--like flying in featureless conditions such as above cloud, in haze, or at night with no visible lights--your eyes don't focus at infinity as you might assume. Instead, they relax to a default resting focus distance of approximately 1-2 metres (some sources say 0.5-2 metres). This is called empty field myopia or the resting state of accommodation. The effect is significant: while your eyes are focused at 1-2 metres, objects at greater distances (like other aircraft) are blurred and much harder to detect. You're essentially short-sighted without knowing it. This is particularly dangerous in conditions where you need to see distant objects: other traffic while cruising on top of cloud, approaching aircraft during hazy conditions, or terrain features at night. To counteract empty field myopia, periodically refocus your eyes by looking at a distant object (wingtip, cloud feature, distant landmark) before scanning for traffic. If nothing is available, briefly focus on something inside the cockpit and then deliberately refocus out to infinity.
exam_tip: Empty field myopia: eyes default to ~1-2 metres focus when no visual features exist. Extremely dangerous in featureless conditions. Counter by periodically refocusing on distant objects.
key_facts: In featureless conditions, eyes relax to a resting focus of approximately 1-2 metres
Called empty field myopia -- effectively makes you short-sighted without awareness
Significantly reduces ability to detect distant objects (aircraft, terrain)
Counter by periodically focusing on distant objects or refocusing deliberately
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain effective visual search techniques.
concept: Effective visual scanning for traffic and hazards requires a systematic technique because the eye can only resolve detail in the narrow foveal area (~2 degrees). A smooth sweep across the sky is ineffective--your eyes skip over objects without registering them. Instead, use a segmented scan: divide the windscreen into sections (typically 10-15 degree segments), fixate briefly on each section for 1-2 seconds (enough time for the eye to focus and the brain to process), then move to the next segment. Scan from one side to the other, or use a systematic pattern that covers the entire visible sky. Most midair collisions occur in the 60-degree cone directly ahead, so give extra attention to this area. The scan should include both the area at your altitude and above and below. Periodically refocus from instruments to outside and vice versa--each transition takes about 1-2 seconds for the eye to refocus. Time spent head-down on charts, GPS, or paperwork is time not scanning. For commercial operations with a crew, assign scanning sectors. In busy airspace like the Ardmore training area, the scan rate should increase and attention to traffic should be heightened.
exam_tip: Segmented scan: 10-15 degree segments, fixate 1-2 seconds each. No smooth sweeps. Most collisions in 60-degree cone ahead. Assign scanning sectors in crew operations.
key_facts: Segmented scan: divide sky into 10-15 degree sections, fixate 1-2 seconds each
Smooth sweeps ineffective--eyes skip over objects without registering them
Most midair collisions occur in the 60-degree cone directly ahead
Eye refocus from instruments to outside takes 1-2 seconds each transition
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations, CAA See and Avoid guidance

syllabus_desc: Explain the see and avoid method of avoiding mid-air collisions.
concept: See and avoid is the primary collision avoidance method in visual flight conditions, supplemented by radio calls and transponder-based systems. The concept is straightforward: look for conflicting traffic, see it, and take action to avoid it. In practice, it's far less reliable than most pilots assume. Research shows that even in clear conditions, pilots only detect about 10-20% of potential conflicts through visual scanning alone. The limitations include: limited field of view from the cockpit, objects on a collision course showing no relative motion, limited visual acuity, scanning time split with other cockpit tasks, and the physiological limitations discussed throughout this section. To maximise the effectiveness of see and avoid: use effective scanning technique, make yourself conspicuous (lights on, bright paint, transponder active), make radio calls at appropriate positions, listen to traffic information from ATC or FISCOM, fly at appropriate altitudes for direction of travel (hemispheric rule), and plan routes to avoid congested areas. In NZ, the uncontrolled airspace around training areas (Ardmore, Hamilton, Christchurch) requires particularly vigilant scanning.
exam_tip: See and avoid has significant limitations: only 10-20% detection rate in studies. Must be supplemented with radio calls, transponder use, correct altitude selection, and effective scanning.
key_facts: Primary collision avoidance method in VMC, but has significant limitations
Research shows only 10-20% of conflicts detected through visual scanning alone
Collision-course traffic shows no relative motion, making detection difficult
Supplement with: lights, radio calls, transponder, hemispheric rule, route planning
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations, CAA See and Avoid guidance

syllabus_desc: Explain the following visual illusions: (a) autokinesis.
concept: Autokinesis is a visual illusion where a stationary point of light appears to move when viewed against a dark, featureless background. If you stare at a single star, distant light, or fixed beacon at night for more than about 6-10 seconds, your brain interprets minor involuntary eye movements as motion of the light itself. You might swear the light is drifting, climbing, descending, or circling. This is dangerous because you might mistake a stationary ground light for another aircraft and attempt to manoeuvre to avoid it, or you might try to follow what you think is a moving aircraft. During night flying over the dark Waikato farmland or over the Hauraki Gulf with scattered boat lights, autokinesis is a real risk. The prevention is simple: don't fixate on a single light source for extended periods. Keep scanning, use multiple reference points, and cross-check any visual observation against your instruments.
exam_tip: Autokinesis: stationary light appears to move after 6-10 seconds of fixation in darkness. Prevention: don't stare at single lights; scan regularly; cross-check instruments.
key_facts: Stationary point of light appears to move in darkness after prolonged fixation
Caused by involuntary eye movements misinterpreted by the brain as light movement
Risk: misidentifying fixed lights as aircraft or vice versa
Prevention: avoid fixation on single lights, maintain scanning, cross-check instruments
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the following visual illusions: (b) stroboscopic illumination illusion/flicker vertigo.
concept: Flicker vertigo (also called the flicker effect or stroboscopic illusion) occurs when a light source flickers at certain frequencies, typically between 4 and 20 Hz. In aviation, the most common cause is sunlight flickering through a rotating propeller blade--as you fly towards the sun, the prop creates a strobe effect at frequencies that can trigger neurological responses. Effects range from mild discomfort and headache to nausea, disorientation, dizziness, and in extreme cases (particularly in susceptible individuals), seizures similar to photosensitive epilepsy. Helicopter pilots are particularly susceptible because of the main rotor disc. The risk increases when the flickering light fills a large portion of the visual field. Prevention includes: wearing sunglasses to reduce the intensity of the flickering, using sun visors, changing heading to move the sun out of the propeller arc, varying engine RPM to change the flicker frequency, and being aware of the phenomenon. If you start feeling unwell with the sun flickering through the prop, change heading or use the visor immediately.
exam_tip: Flicker vertigo: 4-20 Hz flickering (typically sun through propeller) causes nausea, disorientation, possible seizures. Prevention: sunglasses, visor, change heading, vary RPM.
key_facts: Caused by light flickering at 4-20 Hz, commonly sun through propeller disc
Effects: headache, nausea, disorientation, dizziness; seizures in susceptible individuals
Helicopter pilots particularly susceptible due to main rotor disc
Prevention: sunglasses, sun visor, change heading, vary RPM
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the following visual illusions: (c) the break-off phenomenon.
concept: The break-off phenomenon is a psychological/perceptual experience reported by some pilots at high altitude, characterised by a feeling of physical detachment or separation from the earth and the aircraft. Pilots describe it as feeling isolated, disconnected, or as if they're floating alone in space. It tends to occur during solo flights at high altitude in clear conditions with minimal ground features visible--essentially when normal visual and spatial reference cues are reduced. Some pilots find it pleasant (a sense of peace or unreality); others find it deeply unsettling, causing anxiety or even panic. The phenomenon is thought to result from reduced sensory input at high altitude: fewer visual references, reduced engine noise at cruise, stable flight conditions, and the vast emptiness of high-altitude sky. It's been reported most commonly above 30,000-40,000 ft but can occur lower. For CPL operations, it's more relevant to pressurised high-altitude flights. The key is to recognise it for what it is and not let it trigger anxiety or inappropriate control inputs. Refocus on instruments, tasks, and communication to re-engage with reality.
exam_tip: Break-off phenomenon: feeling of detachment from earth at high altitude, reduced sensory input. Can cause anxiety or euphoria. Refocus on instruments and tasks to counter it.
key_facts: Feeling of physical detachment from earth and aircraft at high altitude
Occurs in conditions of reduced sensory input: high altitude, clear conditions, solo flight
Can produce pleasant detachment or unsettling anxiety
Counter by refocusing on instruments, tasks, and communication
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the following visual illusions: (d) sector whiteout.
concept: Sector whiteout occurs when a portion of the terrain--typically snow-covered ground--blends with the overcast sky, eliminating the visible horizon in that sector while other sectors retain visual references. It's different from total whiteout in that you can still see the ground or horizon in some directions, which creates a dangerous false sense of security. You might have a clear horizon to the east but a featureless white void to the west where snow-covered terrain meets overcast cloud. The danger is that your brain may attempt to construct a complete horizon using the partial information available, leading to spatial disorientation if the visible horizon doesn't align with the actual attitude. In NZ, sector whiteout is a genuine risk when flying over snow-covered terrain in the Southern Alps, Central Plateau, or Canterbury foothills during overcast conditions. Pilots operating scenic flights around Mount Cook or helicopter operations in winter conditions must be particularly alert to this. The mitigation is instrument crosscheck and awareness that partial visual references can be misleading.
exam_tip: Sector whiteout: partial loss of horizon where snow meets overcast in one sector while others remain clear. Creates false sense of security. NZ risk: Southern Alps, Canterbury foothills in winter.
key_facts: Portion of terrain blends with overcast sky, eliminating horizon in that sector
Other sectors retain visual references, creating dangerous false sense of security
Brain may construct incorrect horizon from partial visual information
NZ risk: Southern Alps, Central Plateau, Canterbury foothills in winter overcast
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the following visual illusions: (e) the black hole phenomenon.
concept: The black hole phenomenon occurs during night approaches over dark, featureless terrain (water, unlit rural areas) towards a lit runway with no surrounding ground lights. Without peripheral visual cues, the only reference is the runway lighting itself, and your brain has insufficient information to judge height, distance, or glidepath accurately. Pilots consistently tend to fly too low on a black hole approach because the lack of peripheral light cues removes the normal visual impression of being 'above' the terrain. The runway lights appear to be at the correct angle, but without surrounding reference, the pilot unconsciously descends below the normal glidepath. This has caused numerous CFIT (controlled flight into terrain) accidents worldwide. In NZ, black hole conditions are common: night approaches over the Hauraki Gulf into Auckland, over dark farmland into many rural strips, or over water into island aerodromes. The mitigation is critical: use PAPI/VASIS where available, fly a stabilised approach on instruments (ILS, RNAV, or published visual approach procedure), and do not attempt a purely visual approach in black hole conditions.
exam_tip: Black hole: night approach over dark terrain to lit runway -- pilot tends to fly too low. Major CFIT cause. Use PAPI, fly instrument-guided approaches, don't go fully visual in black hole conditions.
key_facts: Night approach over dark featureless terrain to lit runway without surrounding lights
Pilot consistently flies too low due to lack of peripheral visual height references
Major cause of CFIT (controlled flight into terrain) accidents worldwide
Mitigation: use PAPI/VASIS, fly instrument-guided approach, don't go purely visual
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the methods of avoiding and/or coping with: (a) autokinesis.
concept: To avoid or cope with autokinesis: never fixate on a single light source in darkness for more than a few seconds. Maintain a regular scanning pattern, moving your eyes between multiple reference points--instruments, different external lights, and the horizon if visible. If you suspect a light is exhibiting autokinetic movement, cross-check against your instruments (heading, altitude, attitude) to determine whether the movement is real or illusory. Use multiple external light sources as references rather than relying on a single point. If a light appears to be moving, try to identify it positively before taking any evasive action--it may be a star, ground light, or boat light that your brain is making 'move.' Keep cockpit workload manageable so you can maintain the scanning discipline needed. In multi-crew operations, having the other pilot confirm what they see helps differentiate illusion from reality.
exam_tip: Cope with autokinesis: don't fixate, maintain scanning, cross-check instruments, use multiple references, confirm observations with crew. Never take evasive action based on perceived movement of a single light.
key_facts: Never fixate on a single light source for more than a few seconds
Maintain regular scanning pattern using multiple visual references
Cross-check any perceived movement against instruments
In multi-crew, confirm observations with other crew member before acting
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the methods of avoiding and/or coping with: (b) stroboscopic illumination illusion/flicker vertigo.
concept: To avoid flicker vertigo: wear good quality sunglasses when flying toward the sun with a rotating propeller in the line of sight. Use the cockpit sun visor to block the sun from the propeller arc. If neither is effective, change heading to move the sun out of the propeller disc. Vary engine RPM slightly to change the flicker frequency away from the problematic 4-20 Hz range. If symptoms begin (headache, nausea, dizziness), act immediately rather than trying to push through. In helicopters, sun visors and sunglasses are the primary defence against rotor flicker. If you know you're susceptible (some individuals are more sensitive than others), plan routes and timing to minimise direct sun-through-prop exposure. For those with a history of epilepsy or photosensitivity, this is a significant medical consideration for flight fitness.
exam_tip: Cope: sunglasses, sun visor, change heading, vary RPM, act immediately on symptoms. Some individuals more susceptible--history of photosensitivity is a medical consideration.
key_facts: Wear sunglasses to reduce flicker intensity through propeller disc
Use sun visor; change heading to move sun from propeller arc
Vary RPM to shift flicker frequency out of problematic 4-20 Hz range
Act immediately on symptoms; photosensitive individuals are at higher risk
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the methods of avoiding and/or coping with: (c) the break-off phenomenon.
concept: Coping with the break-off phenomenon is primarily about recognition and mental reengagement. If you experience the sensation of detachment at altitude, recognise it for what it is--a normal perceptual response to reduced sensory input, not a sign that something is wrong. Deliberately re-engage with your tasks: scan instruments, verify altitude and heading, make a radio call, review your flight plan. Physical actions help ground you: grip the control column, adjust your seat, or physically look around the cockpit. In multi-crew operations, conversation with the other pilot provides immediate sensory reengagement. If the sensation persists or causes anxiety, consider descending to a lower altitude where more visual references are available. Don't try to fight or suppress the feeling--acknowledge it and redirect your attention. Pre-briefing for flights at high altitude where the phenomenon might occur helps by setting expectations.
exam_tip: Coping: recognise it as normal, re-engage with cockpit tasks, make radio calls, physical actions, crew conversation. Pre-brief for high-altitude flights.
key_facts: Recognise as normal perceptual response to reduced sensory input at altitude
Deliberately re-engage: scan instruments, make radio calls, review flight plan
Physical actions help: grip controls, adjust seat, look around cockpit
Consider descending if persistent; pre-brief for high-altitude flights
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the methods of avoiding and/or coping with: (d) sector whiteout.
concept: Coping with sector whiteout requires recognising that partial visual references may be unreliable. If you notice the horizon has disappeared in one or more sectors while remaining visible in others, immediately transition to instrument references for attitude and altitude. Do not trust a partial horizon--your brain will attempt to extrapolate, often incorrectly. Increase instrument scan rate and maintain a stabilised flight path. If conditions deteriorate, consider turning back to an area with better visual references or diverting. In mountainous NZ terrain, this may mean descending below cloud or routing around areas of snow-covered terrain. Plan ahead: if the forecast suggests overcast conditions over snow-covered areas, include instrument approach-capable alternates in your flight plan. Keep terrain awareness systems active and closely monitored. In multi-crew operations, one pilot should maintain primary instrument reference while the other manages navigation and lookout. Never attempt to descend or manoeuvre in mountainous terrain using only the visible sectors of the horizon.
exam_tip: Sector whiteout: immediately transition to instruments. Don't trust partial horizon. Consider turning back or diverting. In NZ mountains, terrain awareness is critical. Never manoeuvre using partial visual references.
key_facts: Immediately transition to full instrument reference when horizon partially lost
Do not trust partial horizon--brain extrapolates incorrectly
Consider turning back or diverting to areas with better visual references
In NZ mountains, maintain terrain awareness; plan instrument-capable alternates
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the methods of avoiding and/or coping with: (e) the black hole phenomenon.
concept: The black hole phenomenon is best managed by avoidance: never attempt a purely visual approach in black hole conditions. Use every available approach aid: PAPI/VASIS gives a visual glidepath reference independent of surrounding terrain lights. ILS, RNAV, VOR/DME approaches provide electronic glidepath guidance. If no precision or non-precision approach is available, use the published circling minima and a stabilised constant-angle approach technique. Set up the approach early, maintain a stable descent rate, and cross-check altitude against distance-to-go. Be particularly vigilant on final approach: if you're going below glidepath, the PAPI will show increasing red lights. If no approach aids are available and conditions present a black hole scenario, seriously consider diverting to an aerodrome with better approach facilities. In NZ, many rural strips lack PAPI and instrument approaches--night operations to these fields in black hole conditions should be avoided by commercial operators. Brief the approach carefully, set a minimum descent altitude, and execute a go-around if anything doesn't look right.
exam_tip: Black hole: avoid purely visual approaches. Use PAPI/VASIS, ILS/RNAV, stabilised approach. Consider diverting if no approach aids available. Brief minimum altitudes; go around if unsure.
key_facts: Never attempt purely visual approach in black hole conditions
Use PAPI/VASIS, ILS, RNAV, or VOR/DME approach for glidepath guidance
Set up early, maintain stable descent, cross-check altitude vs distance
Consider diverting if no approach aids available; go around if unsure
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe conditions which can lead to the creation of a false horizon.
concept: A false horizon occurs when visual cues create an apparent horizon that doesn't align with the true horizon. Common conditions include: a sloping cloud layer that appears level; city lights on rising terrain that create an angled line of lights mimicking a tilted horizon; stars near the horizon being confused with ground lights (or vice versa); lights on a hillside or mountainside appearing as if they're on flat ground; a band of cloud or haze that creates a distinct visual line that isn't actually horizontal; and flying along a coastline at night where the edge of ground lights creates a visual reference that may be misleading if the coast isn't straight. In NZ, the volcanic terrain of the Central Plateau, the tilted Canterbury Plains viewed against the distant Southern Alps, and the scattered lights of Coromandel Peninsula can all create false horizon situations at night. The danger is that a pilot who uses the false horizon as an attitude reference will unknowingly bank the aircraft. Prevention requires regular instrument crosscheck and awareness that visual horizons can be unreliable, especially at night.
exam_tip: False horizons: sloping cloud, terrain lights, stars confused with ground lights, haze bands, coastal light lines. Prevention: regular instrument crosscheck; never rely solely on visual horizon at night.
key_facts: Sloping cloud layers and haze bands can mimic a tilted horizon
City/terrain lights on rising ground create misleading angled light lines
Stars near the horizon can be confused with ground lights and vice versa
Prevention: maintain instrument crosscheck; visual horizons unreliable at night
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of a false horizon on visual perception.
concept: A false horizon misleads the pilot into believing the aircraft is in a particular attitude when it isn't. If you unconsciously align yourself with a sloping cloud bank that you perceive as level, you'll bank the aircraft without realising it. Your vestibular system, which has its own limitations (especially the somatogyral illusion in sustained turns), may not detect a gradual bank, so the false visual reference goes unchallenged. The result can be a gradual spiral descent or an unintended turn--both potentially dangerous, especially at night or in reduced visibility. The effect is compounded when fatigue reduces your monitoring of instruments, or when high cockpit workload diverts your attention from the attitude indicator. In extreme cases, pilots have flown controlled flight into terrain because they aligned with a false horizon formed by terrain lights. The key defence is to treat the attitude indicator as the primary attitude reference and use outside visual cues only as supporting information, particularly at night and in marginal conditions.
exam_tip: False horizon causes unconscious banking. Vestibular system may not detect gradual bank. Defence: attitude indicator is primary reference; visual cues are supporting only, especially at night.
key_facts: Pilot unconsciously aligns aircraft with false visual horizon, causing undetected bank
Vestibular system may not detect gradual bank (somatogyral illusion)
Can lead to spiral descent or CFIT, especially at night with fatigue
Defence: attitude indicator as primary reference; visual cues supporting only
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain relative motion.
concept: Relative motion is the apparent movement of an object as perceived from your moving viewpoint. In aviation, understanding relative motion is critical for collision avoidance. An aircraft on a collision course with you will appear as a stationary point in your windscreen--it has no relative motion against the background because both aircraft are converging on the same point. This is the most dangerous traffic scenario because the lack of movement makes the target extremely hard to detect. Conversely, an aircraft that's not a collision threat will appear to track across your windscreen--the greater the relative motion, the less the collision risk. Other relative motion effects: ground features appear to move past you faster when you're lower and slower (useful for speed awareness in low-level operations), and parallax effects can make objects at different distances appear to move at different rates, which helps with depth perception during approaches. Understanding relative motion helps you prioritise your scan: focus most on targets with the least apparent motion--those are the ones heading for you.
exam_tip: Collision-course traffic has ZERO relative motion--appears stationary. This makes it the hardest to detect and the most dangerous. Targets moving across your windscreen are NOT collision threats. Prioritise scanning for stationary targets.
key_facts: Collision-course aircraft appears stationary with no relative motion against background
This makes collision-course traffic the hardest to detect visually
Aircraft with relative motion across your field of view are NOT collision threats
Prioritise scanning for targets with least apparent movement
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of fog, haze, and/or dust on visual perception.
concept: Fog, haze, and dust all reduce visibility and degrade visual perception in specific ways. They scatter light, reducing contrast between objects and their background, making everything harder to see. Distance estimation becomes unreliable: in haze, objects appear further away than they actually are because the reduced contrast mimics distance. This can lead to flying closer to terrain or obstacles than you think. Colours become washed out, reducing the effectiveness of colour-coded navigation aids and terrain features. Runway visibility in fog or haze can deteriorate rapidly and unevenly. Slant visibility (your forward view from the cockpit on approach) is always less than ground-level visibility because you're looking through more atmosphere at a shallow angle. In NZ, morning fog in the Waikato valley, sea fog rolling into Wellington, and the haze that builds over Canterbury on hot days all reduce visual perception. Dust is less common in NZ but relevant for unpaved strip operations. The practical consequence: your visual approach may look fine from altitude, but as you descend into the haze layer, forward visibility drops dramatically.
exam_tip: Fog/haze makes objects appear FURTHER away than actual. Slant visibility is always less than ground-level visibility. In haze, you're closer to terrain than you think. NZ: Waikato fog, Wellington sea fog, Canterbury haze.
key_facts: Fog, haze, and dust scatter light, reducing contrast and degrading visual perception
Objects appear further away in haze, leading to underestimation of proximity
Slant visibility is always less than reported ground-level visibility
Distance estimation unreliable; approach planning must account for reduced slant range
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the optical characteristics of the windshield.
concept: Aircraft windshields are not optically perfect, and their characteristics affect visual perception. Curved windshields (common in most GA and commercial aircraft) produce some degree of optical distortion, particularly near the edges and at the junction between windshield panels. This can make objects appear slightly displaced from their true position. Scratches, crazing, and surface contamination scatter light, creating internal glare and reducing contrast--particularly problematic when flying toward the sun or at night with oncoming lights. Rain on the windshield refracts light and distorts the view (addressed separately). Some windshields have heating elements or coatings that can create visual artifacts. Tinted windshields reduce light transmission, which helps with glare but reduces visibility in low-light conditions. Multi-layer laminated windshields may have slight colour tinting between layers. In pressurised aircraft, the windshield thickness (required for pressure containment) adds more optical distortion. The practical implications: don't assume what you see through the windshield is exactly where it actually is, keep the windshield clean, and report any scratching, crazing, or delamination.
exam_tip: Windshield issues: optical distortion (especially near edges), scratches scatter light, reduced contrast, colour tinting. Keep windshields clean and report damage. Curved panels add distortion.
key_facts: Curved windshields produce optical distortion, especially near edges and panel junctions
Scratches and crazing scatter light, creating glare and reducing contrast
Tinted windshields reduce light transmission, problematic in low-light conditions
Pressurised aircraft: thicker windshields add more optical distortion
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of rain on the windshield.
concept: Rain on the windshield significantly degrades visual perception through several mechanisms. Water droplets refract (bend) light passing through them, distorting the image of outside objects and making them appear displaced from their true position. A continuous water film creates a moving lens effect that makes outside references shimmer and shift. Heavy rain dramatically reduces forward visibility and contrast. At night, rain on the windshield scatters light from runway lights, approach lights, and other aircraft, creating a blurred, dispersed glow rather than defined points. During approach, rain on the windshield can make the runway appear further away than it actually is, potentially leading to a higher-than-normal approach. The refraction effect can also make the runway threshold appear to be in a slightly different position than reality, affecting your touchdown point. In NZ, you'll encounter rain frequently: frontal rain on the West Coast, afternoon convective showers in summer, and persistent drizzle in Southland. The key is to recognise that your visual references in rain are degraded and to rely more heavily on instruments, PAPI, and approach aids rather than pure visual judgement.
exam_tip: Rain on windshield: refraction displaces objects, reduces visibility, scatters lights at night. Makes runway appear further away. Rely on instruments and approach aids, not pure visual judgment, in rain.
key_facts: Water droplets refract light, displacing objects from their true position
Heavy rain dramatically reduces forward visibility and contrast
At night, rain scatters light from runway/approach lights into blurred glare
Runway may appear further away in rain; rely on instruments and approach aids
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of sloping terrain on visual perception.
concept: Sloping terrain creates significant visual illusions during approach, particularly when using visual references rather than instruments. If you're approaching over terrain that slopes upward toward the runway, it compresses your visual perspective and makes it look like you're higher than you actually are. Your natural response is to descend below the normal glidepath--exactly the wrong thing to do. Conversely, terrain sloping downward toward the runway expands your perspective, making you think you're lower than you are, and you fly a higher approach. In NZ, sloping terrain is incredibly common: Queenstown's approach over the lake involves rising terrain to the north; many rural strips in the foothills have significant terrain slopes; and river valley aerodromes often have terrain that falls away on one side while rising on the other. The illusion is strongest when you're relying purely on visual cues without PAPI or instrument guidance. Awareness of the illusion, familiarity with the specific aerodrome, and use of PAPI/VASIS or a stabilised approach profile (3-degree descent with altitude checks at known distances) are your best defences.
exam_tip: Upslope terrain: you think you're high, so you fly low (dangerous). Downslope: you think you're low, so you fly high. NZ is full of sloping terrain. Use PAPI and stabilised approach profiles.
key_facts: Upward sloping terrain makes pilot think they're higher -- leads to dangerously low approach
Downward sloping terrain makes pilot think they're lower -- leads to high approach
Illusion strongest in pure visual approaches without PAPI or instrument guidance
Defence: PAPI/VASIS, stabilised approach profile, aerodrome familiarity
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on visual perception during approach situations: (a) steep/shallow approach angles.
concept: Approach angle significantly affects your visual perception of the runway. On a steeper-than-normal approach, the runway appears compressed (shorter and narrower), which can make you think you're higher and closer than you actually are. This can lead to landing long or flaring high. On a shallower-than-normal approach, the runway appears elongated and wider, which can make you feel lower and further away, potentially leading to landing short or making a premature flare. Your brain calibrates runway perspective based on experience with normal 3-degree approaches, so any deviation from that creates a visual mismatch. In NZ, steep approaches are required at some aerodromes (Milford Sound, Queenstown circling approaches, mountain strips) and commercial operators need specific training. The key is to use PAPI/approach aids where available, cross-check your altitude against distance, and maintain a stabilised approach speed and descent rate regardless of visual impressions.
exam_tip: Steep approach: runway looks compressed, fly long or flare high. Shallow approach: runway looks elongated, land short or premature flare. Use PAPI and cross-check altitude vs distance.
key_facts: Steep approach: runway appears shorter and narrower (compressed perspective)
Shallow approach: runway appears longer and wider (elongated perspective)
Brain calibrated for normal 3-degree approach; deviations create visual mismatch
Use PAPI, altitude vs distance crosscheck, and stabilised approach technique
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on visual perception during approach situations: (b) length, width and texture of the runway.
concept: Runway dimensions and surface characteristics powerfully influence your visual perception during approach. A wider-than-usual runway makes you think you're lower than you are (leading to a high approach); a narrower runway makes you think you're higher (leading to a dangerously low approach). Similarly, a longer runway appears closer and can make you fly high; a shorter runway appears further away and can lead to flying low. Runway texture and surface colour affect perception too: a dark-surfaced runway (fresh asphalt) appears shorter than a light-surfaced one (aged concrete); a runway with clear markings and obvious centreline provides better visual cues than one with faded markings. If you normally operate from a 30-metre-wide sealed runway at Ardmore and then visit a 15-metre-wide grass strip in the Wairarapa, the narrow strip will make you think you're higher on approach than you actually are. Awareness of these illusions and disciplined use of PAPI and approach aids is essential, particularly when visiting unfamiliar aerodromes.
exam_tip: Wide/long runway: think you're low, fly high. Narrow/short runway: think you're high, fly LOW (dangerous). This is a classic exam scenario. Always check runway dimensions in AIP before visiting unfamiliar strips.
key_facts: Wider/longer runway: pilot thinks they're lower, approaches high
Narrower/shorter runway: pilot thinks they're higher, approaches dangerously low
Dark surfaces appear shorter; light surfaces appear longer
Check unfamiliar aerodrome dimensions in AIP NZ Vol 4 before approach
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on visual perception during approach situations: (c) the intensity of the approach lights.
concept: Approach light intensity affects your perception of distance during night approaches. Brighter-than-expected approach lights make the runway appear closer than it actually is, potentially leading to a premature descent or early configuration change. Dimmer-than-expected lights make the runway appear further away, which can lead to flying a higher approach or continuing descent below a safe altitude thinking you're still far from the field. The effect is most pronounced at night when approach lights are your primary visual reference. In NZ, the variation between well-lit major aerodromes (Auckland, Christchurch, Wellington with full approach lighting systems) and smaller aerodromes with minimal or no approach lighting creates a significant contrast. A pilot accustomed to the bright approach lights at Christchurch who then flies a night approach into a dimly-lit rural strip will perceive the runway as further away than it is. Conversely, high-intensity approach lights in poor visibility can create a halo effect in rain or fog, making distance estimation even less reliable. The defence is the same as always: use all available approach aids, maintain a stabilised approach, and cross-check altitude against distance.
exam_tip: Bright lights: runway appears closer (premature descent risk). Dim lights: runway appears further (fly too low risk). Night approaches to unfamiliar aerodromes with different lighting intensity require extra caution.
key_facts: Brighter lights make runway appear closer than actual (premature descent risk)
Dimmer lights make runway appear further away (flying too low risk)
Effect most pronounced at night when lights are primary visual reference
Defence: stabilised approach, PAPI, altitude vs distance crosscheck
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic anatomy of the ear.
concept: The ear has three main sections: outer, middle, and inner. The outer ear (pinna and ear canal) collects sound waves and funnels them to the tympanic membrane (eardrum). The middle ear is an air-filled cavity containing three tiny bones--the ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes)--that amplify and transmit vibrations from the eardrum to the inner ear. The middle ear connects to the throat via the eustachian tube, which allows pressure equalisation. The inner ear contains the cochlea (a snail-shaped structure for hearing) and the vestibular system (semicircular canals and otolith organs for balance and spatial orientation). The cochlea converts mechanical vibrations into nerve impulses sent to the brain via the auditory nerve. The vestibular apparatus detects angular acceleration (semicircular canals) and linear acceleration/gravity (utricle and saccule). For pilots, all three sections matter: the outer and middle ear are vulnerable to barotrauma, the cochlea is vulnerable to noise damage, and the vestibular system is your primary source of spatial orientation--and spatial illusions.
exam_tip: Know the three sections and their functions: outer (collection), middle (amplification, pressure equalisation via eustachian tube), inner (hearing via cochlea, balance via vestibular system).
key_facts: Outer ear: pinna and ear canal -- collects and funnels sound to eardrum
Middle ear: ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes) amplify vibrations; eustachian tube equalises pressure
Inner ear cochlea: converts vibrations to nerve impulses for hearing
Inner ear vestibular system: semicircular canals (angular) and otoliths (linear acceleration/gravity)
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the physiology of the ear.
concept: The ear's physiology involves two parallel systems: hearing and balance. For hearing: sound waves vibrate the eardrum, which transfers energy through the ossicle chain (malleus > incus > stapes) to the oval window of the cochlea. Inside the cochlea, fluid waves stimulate hair cells on the basilar membrane, which convert mechanical energy to electrical nerve impulses. Different frequencies stimulate different regions of the basilar membrane--high frequencies near the base, low frequencies near the apex. The auditory nerve carries these impulses to the brain for processing. For balance: the three semicircular canals (oriented roughly at right angles to each other) detect angular acceleration in three planes. Fluid (endolymph) inside the canals moves when your head rotates, deflecting a gelatinous structure called the cupula, which bends hair cells to generate nerve impulses. The otolith organs (utricle and saccule) detect linear acceleration and gravity through tiny calcium carbonate crystals (otoconia) resting on hair cells. The vestibular nerve sends this information to the brain, which integrates it with visual and proprioceptive inputs for spatial orientation.
exam_tip: Hearing: sound > eardrum > ossicles > cochlea > hair cells > auditory nerve. Balance: semicircular canals detect angular acceleration; otolith organs detect linear acceleration and gravity.
key_facts: Hearing: sound vibrates eardrum, transmitted through ossicles to cochlea hair cells
Cochlea basilar membrane: high frequencies at base, low at apex
Semicircular canals: three orthogonal canals detect angular acceleration via endolymph/cupula
Otolith organs (utricle, saccule): detect linear acceleration and gravity via otoconia
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of prolonged noise exposure on hearing.
concept: Prolonged exposure to excessive noise causes progressive, irreversible hearing damage. The mechanism is destruction of the delicate hair cells in the cochlea--once damaged or destroyed, they don't regenerate. The damage typically starts with high-frequency hearing loss (around 4,000 Hz, which appears as the '4 kHz notch' on an audiogram) because the hair cells responsible for high frequencies are located at the base of the cochlea, where they receive the most intense stimulation. This is noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL) and it's the most common occupational health hazard in aviation. It's cumulative: each exposure adds to the total damage, even if individual exposures are below the pain threshold. A pilot spending 20 years in piston aircraft cockpits (noise levels typically 85-100+ dB) without hearing protection will almost certainly develop significant NIHL. The hearing loss is bilateral (both ears), permanent, and progressive. Early stages may go unnoticed because speech frequencies (300-3,000 Hz) are initially preserved. By the time you notice difficulty hearing ATC calls or conversation, significant damage has already occurred.
exam_tip: NIHL is cumulative, irreversible, and starts at 4,000 Hz (the 4 kHz notch). Piston aircraft cockpits are 85-100+ dB. Hair cells don't regenerate. Prevention through hearing protection is the only defence.
key_facts: Prolonged noise destroys cochlear hair cells irreversibly
Damage starts at 4,000 Hz (4 kHz notch on audiogram), then spreads
Piston aircraft cockpits: 85-100+ dB -- exceeds safe exposure limits
Damage is cumulative, bilateral, permanent, and progressive
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe methods of protecting hearing.
concept: Hearing protection in aviation involves reducing the noise energy reaching your cochlea. Methods include: passive noise-attenuating headsets (standard aviation headsets provide about 15-25 dB of noise reduction through physical padding and ear cup seal); active noise-cancelling (ANC) headsets (use electronic processing to generate anti-phase sound that cancels low-frequency noise, providing an additional 15-20 dB reduction--highly effective against engine noise); foam earplugs worn under headsets for additional protection in very noisy environments; helmet-mounted communication systems in helicopters (provide both impact protection and noise attenuation). The best approach is ANR headsets, which are now standard in professional aviation. Beyond wearing protection, limiting exposure duration helps: in environments above 85 dB, reduce continuous exposure time. Keep cockpit noise to a minimum: properly maintained exhausts, good door and window seals, insulation. Regular audiometric testing (part of the aviation medical) monitors for early hearing loss. For NZ CPL pilots flying piston singles, investing in a good ANR headset isn't a luxury--it's essential occupational health equipment.
exam_tip: Methods: passive headsets (15-25 dB), ANR headsets (additional 15-20 dB), earplugs under headsets, helmets. Regular audiometric testing monitors for early damage. ANR headsets are the standard for professional pilots.
key_facts: Passive headsets: 15-25 dB noise reduction through physical attenuation
Active noise-cancelling (ANR): additional 15-20 dB, especially effective at low frequencies
Foam earplugs under headsets for additional protection in very noisy cockpits
Regular audiometric testing as part of aviation medical monitors for early hearing loss
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Specify the various levels of noise in decibels at which various grades of hearing protection are required.
concept: Noise exposure limits in the workplace follow a general framework based on cumulative energy: 85 dB(A) is the action level--above this, hearing protection should be provided and noise monitoring begins. At 85 dB, the maximum recommended continuous exposure is 8 hours. For every 3 dB increase, the safe exposure time halves: 88 dB = 4 hours, 91 dB = 2 hours, 94 dB = 1 hour, 97 dB = 30 minutes, 100 dB = 15 minutes. Above 115 dB, hearing protection is mandatory regardless of exposure duration. The pain threshold is about 130 dB. In typical aviation environments: a Cessna 172 cockpit is about 85-90 dB, a piston twin might be 90-95 dB, a turboprop is 80-90 dB, and a helicopter can be 95-105 dB. Given that commercial pilots may spend 6-8 hours in these environments daily, hearing protection isn't optional. NZ workplace noise regulations (under the Health and Safety at Work Act) align with these international standards. The 85 dB action level means that virtually all unpressurised aviation requires hearing protection.
exam_tip: 85 dB = action level (8 hours max). Each 3 dB increase halves safe time. 88 dB = 4 hrs, 91 dB = 2 hrs, 94 dB = 1 hr. Piston cockpits are 85-100 dB -- hearing protection is mandatory for commercial pilots.
key_facts: 85 dB(A): action level, maximum 8 hours continuous exposure
