For NZ CPL pilots, the physical demands of the job itself (loading luggage, pre-flight inspections, long days) require a baseline fitness level that sedentary habits won't sustain.
exam_tip: Regular exercise improves G tolerance, hypoxia resistance, cognitive function, sleep, and stress management. Don't exercise intensely immediately before flying (fatigue risk). Maintain regular moderate routine.
key_facts: Regular exercise: improves cardiovascular fitness, G tolerance, cognitive function, sleep, stress management
Sedentary lifestyle: poor fitness, weight gain, fatigue susceptibility, declining health
Don't exercise intensely immediately before flying -- allow recovery time
Regular moderate exercise routine preferred over sporadic intense sessions
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: (e) obesity.
concept: Obesity (BMI >30) has multiple negative effects on pilot performance and health. Cardiovascular: increased workload on the heart, higher blood pressure, increased risk of coronary artery disease and stroke. Respiratory: reduced lung capacity, increased susceptibility to hypoxia (the body demands more oxygen but the lungs deliver relatively less), and increased risk of sleep apnoea (which causes chronic fatigue and daytime sleepiness). Metabolic: increased risk of type 2 diabetes, which affects fitness to fly through blood sugar instability and associated complications. Physical: reduced cockpit mobility, difficulty with emergency egress, increased susceptibility to heat stress, and reduced G tolerance. Professional: obesity can threaten your medical certificate if associated conditions develop. Management: balanced diet, regular exercise, address underlying causes (stress eating, sedentary lifestyle), and seek medical support if needed. The aviation medical will monitor BMI and associated health markers. For NZ CPL pilots, maintaining a healthy weight is not vanity--it's a professional obligation that directly affects your ability to do the job safely and to keep your medical.
exam_tip: Obesity: cardiovascular strain, reduced lung capacity, increased hypoxia susceptibility, sleep apnoea risk, reduced cockpit mobility, diabetes risk. Threatens medical certificate. Management: diet, exercise, medical support.
key_facts: Cardiovascular: increased heart workload, higher BP, increased coronary disease risk
Respiratory: reduced lung capacity, increased hypoxia susceptibility, sleep apnoea risk
Metabolic: increased type 2 diabetes risk with blood sugar instability
Physical: reduced cockpit mobility, emergency egress difficulty, reduced G tolerance
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: (f) smoking.
concept: Smoking has devastating effects on pilot performance, both acute and chronic. Acutely: carbon monoxide from cigarette smoke binds to haemoglobin 200-250 times more readily than oxygen, reducing your blood's oxygen-carrying capacity by 5-8% for a pack-a-day smoker. This is equivalent to starting every flight at 3,000-5,000 ft higher in terms of physiological altitude. Night vision degrades earlier, hypoxia symptoms onset at lower altitudes, and cognitive function is subtly impaired. Chronically: smoking causes lung disease (COPD, emphysema) reducing respiratory capacity, cardiovascular disease (heart attack, stroke risk), and cancer. All of these threaten your medical certificate and your life. Nicotine itself causes vasoconstriction (narrowing blood vessels), elevated heart rate, and stimulant effects that can mask fatigue until sudden collapse. Management: stop smoking. There is no safe level. The benefits begin immediately--within hours, CO levels drop; within weeks, circulation improves; within months, lung function begins recovering. Nicotine replacement therapy (patches, gum) is generally acceptable for aviation use during cessation, but check with your DAME. The aviation medical doesn't prohibit smoking, but it monitors the damage.
exam_tip: Smoking: CO binds haemoglobin 200-250x more than O2, effectively adding 3,000-5,000 ft to your physiological altitude. Reduces night vision, G tolerance, and cognitive function. Stop smoking -- there is no safe level.
key_facts: CO from smoking reduces O2 carrying capacity by 5-8%, adding 3,000-5,000 ft equivalent altitude
Degrades night vision, hypoxia tolerance, and cognitive function acutely
Chronically: COPD, cardiovascular disease, cancer -- all threaten medical certificate
Only management: cessation. Benefits begin immediately. NRT generally acceptable for pilots
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: (g) respiratory tract infection/allergies (including colds, sinus, hay fever, influenza, asthma).
concept: Respiratory infections and allergies affect pilot performance in multiple ways. Direct effects: nasal and sinus congestion blocks pressure equalisation (barotrauma risk during climbs and descents), excess mucus causes coughing and distraction, fever increases metabolic rate and oxygen demand (reducing hypoxia tolerance), fatigue and general malaise reduce concentration and decision-making. Specific conditions: a simple cold causes eustachian tube swelling (otic barotrauma risk), sinus blockage (sinus barotrauma risk), and general malaise. Influenza adds fever, muscle aches, and significantly greater incapacitation risk. Asthma causes airway restriction (reduced oxygen intake), potential acute attacks triggered by cold air, altitude, or stress, and most medications are acceptable for aviation but must be disclosed. Hay fever causes congestion, sneezing, itchy/watery eyes (visual distraction), and antihistamine medication often causes drowsiness. Management: don't fly with active respiratory infection. For chronic conditions (asthma, hay fever), maintain control with medications approved for aviation. Don't self-medicate with over-the-counter drugs without checking aviation compatibility.
exam_tip: Respiratory infections: barotrauma risk (blocked equalisation), fever increases hypoxia susceptibility, fatigue impairs performance. Simple rule: active respiratory infection = not fit to fly. Chronic conditions: manage with DAME-approved medications.
key_facts: Nasal/sinus congestion blocks pressure equalisation: ear and sinus barotrauma risk
Fever increases metabolic rate and oxygen demand, reducing hypoxia tolerance
Most asthma medications acceptable for aviation; antihistamines often cause drowsiness
Active respiratory infection = not fit to fly. Chronic conditions: manage with approved medication
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: (h) food poisoning and gastroenteritis.
concept: Food poisoning and gastroenteritis cause nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal cramps, and dehydration--all severely incompatible with flying. The physical symptoms are acutely incapacitating: a pilot experiencing sudden severe nausea or vomiting cannot safely operate an aircraft. Dehydration from fluid loss degrades cognitive function, reduces hypoxia tolerance, and causes dizziness. Abdominal distension from gas is worsened by altitude (Boyle's Law). Some foodborne illnesses can cause fever, further impairing performance. In multi-crew operations, if both crew ate the same contaminated meal, the entire flight crew could be incapacitated simultaneously--this is why the 'different meals for crew' rule exists in many airline operations. Management: don't fly with any GI symptoms. If symptoms develop in flight, hand over to the other pilot immediately and prepare to divert. Prevention: eat at reputable establishments, avoid suspicious food, maintain food hygiene, and where possible, crew should eat different meals. After an episode, don't fly until fully recovered and rehydrated--typically at least 24-48 hours after symptoms resolve.
exam_tip: GI illness: acutely incapacitating (nausea, vomiting, dehydration). Don't fly with any symptoms. Crew should eat different meals. Recovery: wait 24-48 hours after symptoms resolve before flying.
key_facts: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, and dehydration are acutely incapacitating in flight
Dehydration degrades cognitive function and hypoxia tolerance
Abdominal gas worsens with altitude (Boyle's Law)
Crew should eat different meals; wait 24-48 hours after symptom resolution before flying
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: (i) neurological factors (including fits/epilepsy, brain injury, fainting, headaches, migraines).
concept: Neurological conditions pose some of the most serious threats to flight safety because of the risk of sudden incapacitation. Epilepsy: a seizure in the cockpit is immediately life-threatening for everyone on board. Epilepsy is generally disqualifying for a pilot licence, though some well-controlled cases may be considered after extended seizure-free periods. Brain injury: depending on severity, can cause cognitive deficits, personality changes, seizure risk, and balance problems. Requires thorough neurological assessment before return to flying. Fainting (syncope): the unpredictable nature of fainting episodes makes it dangerous in the cockpit. Causes must be thoroughly investigated (cardiac, vasovagal, metabolic). Headaches and migraines: while common headaches may not ground you, they impair concentration and comfort. Migraines with aura (visual disturbance, numbness) can mimic stroke symptoms and are acutely incapacitating, with some migraine medications having sedating side effects. Management: full neurological assessment, medication review with DAME, disclosure of all episodes, and strict adherence to medical certificate conditions. Any loss of consciousness must be reported and investigated before flying resumes.
exam_tip: Neurological conditions: highest sudden incapacitation risk. Epilepsy: generally disqualifying. Any loss of consciousness: must be reported and investigated. Migraines with aura: acutely incapacitating.
key_facts: Epilepsy: generally disqualifying due to seizure risk; some exceptions after extended seizure-free periods
Brain injury: requires thorough neurological assessment for cognitive, seizure, and balance effects
Fainting (syncope): unpredictable; causes must be investigated before return to flying
Migraines with aura can mimic stroke and are acutely incapacitating
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations, NZ CARs Part 67

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: (j) emotional factors (including depression and anxiety).
concept: Emotional and psychological factors significantly affect pilot performance but are often overlooked or concealed due to stigma. Depression: causes loss of motivation, impaired concentration, fatigue, sleep disturbance, slowed decision-making, and in severe cases, suicidal ideation. A depressed pilot may go through the motions but lack the engagement and sharpness needed for safe commercial operations. Anxiety: causes hypervigilance or tunnel vision, impaired decision-making, physical symptoms (rapid heart rate, sweating, trembling), and avoidance behaviour. Both conditions may be treated with medication (some antidepressants are now acceptable for aviation with appropriate monitoring), therapy (CBT is effective and compatible with flying), or a combination. The biggest barrier is disclosure: pilots fear losing their medical certificate and career. However, undisclosed depression or anxiety that leads to an incident or impaired performance has far worse consequences. NZ and international aviation are moving toward more supportive approaches, including peer support programmes. Management: seek professional help early, disclose to your DAME, access peer support, and don't try to fly through it.
exam_tip: Depression: impairs concentration, motivation, decision-making. Anxiety: tunnel vision, physical symptoms, avoidance. Some antidepressants now acceptable for aviation. Biggest barrier: disclosure stigma. Seek help early.
key_facts: Depression: loss of motivation, impaired concentration, fatigue, slowed decision-making
Anxiety: hypervigilance/tunnel vision, physical symptoms, impaired decisions
Some antidepressants acceptable for aviation with monitoring; CBT is effective
Disclosure barriers (career fear) are the biggest obstacle; seek help early
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: (k) physical injuries.
concept: Physical injuries affect pilot performance depending on their nature, severity, and location. Upper limb injuries (arm, hand, wrist, shoulder) can impair control manipulation, throttle operation, switch selection, and writing ability. Lower limb injuries (leg, ankle, foot) affect rudder operation, braking, and the ability to evacuate in an emergency. Back and neck injuries restrict head movement (critical for lookout scanning), cause pain-related distraction, and may require pain medication with sedating effects. Head injuries carry the risk of delayed complications (subdural haematoma) and cognitive impairment. Even minor injuries can be distracting enough to degrade performance. Burns, however minor, can be extremely painful and restrictive. Management: don't fly with any injury that restricts your ability to fully operate all controls, perform normal lookout, or evacuate quickly in an emergency. Don't fly while taking pain medication unless cleared by your DAME. After significant injury, obtain medical clearance before returning to flying. A functional flight test may be required to demonstrate you can fully operate the aircraft.
exam_tip: Physical injuries: assess whether you can fully operate controls, perform lookout, and evacuate quickly. Don't fly on unapproved pain medication. Post-injury: medical clearance and possibly functional flight test required.
key_facts: Upper limb injuries: impair control, throttle, and switch operation
Lower limb injuries: affect rudder, braking, and emergency evacuation
Back/neck injuries: restrict scanning, cause distraction, may require sedating medication
Don't fly with any injury limiting full control operation or emergency evacuation capability
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: (l) dehydration.
concept: Dehydration is one of the most common and underestimated threats to pilot performance. The cockpit environment actively promotes dehydration: low humidity in pressurised cabins (typically 10-20%), dry air in unpressurised aircraft at altitude, increased insensible water loss from breathing dry air, and the diuretic effect of caffeine (which many pilots consume heavily). Effects on performance: by the time you feel thirsty, you're already mildly dehydrated and your cognitive function is starting to decline. At 2% body water loss, concentration, decision-making, and reaction time are measurably impaired. At 5%, fatigue, headache, and dizziness become significant. Dehydration also reduces blood volume, increasing susceptibility to hypoxia and G-forces. It exacerbates the effects of altitude, heat, and fatigue. Management: drink water regularly throughout the flight--don't wait until you're thirsty. Carry adequate water supply. Limit caffeine and alcohol consumption (both are diuretics). In hot conditions or on long flights, increase water intake. A good target is 200-250 ml per hour of flight. Ensure you're well hydrated before departing.
exam_tip: Dehydration: 2% body water loss impairs concentration and decision-making. Cockpit environments are dehydrating. Drink 200-250 ml/hour. Don't wait until thirsty. Limit caffeine. Exacerbates hypoxia and fatigue.
key_facts: 2% body water loss measurably impairs concentration, decision-making, and reaction time
Cockpit environment: low humidity (10-20%), dry air, caffeine consumption all dehydrate
Reduces blood volume, increasing susceptibility to hypoxia and G-forces
Drink 200-250 ml water per hour; carry adequate supply; don't wait until thirsty
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: With regard to the following factors, describe their effects on pilot performance and methods by which they may be minimised/managed: (m) hypoglycaemia.
concept: Hypoglycaemia (low blood sugar) directly impairs brain function because the brain depends almost entirely on glucose for fuel. Causes include: skipping meals (extremely common in busy commercial operations), excessive insulin in diabetic pilots, prolonged physical activity without eating, reactive hypoglycaemia after a high-sugar meal, and excessive alcohol consumption (which inhibits glucose production). Symptoms progress from mild (irritability, difficulty concentrating, hunger, trembling) to moderate (confusion, visual disturbance, slurred speech, poor coordination) to severe (loss of consciousness, seizures). In the cockpit, even mild hypoglycaemia degrades the decision-making and concentration needed for safe commercial operations. The onset can be insidious, particularly when workload is high and you're distracted. Management: eat regular, balanced meals with complex carbohydrates (slow-release energy). Don't skip meals due to schedule pressure. Carry snacks in the cockpit--muesli bars, nuts, fruit. If you feel symptoms developing, eat something immediately. Diabetic pilots must maintain strict blood sugar monitoring as per their medical certificate conditions.
exam_tip: Hypoglycaemia impairs brain function directly: irritability, confusion, poor coordination, then unconsciousness. Don't skip meals. Eat complex carbs for sustained energy. Carry cockpit snacks. Diabetic pilots: strict monitoring.
key_facts: Brain depends on glucose; low blood sugar directly impairs cognitive function
Common cause in aviation: skipping meals due to schedule pressure
Symptoms: irritability, difficulty concentrating, trembling > confusion > unconsciousness
Prevention: regular balanced meals, complex carbs, carry cockpit snacks
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of gastrointestinal problems.
concept: Gastrointestinal (GI) problems present with a range of symptoms that can significantly affect pilot performance. Common symptoms include: nausea (a persistent feeling of sickness that distracts and can progress to vomiting), vomiting (acutely incapacitating--requires both hands off controls), diarrhoea (urgency and inability to leave the cockpit), abdominal pain or cramping (from mild distraction to severe incapacitation depending on cause), bloating and distension (worsened by altitude due to gas expansion), heartburn or acid reflux (distraction and discomfort, especially when leaning forward for instruments), excessive flatulence (socially awkward in a confined cockpit but also indicates gas production that worsens at altitude), and changes in bowel habit (may indicate underlying pathology). GI problems can result from: food poisoning, stress, infection, dietary issues, medication side effects, or underlying conditions (IBS, ulcers, gallstones). Any acute GI symptom should be treated seriously--the cockpit is not the place to 'push through' digestive distress. If symptoms develop in flight, consider the need to divert based on severity.
exam_tip: GI symptoms: nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain, bloating (worse at altitude), heartburn. All degrade performance. Acute symptoms: consider diversion. Don't fly with active GI problems.
key_facts: Nausea and vomiting are acutely incapacitating in the cockpit
Abdominal gas/bloating worsens at altitude due to Boyle's Law
Diarrhoea creates urgency incompatible with cockpit duties
Causes: food poisoning, stress, infection, dietary issues, medication, underlying conditions
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Identify the primary causes of food poisoning.
concept: Food poisoning is caused by consuming food contaminated with pathogenic organisms or their toxins. The primary causes include: Salmonella (found in undercooked poultry, eggs, and dairy products; onset 6-72 hours), Campylobacter (most common bacterial cause in NZ, from undercooked chicken and unpasteurised milk; onset 2-5 days), Staphylococcus aureus toxin (from food handled by infected people, dairy products, cream-filled pastries; rapid onset 1-6 hours), E. coli (from undercooked beef, unpasteurised products, contaminated water; onset 1-8 days), Clostridium perfringens (from reheated meat dishes, buffet food kept warm too long; onset 8-16 hours), and Norovirus (highly contagious, from contaminated food/water/surfaces; onset 12-48 hours). Prevention: eat from reputable sources, ensure food is properly cooked and stored, wash hands, avoid buffet food that's been sitting at room temperature, be cautious with street food or unfamiliar establishments. For commercial crew: different meals is a common policy to prevent simultaneous incapacitation.
exam_tip: Main causes in NZ: Campylobacter (most common), Salmonella, Staph aureus (rapid onset), E. coli, Norovirus. Prevention: reputable sources, proper cooking/storage, hand washing. Crew: eat different meals.
key_facts: Campylobacter: most common in NZ, from undercooked chicken and unpasteurised milk
Salmonella: undercooked poultry, eggs, dairy; Staph aureus: rapid onset from handled food
E. coli: undercooked beef, contaminated water; Norovirus: highly contagious
Prevention: reputable sources, proper cooking/storage, hand hygiene, crew eat different meals
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations, NZ MoH data

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of alcohol on pilot performance.
concept: Alcohol impairs virtually every faculty you need for safe flying. Even small amounts cause: degraded judgement and decision-making (you take risks you wouldn't sober), slowed reaction time, reduced coordination and fine motor skills, impaired vision (reduced acuity, narrowed visual field, slower focus adjustment), degraded short-term memory, reduced ability to multitask, and increased susceptibility to hypoxia, spatial disorientation, and motion sickness. At altitude, the effects of alcohol are amplified because the reduced partial pressure of oxygen compounds the impairment--one drink at 8,000 ft cabin altitude has the effect of roughly two drinks at sea level. Alcohol also disrupts sleep quality (even if it helps you fall asleep, the sleep is less restorative), causes dehydration (a diuretic), and produces hangover effects that can persist long after blood alcohol reaches zero. Hangover effects include headache, nausea, fatigue, cognitive impairment, and irritability--all incompatible with safe commercial operations. The metabolisation rate is approximately one standard drink per hour, but this varies by individual, and residual effects persist well beyond the point where blood alcohol returns to zero.
exam_tip: Alcohol impairs judgement, reaction time, coordination, vision, memory, and multitasking. Effects amplified at altitude (1 drink at 8,000 ft ~ 2 at sea level). Hangover effects persist after BAC reaches zero.
key_facts: Impairs judgement, reaction time, coordination, vision, memory, and multitasking
Effects amplified at altitude due to reduced oxygen partial pressure
Approximately one standard drink metabolised per hour; residual effects persist longer
Hangover effects (fatigue, headache, cognitive impairment) persist after BAC reaches zero
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the restriction associated with the consumption of alcohol and flying.
concept: NZ Civil Aviation Rules are clear: you must not act as a crew member while under the influence of alcohol or within 8 hours of consuming alcohol (the '8 hours bottle to throttle' rule). Additionally, your blood alcohol concentration must be zero--not just below the drink-drive limit, but actually zero. The 8-hour rule is a minimum, not a guarantee of fitness: after heavy drinking, you may still have measurable blood alcohol and significant hangover impairment well beyond 8 hours. A general guideline for commercial operations is to allow 24 hours after significant alcohol consumption. Many operators have stricter company policies (e.g., 12 hours or no alcohol within a certain period before any duty day). The rule applies to all crew members, not just pilots. Violating alcohol rules carries severe consequences: prosecution under the CA Act, loss of medical certificate, loss of employment, and potential criminal charges if an incident occurs. For CPL holders, maintaining a professional relationship with alcohol isn't optional--it's a fundamental requirement of the job.
exam_tip: NZ rule: 8 hours minimum bottle to throttle AND blood alcohol must be zero. 8 hours is the minimum--24 hours recommended after heavy consumption. Violation: prosecution, medical loss, career consequences.
key_facts: NZ CARs: 8 hours minimum after consuming alcohol before acting as crew
Blood alcohol concentration must be zero--not just below drink-drive limit
8 hours is the minimum; 24 hours recommended after significant consumption
Violation carries prosecution, medical certificate loss, and career consequences
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, NZ CARs Part 91, Campbell & Bagshaw -- HPL

syllabus_desc: Describe how individuals differ in the effect of alcohol consumption.
concept: Individual variation in alcohol tolerance is significant and depends on multiple factors. Body weight and composition: larger people and those with more lean mass generally have a lower BAC per drink because alcohol distributes in body water (fat contains less water). Gender: women typically have less body water and more body fat than men of equivalent weight, leading to higher BAC per drink. Genetic factors: enzyme systems (alcohol dehydrogenase and aldehyde dehydrogenase) vary genetically, affecting metabolism speed. Some populations have reduced enzyme activity, leading to slower metabolism and more pronounced effects. Food intake: drinking on an empty stomach produces faster, higher BAC peaks. Regular consumption: chronic drinkers develop some metabolic tolerance (their liver processes alcohol faster), but this doesn't protect against impairment--it means they're impaired more often and may not recognise it. Fatigue and illness: reduce tolerance. Medication interactions: many medications potentiate alcohol's effects. The key point for pilots: you cannot assume your impairment level based on what you drank or how you feel. Two pilots drinking the same amount can be affected very differently. The only safe approach is the combination of time (well beyond 8 hours after significant drinking) and zero BAC.
exam_tip: Factors: body weight/composition, gender, genetics, food intake, regular consumption, fatigue, medications. You can't judge impairment by how you feel. Only safe approach: adequate time + zero BAC.
key_facts: Body weight, composition, and gender affect BAC per drink (women typically higher BAC)
Genetic enzyme variation affects metabolism speed
Empty stomach produces faster, higher BAC peaks; medications can potentiate effects
Cannot judge impairment by subjective feeling; only safe approach: adequate time + zero BAC
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of drugs on pilot performance.
concept: Drugs--whether prescription, over-the-counter, or illicit--can all affect pilot performance. The effects depend on the drug class. Sedatives and hypnotics (sleeping pills, benzodiazepines): cause drowsiness, slowed reactions, impaired judgement--effects can persist well into the next day. Antihistamines (allergy medications): older types (first-generation like diphenhydramine) cause significant sedation; newer types (second-generation like loratadine) are less sedating but can still impair. Decongestants: can cause stimulation, anxiety, elevated heart rate. Painkillers: codeine and stronger opioids cause drowsiness and impaired cognition; even paracetamol indicates an underlying condition worth considering. Antidepressants: some are now acceptable for aviation (certain SSRIs) after a stabilisation period; others are not. Stimulants: can cause overconfidence, risk-taking, anxiety, and crash effects. Even 'harmless' supplements and herbal remedies can have aviation-relevant effects. The fundamental rule: never take any medication or drug before flying without confirming its aviation compatibility with your DAME. The drug itself may be safe, but its side effects or the condition it's treating may be incompatible with flying.
exam_tip: All drug categories can affect flying. Check EVERY medication with DAME before flying. Consider both the drug's effects AND the condition being treated. Even OTC drugs can be disqualifying.
key_facts: Sedatives/hypnotics: drowsiness persisting well into next day
Antihistamines: first-generation cause significant sedation; second-generation less so
Painkillers: opioids impair cognition; even mild analgesics indicate conditions worth assessing
Never take any medication before flying without DAME confirmation of aviation compatibility
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe considerations associated with the taking of over the counter medication and flying.
concept: Over-the-counter (OTC) medications are easily available and often perceived as harmless, but many have significant aviation implications. Antihistamines (for allergies, cold symptoms, motion sickness): first-generation types cause drowsiness, impaired concentration, and slowed reactions for 4-12 hours. Even second-generation 'non-drowsy' types affect some individuals. Cold and flu medications: often contain multiple active ingredients including decongestants (stimulant effects), antihistamines (sedation), and sometimes codeine (opioid effects). Pain relief: ibuprofen and paracetamol are generally acceptable for aviation, but the condition causing the pain may not be. Codeine-containing preparations cause drowsiness. Antacids and anti-diarrhoeal medications: generally acceptable, but the GI condition may make you unfit. Cough suppressants: many contain codeine or dextromethorphan (drowsiness). Sleep aids: diphenhydramine and similar are disqualifying for 24+ hours. The trap is assuming 'over the counter' means 'safe to fly with.' The medication may be fine, but: does the condition it's treating make you unfit? Are you treating symptoms that would otherwise tell you to ground yourself? Always read the label, check with your DAME if unsure, and never fly if the packaging warns about drowsiness or impaired alertness.
exam_tip: OTC doesn't mean aviation-safe. Antihistamines, cold remedies (multi-ingredient), codeine preparations, and sleep aids are all problematic. Consider BOTH the drug effects AND the underlying condition. When in doubt, call your DAME.
key_facts: First-generation antihistamines cause drowsiness for 4-12 hours
Multi-ingredient cold/flu remedies may contain sedating and stimulating components
Codeine in any form (painkillers, cough syrups) causes drowsiness
Always consider: is the underlying condition also making you unfit to fly?
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain why illegal/recreational drugs are unacceptable for pilots.
concept: Illegal and recreational drugs are absolutely incompatible with aviation for multiple reasons. Pharmacological: cannabis impairs reaction time, judgement, memory, and concentration, with effects lasting 24+ hours and metabolites detectable for weeks. Cocaine causes overconfidence, risk-taking, elevated heart rate, and unpredictable crash effects. MDMA causes altered perception, dehydration, and cognitive impairment. Methamphetamine causes extreme stimulation, paranoia, and severe crash fatigue. Even 'mild' substances like cannabis have been shown to degrade complex task performance for longer than users realise. Legal: use of illegal drugs is a criminal offence. If detected through testing (which operators and the CAA can require), you face prosecution, immediate loss of medical certificate, and career termination. There is no acceptable recreational use for a commercial pilot--even on days off, the residual effects of many drugs persist well beyond the period of perceived impairment. Professional: the aviation industry demands reliability and trustworthiness. Drug use demonstrates a willingness to take risks with safety that is fundamentally incompatible with holding a commercial pilot licence. The consequences are absolute: if you use recreational drugs, you cannot safely hold a CPL.
exam_tip: All recreational drugs incompatible with aviation. Cannabis: impairment 24+ hours. Cocaine: overconfidence, crash effects. Detection = prosecution, medical loss, career termination. No acceptable recreational use for CPL holders.
key_facts: Cannabis: impairs reaction time, judgement, memory for 24+ hours; detectable for weeks
Cocaine, MDMA, methamphetamine: severe cognitive and behavioural impairment
Detection through drug testing: prosecution, immediate medical certificate loss, career end
No acceptable recreational drug use for commercial pilots, including on days off
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, NZ Misuse of Drugs Act, Campbell & Bagshaw -- HPL

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect on the body of donating blood.
concept: Donating blood removes approximately 470 ml of whole blood from your circulation, which reduces your total blood volume by about 8-10%. This directly reduces your body's oxygen-carrying capacity because you've lost red blood cells (and their haemoglobin). The immediate effects include: reduced exercise tolerance, increased heart rate to compensate for lower blood volume, mild lightheadedness or dizziness (particularly when standing), and reduced tolerance to hypoxia and G-forces. Your body begins replacing the lost plasma volume within hours (mostly complete within 24 hours), but replacing the lost red blood cells takes significantly longer--typically 4-8 weeks for full haemoglobin recovery. During this recovery period, you're effectively anaemic to some degree, which means reduced oxygen delivery to tissues, including the brain. At altitude, this has the same effect as starting at a higher physiological altitude--similar to the effect of smoking. For a pilot, the practical impact is: you're more susceptible to hypoxia, fatigue more easily, and have reduced tolerance to G-forces until your red blood cell count fully recovers.
exam_tip: Donating 470 ml blood reduces oxygen-carrying capacity by ~8-10%. Plasma replaced in hours; red blood cells take 4-8 weeks. During recovery: increased hypoxia susceptibility, reduced G tolerance, increased fatigue.
key_facts: Standard donation: ~470 ml, reducing blood volume by 8-10%
Plasma volume replaced within ~24 hours; red blood cells take 4-8 weeks
Reduced oxygen-carrying capacity increases susceptibility to hypoxia and fatigue
Effectively anaemic during recovery period, reducing G tolerance and altitude tolerance
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: State the recommended time period between donating blood and flying.
concept: The recommended minimum period between donating blood and flying as a pilot is at least 24 hours for a standard whole blood donation, though many aviation authorities and operators recommend a longer period. Some authorities specify 48-72 hours to allow for full plasma volume recovery and assessment of any adverse effects from the donation (fainting, bruising, infection at the donation site). For commercial operations, a conservative 48-72 hour stand-down is appropriate. After donating plasma only (plasmapheresis), the recovery is faster because red blood cells are returned--12-24 hours is typically sufficient. It's important to note that even after the stand-down period, your red blood cell count won't have fully recovered for weeks, so you'll still be operating with slightly reduced oxygen-carrying capacity. You should not donate blood immediately before known high-altitude operations or demanding flights. If you feel unwell, dizzy, or fatigued after donating, extend the stand-down period. Stay well hydrated and eat iron-rich foods to support recovery.
exam_tip: Minimum stand-down after whole blood donation: 24 hours (48-72 hours recommended for commercial ops). Plasmapheresis: 12-24 hours. Red blood cells not fully recovered for 4-8 weeks. Stay hydrated, eat iron-rich foods.
key_facts: Minimum 24 hours after whole blood donation; 48-72 hours recommended for commercial operations
After plasmapheresis: 12-24 hours (red cells returned, only plasma to replace)
Red blood cell recovery takes 4-8 weeks; slightly reduced O2 capacity persists
Stay hydrated, eat iron-rich foods, extend stand-down if feeling unwell
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: (a) carbon monoxide.
concept: Carbon monoxide (CO) is a colourless, odourless gas that's the most insidious environmental hazard in general aviation. It enters the cockpit from exhaust leaks in piston-engine aircraft, particularly through faulty exhaust-heated cabin heating systems. CO binds to haemoglobin 200-250 times more readily than oxygen, forming carboxyhaemoglobin, which cannot carry oxygen. Symptoms progress from: headache, dizziness, and nausea (mild exposure), to confusion, impaired judgement, blurred vision, and muscle weakness (moderate), to unconsciousness and death (severe). The classic cherry-red skin colour only appears in fatal cases. Treatment: immediately shut off the cabin heater, open all vents and windows to maximise fresh air, use 100% supplemental oxygen if available, descend and land as soon as possible, and seek medical attention. CO exposure is cumulative during a flight and the half-life of carboxyhaemoglobin is 4-5 hours breathing room air (1 hour with 100% O2). CO detectors in the cockpit are essential for early warning.
exam_tip: CO: colourless, odourless, binds haemoglobin 200-250x more than O2. Symptoms: headache, dizziness, nausea, confusion. Treatment: heater off, fresh air, 100% O2, land ASAP. CO detectors essential.
key_facts: Colourless, odourless; enters cockpit via exhaust leaks in cabin heating systems
Binds haemoglobin 200-250x more readily than oxygen, forming carboxyhaemoglobin
Symptoms: headache, dizziness, nausea, confusion, blurred vision, unconsciousness
Treatment: heater off, open vents/windows, 100% O2, descend and land ASAP
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: (b) fuel.
concept: Aviation fuel (avgas or Jet-A1) presents both inhalation and skin contact hazards. Inhalation of fuel vapours causes headache, dizziness, nausea, irritation of the respiratory tract, confusion, and at high concentrations, narcosis and loss of consciousness. Avgas contains tetraethyl lead (TEL), which is additionally toxic and can cause neurological damage with chronic exposure. Skin contact causes defatting (removing natural oils from the skin), leading to dermatitis, cracking, and increased susceptibility to infection. Ingestion is rare but dangerous--causes GI irritation, aspiration pneumonia if vomited and inhaled, and systemic toxicity. During refuelling, pilots (particularly in smaller operations) may be exposed to fuel vapours, especially in still air conditions. Immediate treatment for inhalation: move to fresh air, administer oxygen if available, seek medical attention if symptoms persist. For skin contact: wash thoroughly with soap and water. For ingestion: do not induce vomiting (aspiration risk), seek immediate medical attention. Prevention: avoid prolonged exposure to fuel vapours, refuel in ventilated areas, wear gloves during refuelling, don't sniff fuel to check type.
exam_tip: Fuel vapour: headache, dizziness, nausea, narcosis. Avgas contains TEL (neurotoxic). Skin: defatting/dermatitis. Treatment: fresh air, O2, wash skin. Don't induce vomiting if ingested. Wear gloves refuelling.
key_facts: Inhalation: headache, dizziness, nausea, confusion, narcosis at high concentrations
Avgas contains tetraethyl lead (TEL) -- chronic neurological toxicity risk
Skin contact: defatting, dermatitis; ingestion: aspiration pneumonia risk (don't vomit)
Prevention: ventilated refuelling, wear gloves, avoid prolonged vapour exposure
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: (c) chemical sprays.
concept: Chemical sprays relevant to aviation include agricultural chemicals (pesticides, herbicides, fungicides) encountered during agricultural aviation operations, and de-icing fluids used at airports. Agricultural chemicals vary enormously in toxicity--organophosphates are the most dangerous, inhibiting acetylcholinesterase and causing SLUDGE symptoms (Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis), plus muscle weakness, respiratory failure, and death. Pyrethroids and newer chemicals are generally less acutely toxic but still cause respiratory irritation, skin reactions, and eye irritation. Symptoms of exposure: respiratory irritation, coughing, eye watering, headache, nausea, skin irritation or burns. Severe organophosphate exposure: pinpoint pupils, excessive salivation, muscle twitching, difficulty breathing, confusion. Treatment: remove from exposure (fresh air), remove contaminated clothing, wash affected skin with copious water, administer oxygen, and seek immediate medical attention. For organophosphate poisoning, atropine is the specific antidote. Prevention: proper PPE during agricultural operations, functional cockpit ventilation systems, awareness of spray drift.
exam_tip: Agricultural chemicals vary in toxicity. Organophosphates most dangerous: SLUDGE symptoms (Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis). Treatment: remove from exposure, decontaminate, O2, medical help. Atropine for organophosphate.
key_facts: Agricultural chemicals range from low to extreme toxicity; organophosphates most dangerous
Organophosphate exposure: SLUDGE symptoms, pinpoint pupils, muscle twitching, respiratory failure
Treatment: remove from exposure, decontaminate, oxygen, medical attention, atropine for OP
Prevention: PPE, functional cockpit ventilation, awareness of spray drift
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: (d) lubricating oils.
concept: Lubricating oils in aircraft contain base oils and additives that can be hazardous through skin contact, ingestion, or inhalation of heated oil mist. Engine oil leaks in the cockpit or cabin are concerning because heated oil produces fumes that can cause respiratory irritation, headache, nausea, and eye irritation. Some synthetic oils contain tricresyl phosphate (TCP), an organophosphate additive that at very high exposures can cause neurological effects. Skin contact with used engine oil over long periods is associated with dermatitis and, with chronic exposure, an increased cancer risk from polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. Symptoms of acute fume exposure: characteristic oily smell in cockpit, eye and respiratory tract irritation, headache, nausea, coughing. Treatment: identify and shut off the source if possible (oil leak from engine into cabin heating system), increase ventilation, use supplemental oxygen if available, land as soon as practicable. For skin contact: wash thoroughly with soap and water. For chronic exposure concerns: use barrier creams and gloves during maintenance tasks, avoid prolonged skin contact with used oils.
exam_tip: Oil fume exposure: characteristic smell, eye/respiratory irritation, headache, nausea. Some synthetic oils contain TCP (organophosphate). Treatment: ventilate, O2, land ASAP. Chronic skin contact: cancer risk from PAHs.
key_facts: Heated oil fumes cause respiratory irritation, headache, nausea, eye irritation
Some synthetic oils contain TCP (tricresyl phosphate) with neurological toxicity potential
Chronic skin contact with used oil: dermatitis and increased cancer risk from PAHs
Treatment: ventilate cockpit, shut off fume source, O2, land as soon as practicable
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: (e) hydraulic fluids.
concept: Hydraulic fluids used in aircraft systems vary in composition--some are petroleum-based (Mil-H-5606), some are synthetic (Skydrol/phosphate ester-based), and some are mineral-based. Phosphate ester-based fluids (Skydrol) are the most commonly encountered irritant: they cause severe skin and eye irritation on contact, with burning sensation, redness, and potential chemical burns. Inhalation of heated hydraulic fluid mist causes respiratory irritation and can trigger asthma-like symptoms. Ingestion causes GI irritation. Petroleum-based fluids have effects similar to other petroleum products (skin defatting, vapour inhalation effects). The main concern for pilots is hydraulic fluid leaks entering the cockpit, either as liquid drips or as heated mist from a pressurised system breach. Symptoms: strong chemical smell, eye irritation and watering, skin burning if contacted, coughing, difficulty breathing if mist is inhaled. Treatment: move away from the leak, ventilate the cockpit, flush affected skin and eyes with copious water immediately (Skydrol burns worsen over time if not washed off promptly), use supplemental oxygen if breathing is affected, and land as soon as practicable.
exam_tip: Skydrol (phosphate ester): severe skin/eye irritant, causes chemical burns. Flush immediately with water -- burns worsen if left. Treatment: ventilate, flush affected areas, O2, land ASAP.
key_facts: Skydrol (phosphate ester) fluids cause severe skin and eye irritation/chemical burns
Heated hydraulic mist causes respiratory irritation and asthma-like symptoms
Flush skin and eyes immediately with copious water -- Skydrol burns worsen over time
Treatment: ventilate cockpit, decontaminate, oxygen if needed, land as soon as practicable
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: (f) compressed gases.
concept: Compressed gases in aviation include oxygen (for supplemental breathing systems), nitrogen (for tyre inflation and some pneumatic systems), CO2 (in some fire extinguishers), and halon/replacement agents (in fire suppression systems). Hazards include: rapid release of pressure (causing physical injury from the blast or from flying components), frostbite from rapidly expanding gas (which cools dramatically--the Joule-Thomson effect), oxygen enrichment creating fire/explosion risk, displacement of breathable air in confined spaces (nitrogen, CO2, halon), and specific toxic effects of certain agents (halon replacements can be toxic in enclosed spaces). A ruptured high-pressure line can cause severe injury from the gas stream itself. Symptoms of oxygen-displacing gas exposure: rapid onset of breathlessness, confusion, unconsciousness in an enclosed space. Symptoms of frostbite from gas expansion: localised intense cold, white/numb skin, pain. Treatment: remove from exposure, fresh air, treat frostbite (warm gradually, don't rub), treat injuries, seek medical attention. Prevention: proper maintenance and inspection of pressurised systems, awareness of hazards during ground handling.
exam_tip: Compressed gas hazards: blast injury, frostbite from expansion, O2 enrichment (fire risk), air displacement (asphyxiation), toxicity. Treatment: remove from exposure, fresh air, treat frostbite/injuries, medical attention.
key_facts: Rapid release causes blast injury and frostbite from gas expansion cooling
Oxygen enrichment creates extreme fire/explosion risk
Nitrogen, CO2, halon displace breathable air in confined spaces, causing asphyxiation
Prevention: proper maintenance of pressurised systems; awareness during ground handling
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: (g) liquid oxygen.
concept: Liquid oxygen (LOX) is stored at extremely low temperatures (approximately -183 degrees C) and presents unique hazards. Cryogenic burns: contact with liquid oxygen or cold equipment causes immediate severe frostbite and tissue damage similar to severe burns. Oxygen enrichment: spilled LOX rapidly evaporates, creating an oxygen-enriched atmosphere that dramatically increases fire risk--materials that normally don't burn readily (clothing, lubricants, grease) can ignite violently or even spontaneously in oxygen-enriched environments. LOX reacts violently with hydrocarbon-based substances (oil, grease, fuel). A small spark in an oxygen-enriched environment can cause explosive combustion. Symptoms of LOX contact: immediate intense cold/burning sensation, white hard skin (frostbite), blistering. Treatment for cryogenic burns: remove from contact, warm affected area gradually with body heat or lukewarm water (never hot water), do not rub, cover with sterile dressing, seek medical attention. For oxygen enrichment: evacuate the area, eliminate ignition sources, ventilate. Prevention: proper handling training, no smoking near LOX systems, keep hydrocarbons away, use approved equipment.
exam_tip: LOX: -183 degrees C. Cryogenic burns on contact. Creates extreme fire risk in oxygen-enriched atmosphere -- hydrocarbons ignite violently. Treatment: warm gradually, don't rub, sterile dressing. No hydrocarbons near LOX.
key_facts: Stored at -183 degrees C; contact causes immediate severe cryogenic burns
Evaporating LOX creates oxygen-enriched atmosphere with extreme fire/explosion risk
Hydrocarbons (oil, grease, fuel) react violently with LOX -- spontaneous ignition
Treatment: warm gradually (not hot water), don't rub, sterile dressing, medical attention
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: (h) de-icing fluids.
concept: De-icing and anti-icing fluids used in aircraft ground operations are typically glycol-based (ethylene glycol or propylene glycol). They're applied to remove ice from aircraft surfaces before takeoff and to prevent ice formation during taxi. Hazards: glycol-based fluids are skin and eye irritants, and ethylene glycol is toxic if ingested (it metabolises to oxalic acid, causing kidney failure). Propylene glycol is less toxic but still irritating. During application, aerosol mist can be inhaled, causing respiratory irritation. Hot de-icing fluid produces more vapour. Skin contact with concentrated fluid causes drying and irritation. Eye contact causes stinging and watering. If de-icing fluid enters the cockpit through open vents or doors, it can create a slippery floor and unpleasant fumes. Symptoms: eye and skin irritation, respiratory irritation from vapour/mist, nausea if significant inhalation. Treatment: flush eyes with water for 15 minutes, wash skin with soap and water, move to fresh air for inhalation exposure, seek medical attention if symptoms persist or if ingestion is suspected. Prevention: close cockpit vents and windows during de-icing, wear eye protection if involved in application.
exam_tip: De-icing fluids: glycol-based, skin/eye irritant. Ethylene glycol toxic if ingested (kidney failure). Treatment: flush eyes 15 min, wash skin, fresh air. Close cockpit vents during de-icing operations.
key_facts: Glycol-based (ethylene or propylene glycol); skin and eye irritants
Ethylene glycol toxic if ingested -- metabolises to oxalic acid, causes kidney failure
Aerosol mist during application causes respiratory irritation
Prevention: close cockpit vents during de-icing; flush eyes/skin immediately if contacted
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: (i) fire extinguishing agents.
concept: Fire extinguishing agents in aircraft include halon 1211 (BCF/bromochlorodifluoromethane) and halon 1301 in older systems, and newer replacement agents (HFC-227ea/FM-200, Novec 1230). Halon-based agents: effective fire suppressants but decompose in fire to produce toxic by-products (hydrogen fluoride, hydrogen bromide) that cause severe respiratory tract irritation and damage. Even without fire decomposition, halon displaces air oxygen in enclosed spaces, creating asphyxiation risk. The newer replacement agents are generally less toxic but still displace oxygen. CO2 extinguishers: effective on electrical and liquid fires but displace oxygen rapidly in confined spaces, can cause frostbite from the discharge, and high concentrations cause rapid unconsciousness. Dry chemical extinguishers: the powder is irritating to eyes and respiratory tract and creates a visibility-reducing cloud in the cockpit. Symptoms of exposure: coughing, eye irritation, breathing difficulty, dizziness from oxygen displacement, frostbite from CO2. Treatment: ventilate the cockpit immediately after fire extinguisher discharge, use supplemental oxygen if available, land as soon as practicable, seek medical assessment for significant exposure.
exam_tip: Halon decomposes in fire to toxic gases (HF, HBr). All agents displace oxygen in enclosed spaces. CO2: frostbite and rapid asphyxiation risk. After discharge: ventilate immediately, use O2, land ASAP.
key_facts: Halon decomposes in fire to toxic hydrogen fluoride and hydrogen bromide
All extinguishing agents displace oxygen in enclosed spaces -- asphyxiation risk
CO2 extinguishers: frostbite from discharge and rapid oxygen displacement
After discharge in cockpit: ventilate immediately, supplemental O2, land ASAP
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: (j) fire accelerant substances.
concept: Fire accelerant substances in the aviation environment include aviation fuel (both avgas and Jet-A1), hydraulic fluids, lubricating oils, and cleaning solvents. These are all flammable or combustible and can cause or worsen fires. The hazard is twofold: the fire itself and the toxic products of combustion. Burning aviation fuel produces intense heat, carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, and dense black smoke. Burning synthetic materials (cabin furnishings, wiring insulation, composites) produce even more toxic fumes including hydrogen cyanide, acrolein, and other irritant gases. Symptoms of fire and smoke exposure: choking, coughing, eye and respiratory tract irritation, carbon monoxide poisoning, confusion, loss of consciousness. Burns cause pain, blistering, and tissue damage. Treatment: extinguish the fire using appropriate extinguisher, ventilate to clear smoke, apply oxygen, treat burns (cool with water, cover with sterile dressings, don't remove adhered clothing), evacuate if fire cannot be controlled. Prevention: proper storage and handling of flammable substances, fire detection and suppression systems maintained, crew trained in fire-fighting procedures.
exam_tip: Fire hazard: fuel, hydraulic fluid, oils, solvents. Combustion products are highly toxic (CO, HCN). Burns + smoke inhalation = the combined threat. Treatment: extinguish, ventilate, O2, treat burns, evacuate if uncontrolled.
key_facts: Aviation fuels, hydraulic fluids, oils, and solvents are all flammable/combustible
Combustion products include CO, CO2, hydrogen cyanide, and other toxic gases
Smoke inhalation is often more dangerous than the fire itself
Treatment: extinguish, ventilate, oxygen, treat burns with cool water, evacuate if uncontrolled
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: State the source of carbon monoxide poisoning in general aviation aircraft.
concept: In general aviation aircraft, the primary source of carbon monoxide is exhaust gas leaking into the cockpit, most commonly through the cabin heating system. Most piston GA aircraft use exhaust-heated air for cabin heat: fresh air is ducted over a heat exchanger (muff) wrapped around the exhaust system, and the heated air is then directed into the cabin. If the exhaust system develops a crack, hole, or loose connection, CO-rich exhaust gas can leak into the heating duct and enter the cabin mixed with the heated air. Other sources include: exhaust gas entering through gaps in the firewall, exhaust leaks being blown back into the cockpit through open windows or vents (particularly during low-airspeed ground operations), and CO from other aircraft during ground operations (taxiing behind another aircraft). The risk is highest when: the cabin heater is in use, the exhaust system is old or poorly maintained, there are firewall seal deficiencies, and during low-speed or ground operations where exhaust gas doesn't clear away quickly.
exam_tip: Primary source: exhaust gas leaking through the cabin heating system (exhaust muff heater). Also: firewall gaps, exhaust blown through vents at low speed, ground operations. Risk highest with heater ON in old/poorly maintained aircraft.
key_facts: Primary source: exhaust leaks into cabin heating system through cracked heat exchanger
Cabin heater ducts air over exhaust muff -- cracks allow CO into heated air stream
Also from firewall gaps and exhaust blown through vents during low-speed operations
Risk highest with heater in use on aircraft with ageing or poorly maintained exhaust systems
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe reliable methods for the detection of carbon monoxide.
concept: Since CO is colourless and odourless, you cannot rely on your senses to detect it. Reliable detection methods include: chemical spot detectors--small adhesive cards placed in the cockpit that contain a chemical (typically palladium chloride or similar) that changes colour in the presence of CO (these are cheap, widely available, and should be standard in every piston GA cockpit); electronic CO detectors--battery-powered devices that provide a digital readout of CO concentration and alarm at dangerous levels (more accurate and responsive than spot detectors, and increasingly affordable); and panel-mounted CO detection systems in some newer aircraft. Symptoms can also serve as secondary indicators--if you develop an unexplained headache while the cabin heater is running, treat it as potential CO exposure until proven otherwise. The smell of exhaust fumes in the cockpit is an indirect indicator (while CO itself is odourless, other exhaust components do have a smell). However, relying on smell alone is dangerous because low-level CO poisoning can occur without detectable exhaust odour. For NZ CPL pilots flying piston aircraft, carrying an electronic CO detector is a sensible investment.
exam_tip: CO is colourless and odourless -- can't detect by senses. Methods: chemical spot detectors (colour-change cards), electronic CO detectors (digital readout + alarm). Unexplained headache with heater ON = suspect CO.
key_facts: Chemical spot detectors: adhesive cards that change colour in presence of CO (cheap, simple)
Electronic CO detectors: digital readout with alarm at dangerous levels (more accurate)
Exhaust smell is indirect indicator but CO itself is odourless -- don't rely on smell alone
Unexplained headache with cabin heater running should be treated as potential CO exposure
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe methods of eliminating carbon monoxide from the cockpit.
concept: If CO contamination is detected or suspected, immediate action is required. First: turn off the cabin heater completely--this is the most common entry point. Second: open all cockpit vents, windows, and any fresh air sources to maximum--you need to flush the contaminated air out with fresh air. Third: if supplemental oxygen is available, use it at 100%--this both provides clean breathing air and accelerates the removal of CO from your blood (CO half-life drops from 4-5 hours on room air to about 1 hour on 100% O2). Fourth: increase airspeed if safe to do so, which improves ventilation through the cockpit. Fifth: plan to land as soon as practicable--even if you've eliminated the source, you may already have significant carboxyhaemoglobin levels that are impairing your performance without you realising it. Long-term elimination requires maintenance action: inspection and repair of the exhaust system, heat exchanger, firewall seals, and cabin ducting. A post-incident CO test should be conducted before returning the aircraft to service.
exam_tip: Actions in order: heater OFF, open all vents/windows, 100% O2 if available, increase airspeed for ventilation, land ASAP. Post-landing: maintenance inspection of exhaust system and heat exchanger required.
key_facts: Turn off cabin heater immediately (most common CO entry point)
Open all vents and windows to maximum; increase airspeed for better ventilation
Use 100% supplemental O2 (reduces CO half-life from 4-5 hours to ~1 hour)
Land as soon as practicable; maintenance inspection required before next flight
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Identify the negative effects of vibration and resonance.
concept: Aircraft vibration affects pilot performance through multiple pathways. Low-frequency vibration (1-20 Hz) causes the most physiological problems: at 4-8 Hz, resonance with the thoracic and abdominal cavities causes discomfort, breathing difficulty, and visual blurring (the eyes vibrate in their sockets, making instrument reading difficult). At 1-2 Hz, motion sickness is triggered. Higher frequency vibration (20-1000 Hz) causes numbness and tingling in the hands and arms from prolonged contact with vibrating controls. Chronic exposure to hand-arm vibration can lead to vibration white finger (Raynaud's phenomenon) with reduced blood circulation in the fingers. Whole-body vibration causes fatigue, back pain, and chronic musculoskeletal problems. Resonance occurs when the vibration frequency matches the natural frequency of a body part or system, amplifying the effect dramatically. In the cockpit, vibration makes instrument reading harder, increases fatigue, degrades fine motor skills, and contributes to long-term health issues for career pilots. Helicopter pilots are particularly affected due to the constant vibration from the rotor system.
exam_tip: Low-frequency (4-8 Hz) causes visual blurring and breathing difficulty from thoracic resonance. 1-2 Hz triggers motion sickness. Hand-arm vibration: numbness, vibration white finger. Helicopter pilots most affected.
key_facts: 4-8 Hz resonance with thorax/abdomen: visual blurring, breathing difficulty, discomfort
1-2 Hz vibration triggers motion sickness
Chronic hand-arm vibration: numbness, tingling, vibration white finger (Raynaud's)
Whole-body vibration causes fatigue, back pain, and chronic musculoskeletal problems
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Outline ways that vibration can be controlled.
concept: Controlling vibration in aviation involves addressing it at the source, in the transmission path, and at the receiver (the pilot). At the source: proper engine balancing and maintenance, propeller balancing, rotor blade tracking and balancing (helicopters), replacement of worn engine mounts and vibration dampers. In the transmission path: anti-vibration mounts between the engine and airframe, vibration-absorbing materials in the cockpit structure, proper seat design with vibration-damping cushions and suspension. At the receiver: limiting exposure duration (take breaks during long flights), using anti-vibration gloves for hand-arm vibration, ensuring proper seating posture (a well-supported back reduces spinal loading from vibration), and using noise-cancelling headsets that also reduce the auditory component of vibration. For helicopter operations, active vibration control systems (counterweights, active dampers) significantly reduce cabin vibration. Regular maintenance is the most effective single measure--vibration often indicates something loose, worn, or out of balance, which means it's also a maintenance indicator. Report unusual or increased vibration promptly.
exam_tip: Control at source (engine/prop balance, maintenance), path (anti-vibration mounts, seat damping), and receiver (exposure limits, posture, anti-vibration gloves). Unusual vibration = maintenance indicator -- report it.
key_facts: Source: engine/prop balancing, rotor tracking, replace worn mounts and dampers
Transmission path: anti-vibration mounts, vibration-absorbing materials, damped seats
Receiver: limit exposure duration, proper posture, anti-vibration gloves
Unusual or increased vibration is a maintenance indicator -- report promptly
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe a simple model of stress.
concept: A useful model of stress is the demand-capacity model: stress occurs when the demands placed on you (physical, mental, emotional) exceed your perceived capacity to cope. Demands include: cockpit workload, time pressure, environmental factors (heat, noise, turbulence), organisational pressures (schedules, commercial expectations), and personal life stressors. Your capacity to cope depends on: skill and training, physical fitness, rest and sleep quality, personal resilience, coping strategies, and support systems. Stress isn't inherently bad--moderate stress (arousal) actually improves performance, keeping you alert and focused. This follows the Yerkes-Dodson law (inverted-U model): performance increases with arousal up to an optimal point, then degrades rapidly as stress exceeds your coping capacity. Below the optimal point, you're under-aroused (bored, complacent). Above it, you're overloaded (anxious, panicked, performance collapsing). The model explains why the same situation (say, an engine failure) can be managed competently by one pilot (demands within capacity) and overwhelm another (demands exceed capacity) on different days.
exam_tip: Demand-capacity model: stress = demands exceeding coping capacity. Yerkes-Dodson law: inverted-U relationship between arousal and performance. Moderate stress improves performance; excessive stress degrades it.
key_facts: Stress occurs when demands exceed perceived capacity to cope
Yerkes-Dodson law: inverted-U curve -- optimal performance at moderate arousal
Below optimum: under-arousal (boredom, complacency); above: overload (anxiety, panic)
Same situation can be stressful or manageable depending on individual capacity that day
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between stress and arousal.
concept: Stress and arousal are closely linked through the autonomic nervous system. When you encounter a stressor, your sympathetic nervous system activates the 'fight or flight' response: adrenaline and cortisol are released, heart rate increases, blood pressure rises, breathing quickens, muscles tense, and your brain becomes more alert. At low to moderate levels, this arousal enhances performance--you're sharper, quicker, more focused. This is why some pressure before a check ride can actually help you perform better. However, as arousal continues to increase beyond the optimal point, the effects become counterproductive: attention narrows (tunnel vision, both literal and cognitive), fine motor skills degrade, decision-making becomes more rigid (reverting to habit rather than adaptive thinking), short-term memory suffers, and communication becomes less effective. At extreme levels, arousal leads to panic, freezing, or task shedding. The relationship is not fixed--your personal optimal arousal level varies with experience, training, fatigue, and other factors. A well-rested, proficient pilot handles a given stressor at lower arousal than a fatigued, rusty one.
exam_tip: Moderate arousal enhances performance (sharper, quicker). Excessive arousal: attention narrows, motor skills degrade, decision-making becomes rigid, memory suffers. Optimal point varies by individual, day, and conditions.
key_facts: Sympathetic nervous system drives arousal: adrenaline, cortisol, increased HR and BP
Low-moderate arousal enhances performance (focus, alertness, reaction speed)
Excessive arousal: tunnel vision, degraded motor skills, rigid decision-making, memory loss
Optimal arousal level varies with experience, training, fatigue, and other factors
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Identify and give examples of physical, environmental, task-related, organisational and psychological stressors.
concept: Stressors in aviation fall into five broad categories. Physical: fatigue, hunger, dehydration, pain, illness, extreme temperatures, vibration, noise, hypoxia. Environmental: turbulence, icing conditions, poor visibility, mountainous terrain, night operations, weather deterioration, high-density traffic areas (like the Ardmore training area on a busy Saturday). Task-related: high workload during critical phases, unfamiliar procedures, equipment malfunctions, complex ATC instructions, time pressure, novel situations with no standard procedure. Organisational: commercial pressure to complete flights, inadequate rostering, poor maintenance standards, unclear SOPs, unsupportive management, job insecurity, inadequate training resources. Psychological: personal relationship problems, financial stress, bereavement, fear of failure, bullying or harassment, career uncertainty, performance anxiety. The reality is that these stressors rarely occur in isolation--a fatigued pilot (physical) dealing with deteriorating weather (environmental) while under schedule pressure (organisational) and worried about a relationship (psychological) is carrying a combined stress load that far exceeds any single stressor.
exam_tip: Five categories: physical, environmental, task-related, organisational, psychological. They compound--combined stress load is greater than sum of parts. Give a concrete aviation example for each category.
key_facts: Physical: fatigue, hunger, dehydration, illness, noise, vibration, hypoxia
Environmental: turbulence, poor visibility, icing, terrain, weather deterioration
Task-related: high workload, malfunctions, time pressure, unfamiliar procedures
Organisational: commercial pressure, poor rostering, job insecurity; Psychological: personal problems, anxiety
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe how the following environmental stressors affect pilot performance: (a) heat.
concept: Heat stress occurs when the cockpit temperature exceeds comfortable levels, which is common in GA aircraft parked in direct sun, during ground operations with limited airflow, or in aircraft with inadequate ventilation. Heat causes: increased heart rate and reduced cardiovascular efficiency, increased dehydration through sweating, reduced concentration and cognitive performance, increased irritability and reduced decision-making quality, and physical discomfort that distracts from flying tasks. At body temperatures above 38 degrees C, cognitive performance drops measurably. At 40+ degrees C, heat exhaustion and potentially heatstroke become risks. In NZ, heat stress is less common than in tropical or desert environments, but it occurs during summer operations--an aircraft sitting on the Ardmore apron on a hot January day can have cockpit temperatures exceeding 50 degrees C before engine start. Management: ventilate before entry (open doors/windows to cool the cockpit), use air conditioning or ram air vents in flight, stay hydrated, wear light clothing, use sun shading, and plan rest stops for long hot-weather operations.
exam_tip: Heat: increased heart rate, dehydration, reduced concentration, irritability. Cockpit temperatures can exceed 50 degrees C on apron in summer. Manage: ventilate, hydrate, use AC/vents, light clothing.
key_facts: Increased heart rate, dehydration, reduced concentration, irritability
Cognitive performance drops measurably above 38 degrees C body temperature
Parked cockpits can exceed 50 degrees C on hot days
Management: ventilate, hydrate, use cooling systems, light clothing, rest stops
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe how the following environmental stressors affect pilot performance: (b) cold.
concept: Cold stress occurs when body temperature drops below comfortable levels, particularly at altitude in unpressurised aircraft or in poorly heated cockpits. Cold causes: reduced fine motor skills and dexterity (fingers become stiff and clumsy, making instrument operation difficult), reduced blood flow to extremities (body prioritises core temperature), shivering (distracting and physically fatiguing), reduced cognitive performance, and increased urinary frequency (cold diuresis, adding to dehydration). Prolonged cold exposure leads to hypothermia, progressing through stages of increasingly impaired judgement, coordination, and consciousness. In NZ winter operations, cockpit temperatures can be uncomfortably low, particularly in older GA aircraft with minimal heating. Flying at altitude in unpressurised aircraft without adequate heating is a cold stress risk. Management: dress appropriately in layers, ensure cabin heating is functional, carry warm clothing even for short flights in winter, keep extremities warm (gloves), maintain hydration (cold environments are dehydrating), and limit exposure duration in cold cockpits.
exam_tip: Cold: reduced dexterity (fingers), shivering, impaired cognition, cold diuresis (dehydration). Hypothermia risk in NZ winter ops. Manage: layers, functional heating, gloves, hydration, limit exposure.
key_facts: Reduced fine motor skills and dexterity (stiff, clumsy fingers)
Shivering causes distraction and physical fatigue
Impaired cognitive performance and judgement
Management: dress in layers, ensure heating works, gloves, hydrate, limit cold exposure
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe how the following environmental stressors affect pilot performance: (c) noise.
concept: Noise in the cockpit is a chronic stressor that degrades performance in multiple ways. Direct effects: interference with radio communications (you miss or misinterpret ATC calls), masking of aural warnings and alerts, difficulty with crew communication, and permanent hearing damage from chronic exposure. Indirect effects: noise is physically fatiguing--the effort of communicating and concentrating against a high noise background drains your energy faster than working in a quiet environment. Noise increases general stress levels, contributing to irritability and reduced patience. It degrades concentration and working memory--you have to work harder to think clearly, and some of your cognitive capacity is consumed by filtering noise. In piston GA aircraft (85-100+ dB cockpit noise), long days of flying produce significant noise-related fatigue. Management: use ANR headsets (the single most effective measure), maintain aircraft noise-reducing features (exhaust, door seals, insulation), limit exposure duration where possible, schedule rest breaks, and protect hearing with regular audiometric monitoring.
exam_tip: Noise: interferes with comms, masks warnings, causes fatigue, degrades concentration. Piston cockpits: 85-100+ dB. Best single measure: ANR headset. Noise fatigue is cumulative over long duty days.
key_facts: Interferes with radio communications and masks aural warnings
Physically fatiguing: concentrating against noise consumes cognitive capacity
Increases general stress, irritability, and reduces patience
Best management: ANR headset; maintain noise insulation; limit exposure; regular hearing tests
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe how the following environmental stressors affect pilot performance: (d) vibration.
concept: Vibration as an environmental stressor (covered in more detail under 34.36.10) affects pilot performance through both direct physical effects and indirect fatigue. Direct: visual degradation at resonant frequencies (4-8 Hz blurs the retinal image, making instruments hard to read), reduced fine motor control (hands and fingers vibrate, making precise switch and knob operation difficult), discomfort and pain from prolonged exposure. Indirect: vibration is physically fatiguing even when not consciously noticed--the constant micro-adjustments your muscles make to maintain posture against vibration consume energy. Over a long duty day, this contributes significantly to overall fatigue. Vibration also causes motion sickness in susceptible individuals and contributes to chronic back and neck problems for career pilots. Helicopter pilots experience the greatest vibration exposure. Management: vibration-damping seats, proper posture, limiting exposure duration, regular breaks, maintaining aircraft vibration dampers and mounts, and reporting unusual vibration for maintenance action.
