exam_tip: Vibration: visual blurring at 4-8 Hz, reduced motor control, physical fatigue (even when not noticed), chronic back problems. Helicopter pilots most affected. Manage: damped seats, posture, breaks, maintenance.
key_facts: 4-8 Hz resonance blurs vision; makes instrument reading difficult
Reduces fine motor control for switch and knob operation
Physically fatiguing through constant micro-muscular adjustments
Chronic effects: back/neck problems, especially for helicopter pilots
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe how the following environmental stressors affect pilot performance: (e) humidity.
concept: Humidity affects pilot performance at both extremes. Low humidity (common in pressurised aircraft cabins at 10-20%, and at altitude in unpressurised aircraft) causes dehydration through increased insensible water loss from breathing and skin evaporation. This leads to dry eyes (uncomfortable and potentially problematic for contact lens wearers), dry nasal passages and throat (making you more susceptible to respiratory infections and reducing comfort), and general dehydration with its associated cognitive impairment. High humidity (common in tropical operations or on the ground in NZ summer) reduces the body's ability to cool through evaporation of sweat, increasing heat stress risk. It makes the cockpit feel hotter than the actual temperature, increasing discomfort and irritability. High humidity combined with heat is more debilitating than either alone. Fog and condensation can form on cold cockpit surfaces in humid conditions, degrading visibility. Management for low humidity: drink water regularly, use eye drops for contact lens wearers, consider a nasal moisturiser for very long flights. For high humidity: maximise ventilation, use air conditioning, dress lightly.
exam_tip: Low humidity (10-20% in pressurised cabins): dehydration, dry eyes, respiratory susceptibility. High humidity: impairs sweat cooling, increases heat stress. Manage low: hydrate, eye drops. Manage high: ventilate, AC, light clothing.
key_facts: Low humidity: dehydration, dry eyes/nasal passages, respiratory infection susceptibility
High humidity: impairs evaporative cooling, increases heat stress and discomfort
Combined heat + high humidity more debilitating than either alone
Management: hydrate (low), ventilate and cool (high), eye drops for contact lens wearers
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain methods of identifying stress.
concept: Identifying stress in yourself and others requires awareness of the physical, emotional, cognitive, and behavioural indicators. Physical signs: increased heart rate, sweating, muscle tension (especially shoulders and jaw), headaches, digestive problems, disturbed sleep, fatigue that doesn't resolve with rest. Emotional signs: irritability, anxiety, feeling overwhelmed, loss of motivation, mood swings, withdrawal from social interaction, feeling detached. Cognitive signs: difficulty concentrating, forgetfulness, indecisiveness, negative thinking, racing thoughts, making more errors than usual. Behavioural signs: changes in eating or drinking habits (including increased alcohol consumption), deteriorating work performance, increased absenteeism, avoidance of previously normal activities, reduced communication, becoming more accident-prone. In a crew environment, watching for these signs in your colleagues is part of CRM. Self-identification is hardest because stress affects the very cognitive functions you need to recognise it. Using structured tools like the IMSAFE checklist regularly, and having honest conversations with colleagues and partners, helps catch stress before it becomes a performance problem.
exam_tip: Four categories: physical (heart rate, tension, headaches), emotional (irritability, anxiety), cognitive (poor concentration, errors), behavioural (habit changes, withdrawal). Self-identification is hardest. Use IMSAFE; watch colleagues.
key_facts: Physical: increased heart rate, muscle tension, headaches, sleep disturbance
Emotional: irritability, anxiety, feeling overwhelmed, mood swings
Cognitive: poor concentration, forgetfulness, indecisiveness, increased errors
Behavioural: changed eating/drinking habits, reduced performance, withdrawal, avoidance
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the difference between acute and chronic stress.
concept: Acute stress is the immediate response to a specific stressor--an engine failure, a weather diversion, a difficult approach, a conflict with a passenger. It's short-term, has a clear cause, and typically resolves once the stressor is removed or the situation is dealt with. Acute stress can actually be helpful: the adrenaline response improves alertness and reaction speed, allowing you to deal with emergencies effectively. The body returns to normal relatively quickly after acute stress. Chronic stress is long-term, persistent stress from ongoing situations that don't resolve: a difficult work environment, financial problems, relationship breakdown, chronic illness, job insecurity, or sustained high workload over weeks or months. Chronic stress is far more dangerous than acute stress because the body doesn't get a chance to recover. Cortisol levels remain elevated, causing immune system suppression, sleep disturbance, weight gain, cardiovascular strain, and progressive degradation of cognitive function. Chronic stress builds gradually--you may not notice it until the effects are significant. For CPL pilots, the combination of irregular hours, commercial pressures, and personal stressors can create chronic stress that slowly erodes performance.
exam_tip: Acute: short-term, specific cause, resolves, can be helpful (improved alertness). Chronic: long-term, ongoing causes, doesn't resolve, body can't recover -- far more dangerous. Chronic stress builds gradually and is harder to identify.
key_facts: Acute: short-term response to specific stressor; can improve performance; resolves quickly
Chronic: long-term, ongoing; body doesn't recover; progressive health and cognitive damage
Chronic stress causes elevated cortisol, immune suppression, sleep disturbance, cognitive decline
CPL pilots: irregular hours + commercial pressures + personal stressors = chronic stress risk
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the physiological effects of stress.
concept: The physiological stress response is mediated primarily through the autonomic nervous system and the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. Acute stress triggers the sympathetic nervous system: adrenaline (epinephrine) is released, causing increased heart rate and blood pressure, bronchodilation (airways open), pupil dilation, blood flow redirection from digestive organs to muscles and brain, increased blood glucose for energy, sweating, and suppression of non-essential functions (digestion, immune response). The HPA axis releases cortisol, which sustains the stress response and mobilises energy reserves. In the short term, these changes are adaptive and helpful for dealing with threats. With chronic stress, sustained elevated cortisol causes: immune system suppression (increased illness), disturbed sleep architecture, weight gain (particularly abdominal fat), elevated blood pressure and cardiovascular strain, impaired memory and learning, reduced wound healing, and digestive problems. The physiological effects of chronic stress are a significant occupational health concern for commercial pilots and can ultimately threaten the medical certificate.
exam_tip: Acute: adrenaline (increased HR, BP, blood glucose, muscle blood flow). Sustained: cortisol (immune suppression, sleep disturbance, weight gain, cardiovascular strain, memory impairment). Chronic stress threatens medical certificate.
key_facts: Acute: adrenaline increases HR, BP, blood glucose, redirects blood to muscles and brain
Cortisol sustains stress response and mobilises energy reserves
Chronic elevated cortisol: immune suppression, sleep disturbance, weight gain, CV strain
Memory and cognitive function progressively impaired by chronic stress
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the psychological effects of stress.
concept: Psychological effects of stress compound the physiological effects and can be just as debilitating. Cognitive effects: impaired concentration, difficulty making decisions (either paralysis or impulsive choices), reduced working memory capacity, tunnel vision (focusing on one aspect while ignoring others), increased error rate, and difficulty prioritising tasks. Emotional effects: anxiety, irritability, anger (disproportionate reactions to minor issues), feelings of being overwhelmed or out of control, reduced motivation, emotional flatness or detachment, and mood swings. Behavioural effects: social withdrawal, increased alcohol or substance use as coping mechanisms, avoidance of challenging situations, deterioration in professional standards, and reduced communication (both in quantity and quality). Interpersonal effects: increased conflict with colleagues and family, reduced empathy, difficulty working in a crew environment, and reduced tolerance for others' mistakes. For pilots, the psychological effects of stress directly impact CRM effectiveness: communication, teamwork, decision-making, and workload management all suffer. These effects are often the first things noticed by colleagues, even before the stressed pilot recognises them themselves.
exam_tip: Cognitive: poor decisions, tunnel vision, increased errors. Emotional: anxiety, irritability, mood swings. Behavioural: withdrawal, substance use, avoidance. Interpersonal: conflict, reduced CRM effectiveness. Colleagues often notice before the pilot does.
key_facts: Cognitive: impaired concentration, poor decision-making, tunnel vision, increased errors
Emotional: anxiety, irritability, disproportionate reactions, feeling overwhelmed
Behavioural: social withdrawal, increased alcohol use, avoidance, declining standards
Interpersonal: increased conflict, reduced empathy, degraded CRM effectiveness
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of stress on attention, motivation and performance.
concept: Stress affects attention, motivation, and performance in a pattern that follows the Yerkes-Dodson inverted-U curve. Attention: under moderate stress, attention sharpens and focuses effectively. Under excessive stress, attention narrows pathologically (attentional tunnelling)--you fixate on one thing while missing other critical information. You might focus entirely on an engine problem while ignoring your decreasing altitude or an ATC instruction. Alternatively, attention may scatter--jumping rapidly between tasks without completing any, which is equally dangerous. Motivation: moderate stress energises and motivates (you want to solve the problem). Excessive stress can either create desperate over-motivation (frantic, poorly-directed action) or complete demotivation (giving up, learned helplessness). Performance: follows the inverted-U precisely. Moderate stress improves performance through heightened alertness and focus. Excessive stress degrades performance through cognitive overload, error increase, skill regression (reverting to earlier-learned but less appropriate responses), and eventually task paralysis. The transition from helpful to harmful stress can be rapid, especially when stressors combine.
exam_tip: Inverted-U (Yerkes-Dodson): moderate stress improves attention, motivation, and performance. Excessive stress causes attentional tunnelling, frantic or frozen motivation, and skill regression. The transition from helpful to harmful can be rapid.
key_facts: Attention: moderate stress sharpens focus; excessive stress causes tunnelling or scattering
Motivation: moderate stress energises; excessive causes frantic action or helplessness
Performance follows inverted-U: improves to optimum, then degrades rapidly
Skill regression under extreme stress: reverting to earlier-learned (often inappropriate) responses
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors that improve personal stress tolerance.
concept: Building stress resilience is like building physical fitness--it requires deliberate effort and maintenance. Physical factors: regular exercise improves cardiovascular fitness and the body's stress response efficiency. Good nutrition provides the energy and nutrients needed for stress coping. Adequate sleep is fundamental--a well-rested person handles stress far better than a fatigued one. Avoid excessive alcohol and caffeine (both worsen stress responses in the medium term). Psychological factors: developing positive coping strategies (problem-solving, seeking support, maintaining perspective), realistic self-assessment (knowing your limits reduces anxiety), maintaining interests and social connections outside aviation, learning relaxation techniques (breathing exercises, progressive muscle relaxation, mindfulness). Professional factors: maintaining proficiency and currency builds confidence that reduces task-related stress. Good CRM skills distribute workload. Standard procedures reduce the cognitive load of routine tasks, preserving capacity for unexpected events. Building strong professional relationships provides peer support. Organisational factors: a supportive workplace culture where discussing stress isn't stigmatised, reasonable rostering, access to employee assistance programmes.
exam_tip: Physical: exercise, nutrition, sleep, limit alcohol/caffeine. Psychological: coping strategies, realistic self-assessment, relaxation techniques, social connections. Professional: proficiency, CRM, SOPs. Organisational: supportive culture, reasonable rostering.
key_facts: Physical: regular exercise, good nutrition, adequate sleep, limit alcohol and caffeine
Psychological: positive coping strategies, realistic self-assessment, relaxation techniques
Professional: currency and proficiency, CRM skills, standard procedures, peer relationships
Organisational: supportive culture, reasonable rostering, employee assistance access
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the relationship between stress and fatigue.
concept: Stress and fatigue have a bidirectional, compounding relationship--each makes the other worse. Stress causes fatigue: the sustained physiological activation of the stress response (elevated cortisol, increased heart rate, muscle tension) consumes energy. Mentally, processing stressors consumes cognitive resources. Emotional stress disrupts sleep, preventing recovery. Chronically stressed people report higher fatigue levels even with apparently adequate sleep, because stress reduces sleep quality. Fatigue causes stress: when you're fatigued, your capacity to cope with demands is reduced, so situations that you'd handle easily when rested become stressful. Your emotional regulation deteriorates (you become irritable, reactive), your decision-making degrades, and your threshold for overload drops. This creates a vicious cycle: stress causes poor sleep, which causes fatigue, which reduces coping capacity, which increases stress, which further disrupts sleep. Breaking this cycle requires addressing both elements simultaneously. For commercial pilots, the combination of flight-related stress and fatigue from irregular hours is a significant safety concern that operators must manage through fatigue risk management systems.
exam_tip: Bidirectional vicious cycle: stress causes fatigue (energy depletion, sleep disruption); fatigue reduces stress coping capacity, increasing stress. Must address both simultaneously. FRMS addresses this for commercial operators.
key_facts: Stress causes fatigue through sustained physiological activation and disrupted sleep
Fatigue reduces stress coping capacity, making normal demands feel overwhelming
Creates a vicious cycle: stress > poor sleep > fatigue > reduced coping > more stress
Breaking the cycle requires addressing both stress and fatigue simultaneously
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain methods of managing stress.
concept: Effective stress management uses multiple strategies simultaneously. Immediate/acute strategies: breathing techniques (slow, deep breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system, counteracting the stress response), progressive muscle relaxation, mental reframing (asking 'what's the worst that can realistically happen?' or 'what would I advise someone else in this situation?'), and task prioritisation (aviate, navigate, communicate). Short-term strategies: talking about stressors with trusted colleagues or friends, physical exercise (burns off stress hormones), adequate rest and sleep, maintaining hobbies and interests outside aviation, limiting alcohol and caffeine. Long-term strategies: developing robust personal coping mechanisms, maintaining physical fitness, building strong social support networks, addressing the root causes of stress where possible (changing work patterns, resolving relationship issues, seeking financial advice), professional support (counselling, CBT), and learning to recognise personal stress indicators early. Organisational strategies: using CRM principles to distribute workload, following SOPs to reduce cognitive load, peer support programmes, fatigue risk management, and maintaining open communication with management about operational pressures.
exam_tip: Manage at multiple levels: immediate (breathing, reframing, prioritise), short-term (talk, exercise, rest, hobbies), long-term (address root causes, fitness, support networks, professional help), organisational (CRM, SOPs, peer support, FRMS).
key_facts: Immediate: breathing techniques, mental reframing, task prioritisation (ANC)
Short-term: exercise, rest, talking with trusted people, hobbies, limit alcohol/caffeine
Long-term: address root causes, professional support (counselling, CBT), fitness, social networks
Organisational: CRM, SOPs, peer support, FRMS, open communication with management
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the stages of sleep.
concept: Sleep progresses through distinct stages in approximately 90-minute cycles. Stage 1 (N1): light sleep, the transition from wakefulness--you're easily woken, may experience hypnic jerks (those falling sensations), and brain waves shift from alpha to theta. Lasts about 5-10 minutes. Stage 2 (N2): brain waves slow further with sleep spindles and K-complexes; body temperature drops, heart rate slows. You spend about 50% of total sleep time in Stage 2. Stage 3 (N3, formerly Stages 3 and 4): deep sleep or slow-wave sleep (SWS)--delta waves dominate. This is the most physically restorative stage: growth hormone is released, tissue repair occurs, immune function is enhanced. Very hard to wake someone from this stage; if woken, you feel groggy (sleep inertia). REM sleep: brain activity increases to near-waking levels, eyes move rapidly, muscle tone is suppressed (atonia), and dreaming occurs. REM is important for memory consolidation, learning, and emotional processing. A full night's sleep cycles through these stages 4-6 times. Deep sleep dominates early cycles; REM dominates later cycles. This is why both going to bed early enough and sleeping late enough matter.
exam_tip: Stages: N1 (light, transition), N2 (50% of sleep, spindles), N3 (deep/SWS, physically restorative), REM (dreaming, memory consolidation). ~90-minute cycles. Deep sleep early, REM late. Both essential.
key_facts: N1 (light transition), N2 (50% of sleep), N3 (deep/SWS, physically restorative), REM (dreaming, memory)
Sleep cycles approximately 90 minutes; 4-6 cycles per night
Deep sleep (N3) dominates early cycles; REM dominates later cycles
Sleep inertia (grogginess) worst when woken from deep sleep (N3)
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the mechanism of sleep regulation.
concept: Sleep is regulated by two interacting processes. Process S (homeostatic sleep drive): a pressure to sleep that builds progressively the longer you've been awake. The longer you're awake, the stronger the drive to sleep becomes. This is mediated by the accumulation of adenosine in the brain (which is why caffeine, an adenosine blocker, temporarily reduces sleepiness). After about 16 hours of wakefulness, the sleep drive becomes very strong. Sleep dissipates the accumulated drive, and you wake feeling refreshed. Process C (circadian rhythm): an internal body clock controlled by the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) in the hypothalamus, which cycles approximately every 24 hours, promoting wakefulness during the day and sleep at night. This clock is synchronised to the external light-dark cycle through specialised photoreceptors in the retina. The two processes interact: you feel most alert when Process C is promoting wakefulness and Process S pressure is low (mid-morning after a good night's sleep). You feel most sleepy when Process C promotes sleep and Process S pressure is high (early hours of the morning after a long day). Understanding these two processes explains why jet lag, shift work, and early starts are so disruptive.
exam_tip: Two-process model: Process S (homeostatic sleep pressure, builds with wakefulness, mediated by adenosine) + Process C (circadian rhythm, ~24 hr cycle, SCN). Most alert: morning after good sleep. Most sleepy: 0200-0600.
key_facts: Process S: homeostatic sleep pressure builds with time awake, mediated by adenosine
Process C: circadian rhythm (~24 hrs), controlled by SCN, synchronised by light
Most alert: Process C promoting wakefulness + low Process S (mid-morning after good sleep)
Most sleepy: Process C promoting sleep + high Process S (0200-0600 after long wakefulness)
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe problems associated with sleep at abnormal times of the day.
concept: Sleeping at abnormal times (daytime sleep for night shift workers, early sleep for very early starts) is inherently less effective than normal nighttime sleep because it conflicts with your circadian rhythm. Process C actively promotes wakefulness during the day, making it harder to fall asleep and harder to stay asleep. Daytime sleep is typically shorter (by 2-4 hours), lighter (less deep sleep), and more fragmented (more awakenings) than nighttime sleep. External factors compound the problem: daylight (even through curtains stimulates wakefulness), ambient noise (daytime is noisier), social demands (family, phone calls, deliveries), and internal temperature rhythm (body temperature peaks in the afternoon, promoting wakefulness). The result is chronic sleep debt for anyone regularly sleeping at abnormal times. For NZ CPL pilots doing early morning freight operations, late evening charter returns, or irregular schedules, the inability to obtain quality sleep during off-duty periods is a significant fatigue risk. Strategies to improve off-schedule sleep: blackout curtains, earplugs, consistent sleep routines, avoiding caffeine in the hours before sleep, and communicating sleep needs to household members.
exam_tip: Daytime sleep: 2-4 hours shorter, lighter, more fragmented due to circadian wakefulness drive. Leads to chronic sleep debt. Strategies: blackout curtains, earplugs, consistent routine, avoid caffeine, communicate with household.
key_facts: Daytime sleep conflicts with circadian wakefulness drive (Process C)
Typically 2-4 hours shorter, lighter, and more fragmented than nighttime sleep
External factors: daylight, noise, social demands, body temperature all disrupt
Leads to chronic sleep debt for shift workers and pilots on irregular schedules
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by sleep debt.
concept: Sleep debt (or sleep deficit) is the cumulative difference between the amount of sleep you need and the amount you actually get. Most adults need 7-9 hours of sleep per night for optimal function. If you need 8 hours but only get 6, you accumulate 2 hours of sleep debt per night. After a five-day working week of 6-hour nights, you've got 10 hours of sleep debt. Sleep debt doesn't reset on its own--it accumulates and must be 'repaid' with extra sleep. The performance effects of accumulated sleep debt are similar to the effects of total sleep deprivation: impaired reaction time, degraded decision-making, increased errors, microsleeps (brief involuntary sleep episodes lasting seconds), reduced situational awareness, and impaired mood. Research shows that 17 hours of sustained wakefulness produces impairment equivalent to a blood alcohol level of 0.05%. After 24 hours awake, impairment is equivalent to 0.10% BAC. The insidious aspect of sleep debt is that you adapt subjectively--you stop feeling sleepy and think you're performing normally--even though objective performance tests show significant impairment. This is why sleep debt is often called 'the silent killer' in aviation.
exam_tip: Sleep debt: cumulative gap between needed and actual sleep. Doesn't self-reset. 17 hrs awake = 0.05% BAC equivalent. 24 hrs = 0.10%. Subjective adaptation: you FEEL okay but performance is measurably impaired.
key_facts: Cumulative difference between sleep needed (~7-9 hrs) and sleep obtained
Doesn't self-reset; must be repaid with extra sleep
17 hours awake = impairment equivalent to 0.05% BAC; 24 hours = 0.10% BAC
Subjective adaptation masks impairment: pilots feel fine but perform measurably worse
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain how individuals differ in their requirement for sleep.
concept: Sleep need varies between individuals due to genetic, age, and lifestyle factors. Most adults need 7-9 hours, with the average being about 8 hours. A small percentage of people (true 'short sleepers') genuinely function well on 6 hours or less--this is genetically determined and rare (probably less than 5% of the population). Many more people think they're short sleepers but are actually running on accumulated sleep debt and have adapted to feeling tired as 'normal.' Age affects sleep need: teenagers need 8-10 hours; young adults typically need 7-9 hours; older adults may need slightly less (7-8 hours) but their sleep is often more fragmented. Individual chronotype (whether you're a 'morning person' or 'evening person') affects when you sleep best but not necessarily how much you need. Physical activity level, stress, illness, and recovery from sleep debt all temporarily increase sleep need. For CPL pilots, the key message is: know your personal sleep requirement and respect it. If you need 8 hours, scheduling 6 hours of sleep opportunity and telling yourself you're fine is a safety hazard.
exam_tip: Most adults need 7-9 hours; average 8 hours. True short sleepers are rare (<5%). Many think they need less but are running on sleep debt. Know YOUR requirement and respect it--it's a safety issue.
key_facts: Most adults need 7-9 hours; average approximately 8 hours
True genetic short sleepers (<6 hrs) are rare, probably less than 5%
Many people are adapted to chronic sleep debt and wrongly think they need less
Age, chronotype, activity, stress, and illness all affect individual sleep requirements
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following alertness management techniques: (a) napping.
concept: Strategic napping is one of the most effective short-term fatigue countermeasures available. A 20-30 minute nap (a 'power nap') can significantly improve alertness, reaction time, and cognitive performance for 1-3 hours after waking. The key is timing: keep naps under 30 minutes to avoid entering deep sleep (Stage N3), which causes significant sleep inertia (grogginess) on waking that can last 15-30 minutes and temporarily make performance worse than before the nap. If a longer nap is needed, a 90-minute nap allows a full sleep cycle and waking from lighter sleep with less inertia. NASA research on long-haul flight crew found that a planned 40-minute nap opportunity improved performance by 34% and alertness by 54%. For NZ CPL operations, napping during breaks or before a known demanding flight is a legitimate and effective fatigue management tool. However, napping is a supplement to, not a replacement for, adequate sleep. It manages acute fatigue but doesn't repay accumulated sleep debt.
exam_tip: Power nap: 20-30 minutes improves alertness for 1-3 hours. Keep under 30 min to avoid deep sleep inertia. If longer: 90 min (full cycle). NASA research: 40-min nap = 34% performance improvement. Supplement, not replacement for sleep.
key_facts: 20-30 minute nap improves alertness, reaction time, and cognition for 1-3 hours
Keep under 30 minutes to avoid sleep inertia from deep sleep entry
90-minute nap allows a full cycle with less inertia on waking
NASA: 40-minute nap opportunity improved performance by 34%, alertness by 54%
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations, NASA napping study

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following alertness management techniques: (b) caffeine consumption.
concept: Caffeine is the most widely used psychoactive substance in aviation and the world. It works by blocking adenosine receptors in the brain (adenosine is the chemical that accumulates during wakefulness, making you sleepy). The result is temporarily increased alertness, improved reaction time, and reduced subjective sleepiness. Caffeine reaches peak blood levels about 30-60 minutes after consumption and has a half-life of approximately 5-6 hours (though this varies significantly between individuals). In moderate doses (200-400 mg, equivalent to 2-4 cups of coffee), caffeine is an effective short-term alertness aid. However, caffeine has significant limitations: it doesn't restore the cognitive functions degraded by sleep loss (judgement, decision-making, complex reasoning remain impaired even though you feel more alert), it can cause anxiety, jitteriness, and increased heart rate, it disrupts subsequent sleep if consumed too late in the day, and tolerance develops with regular use, reducing its effectiveness. The recommendation is to use caffeine strategically (before known demanding periods) rather than continuously, and to avoid it within 6 hours of planned sleep.
exam_tip: Caffeine blocks adenosine receptors, increasing alertness. Peak at 30-60 min, half-life ~5-6 hours. Effective short-term but doesn't restore complex cognition. Avoid within 6 hours of sleep. Tolerance develops with regular use.
key_facts: Blocks adenosine receptors, temporarily increasing alertness and reaction time
Peak blood levels 30-60 minutes after consumption; half-life ~5-6 hours
Does NOT restore complex cognitive functions degraded by sleep loss
Avoid within 6 hours of planned sleep; tolerance develops with regular use
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following alertness management techniques: (c) taking sedatives.
concept: Sedatives (sleeping pills) are sometimes used by pilots to obtain sleep during abnormal sleep opportunities, such as before early starts or during layovers in unfamiliar environments. However, their use in aviation is highly problematic. Benzodiazepines (e.g., diazepam, temazepam): cause residual sedation, impaired coordination, and reduced alertness lasting well beyond the apparent 'sleep' period--commonly 12-24 hours. They alter sleep architecture, reducing the amount of restorative deep sleep and REM sleep. They also cause anterograde amnesia. Z-drugs (e.g., zopiclone, zolpidem): shorter acting but still carry residual sedation risk, and can cause bizarre sleep behaviours. Melatonin: a hormone that assists with circadian rhythm shifting and sleep initiation, with fewer residual effects than sedatives. It's available over-the-counter in NZ and is generally the only sleep aid that may be acceptable for aviation use, but you should still consult your DAME. The NZ CAA general position: no pilot should use sedatives within a specified stand-down period before flying (typically 24-48 hours depending on the drug). If you need sedatives regularly to sleep, the underlying cause needs medical investigation.
exam_tip: Sedatives are generally incompatible with aviation: residual sedation 12-24+ hours, altered sleep architecture, impaired function. Melatonin may be acceptable with DAME approval. Regular sedative use requires medical investigation.
key_facts: Benzodiazepines: residual sedation 12-24 hours, altered sleep architecture, amnesia
Z-drugs: shorter acting but still residual sedation risk and bizarre sleep behaviours
Melatonin: generally most acceptable for aviation, fewer residual effects, but consult DAME
Regular need for sedatives indicates underlying condition requiring medical investigation
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following alertness management techniques: (d) taking stimulants other than caffeine.
concept: Stimulants other than caffeine used to combat fatigue are generally incompatible with aviation. Amphetamines (historically used by military pilots): effective at maintaining wakefulness but cause elevated heart rate, increased blood pressure, anxiety, overconfidence, risk-taking behaviour, and a severe 'crash' effect when they wear off. Not approved for civil aviation. Modafinil: a wakefulness-promoting agent used for narcolepsy and shift-work sleep disorder. It has fewer side effects than amphetamines but still causes headache, nausea, and anxiety in some users. Not generally approved for aviation use without specific medical authorisation. Ephedrine and pseudoephedrine (found in some cold remedies): stimulant effects that cause anxiety, elevated heart rate, and restlessness. Energy drinks: contain high doses of caffeine plus other stimulants (taurine, guarana) with unpredictable combined effects and high crash risk. Nicotine: a stimulant with short-lived alertness benefit but significant health consequences (covered under smoking). The fundamental problem with stimulants is that they mask fatigue without addressing it--you feel alert but your cognitive function remains degraded. The only genuine cure for fatigue is sleep.
exam_tip: Stimulants other than caffeine are generally incompatible with aviation. Amphetamines: overconfidence, crash effect. Modafinil: not generally approved. Energy drinks: unpredictable. Key message: stimulants mask fatigue without curing it. Only sleep cures fatigue.
key_facts: Amphetamines: effective but cause overconfidence, anxiety, crash effect; not approved for civil aviation
Modafinil: fewer side effects but not generally approved without specific medical authorisation
Energy drinks: unpredictable combined stimulant effects and crash risk
All stimulants mask fatigue without restoring cognitive function; only sleep cures fatigue
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe sleep disorders and their effects on pilot performance.
concept: Sleep disorders are more common than most pilots realise and can be career-threatening if undiagnosed. Obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA): the most significant sleep disorder for aviation--the airway collapses during sleep, causing repeated brief awakenings (often hundreds per night) that the sleeper may not recall. Results in chronic unrestorative sleep, excessive daytime sleepiness, impaired cognitive function, and increased cardiovascular risk. Strongly associated with obesity, male gender, and middle age. Treated with CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure). Insomnia: difficulty falling asleep or staying asleep, causing daytime fatigue and impaired function. Can be primary or secondary to other conditions (anxiety, depression, pain). Restless legs syndrome: uncomfortable sensations in the legs that cause an urge to move, disrupting sleep onset and quality. Narcolepsy: sudden involuntary sleep episodes--extremely dangerous in the cockpit and generally disqualifying. Parasomnias: sleepwalking, sleep terrors, REM behaviour disorder. For pilots, any suspected sleep disorder should be reported to the DAME for investigation. Undiagnosed OSA is particularly concerning because the pilot may not know they're sleeping badly.
exam_tip: OSA: most significant for aviation -- chronic unrestorative sleep, daytime sleepiness, cardiovascular risk. Associated with obesity. Treated with CPAP. Narcolepsy: generally disqualifying. Report any suspected sleep disorder to DAME.
key_facts: OSA: repeated airway collapse during sleep; chronic unrestorative sleep; CPAP treatment
Associated with obesity, male gender, middle age; often undiagnosed
Insomnia: difficulty sleeping, causing daytime impairment; investigate underlying causes
Narcolepsy (involuntary sleep episodes): generally disqualifying for aviation
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of fatigue and its effect on pilot performance.
concept: Fatigue in aviation has multiple interacting causes: insufficient sleep (the primary cause), disrupted sleep quality, extended wakefulness, circadian disruption (working during the body's normal sleep period), high workload over extended periods, environmental stressors (noise, vibration, temperature), boring or monotonous tasks (vigilance fatigue), and physical exertion. The effects on pilot performance are comprehensive: slowed reaction time, impaired decision-making, reduced situational awareness, increased errors and difficulty detecting errors, microsleeps (involuntary sleep episodes of 1-10 seconds where you're completely unaware), attentional failures (fixation or mind wandering), degraded communication, reduced motivation, increased irritability, and impaired risk perception (fatigued pilots accept risks they wouldn't accept when rested). Fatigue has been identified as a contributing factor in a significant percentage of aviation accidents and incidents. The most dangerous aspect is that fatigued individuals consistently underestimate their own impairment--just like alcohol, fatigue removes the ability to accurately self-assess performance.
exam_tip: Causes: insufficient/disrupted sleep, extended wakefulness, circadian disruption, workload, environmental stressors. Effects: slowed reactions, poor decisions, microsleeps, errors. Most dangerous: pilots underestimate their own fatigue impairment.
key_facts: Primary cause: insufficient sleep; also circadian disruption, workload, environmental stressors
Microsleeps (1-10 second involuntary sleep episodes): pilot completely unaware
Impairs reaction time, decision-making, situational awareness, error detection, communication
Fatigued pilots consistently underestimate their own impairment (like alcohol)
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of fatigue.
concept: Fatigue symptoms span physical, cognitive, and emotional domains. Physical: heavy eyelids, yawning, difficulty keeping eyes open, slumped posture, reduced physical coordination, headache, muscle aches. Cognitive: difficulty concentrating, mind wandering, fixating on irrelevant details, forgetting items from checklists or ATC instructions, difficulty making decisions, slow mental processing, increased errors (particularly errors of omission--forgetting to do things), narrow attention. Emotional: irritability, reduced motivation, social withdrawal, apathy, short temper. Behavioural: longer response times, sloppy radio calls, missed radio calls, deviations from standard procedures, less communication with crew, reluctance to take on additional tasks. Dangerous signs indicating severe fatigue: microsleeps (brief involuntary sleep episodes), head nodding, 'zoning out' (arriving at a point with no memory of getting there), and an inability to remember the last few minutes. If any crew member notices these signs in themselves or their colleague, it should be treated as an urgent safety concern. The challenge: fatigued people often don't recognise their own symptoms because the recognition faculty itself is impaired.
exam_tip: Physical: yawning, heavy eyelids. Cognitive: mind wandering, forgetting tasks, slow processing. Emotional: irritability, apathy. Dangerous signs: microsleeps, head nodding, memory gaps. Fatigued people often don't recognise their own symptoms.
key_facts: Physical: yawning, heavy eyelids, slumped posture, reduced coordination
Cognitive: mind wandering, forgetting items, difficulty concentrating, increased errors of omission
Emotional: irritability, reduced motivation, apathy, short temper
Dangerous signs: microsleeps, head nodding, memory gaps -- treat as urgent safety concern
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the difference between acute and chronic fatigue.
concept: Acute fatigue results from recent sleep loss, extended wakefulness, or a single period of high workload. It has a clear cause, is usually recognised by the individual, and is resolved by a single adequate sleep period (7-9 hours). A pilot who flew a demanding 12-hour day and feels exhausted has acute fatigue--a good night's sleep will restore them. Chronic fatigue builds over days, weeks, or months from cumulative sleep debt, sustained high workload, ongoing stress, or medical conditions. It's harder to recognise because the pilot adapts to feeling tired as their new 'normal.' Chronic fatigue is NOT resolved by a single good sleep--it requires several days of adequate sleep to repay the accumulated debt and restore full function. Symptoms of chronic fatigue may include persistent tiredness despite apparently adequate sleep, reduced overall performance baseline, increased illness frequency (immune suppression), and emotional flatness or depression. For commercial pilots, chronic fatigue is the more dangerous condition because it's self-reinforcing and often unrecognised. It requires systematic management: reduced duty hours, extended rest periods, and potentially time off to fully recover.
exam_tip: Acute: recent cause, recognised, resolved by one good sleep. Chronic: cumulative over days/weeks, harder to recognise, NOT resolved by one sleep -- requires extended recovery. Chronic is more dangerous because it's self-reinforcing and unrecognised.
key_facts: Acute: recent cause (long day, sleep loss), recognised, resolved by one adequate sleep
Chronic: cumulative over days/weeks, person adapts to tiredness as 'normal'
Chronic NOT resolved by single sleep -- requires several days of adequate rest
Chronic fatigue: more dangerous, self-reinforcing, often unrecognised, requires systematic management
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe methods of managing fatigue.
concept: Fatigue management operates at individual and organisational levels. Individual: prioritise sleep (7-9 hours per night), maintain regular sleep/wake schedules where possible, use strategic napping (20-30 min power naps), manage caffeine use (strategic, not continuous, avoid before sleep), maintain physical fitness, eat well and stay hydrated, recognise your personal fatigue signs and act on them (don't try to push through), communicate your fatigue state honestly to crew and dispatch. Organisational (FRMS - Fatigue Risk Management System): science-based duty time and flight time limitations, minimum rest periods between duties, controlled scheduling to minimise circadian disruption, fatigue reporting systems (non-punitive), fatigue education and awareness training, workload management during flights, and the provision of suitable rest facilities. Operational: use autopilot to reduce workload when fatigued, share tasks between crew members, simplify flight planning (choose easier routes/approaches when fatigued), and consider declining or deferring a flight if fatigue risk is unacceptable. The fundamental principle: the only cure for fatigue is sleep. All other measures are temporary mitigations.
exam_tip: Individual: prioritise sleep, naps, strategic caffeine, fitness, honest communication. Organisational: FRMS with duty/rest limits, scheduling, reporting, education. Operational: autopilot, task sharing, simpler plans. Only cure: sleep.
key_facts: Individual: prioritise 7-9 hrs sleep, strategic napping, managed caffeine, fitness, hydration
Organisational: FRMS with duty/rest limits, science-based scheduling, non-punitive reporting
Operational: use autopilot, share tasks, simplify plans, consider declining flights
Fundamental principle: only genuine cure for fatigue is sleep
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms: (a) biological clock.
concept: The biological clock is your body's internal timekeeping mechanism, centred in the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus. It generates approximately 24-hour rhythms in virtually every physiological function: sleep/wake cycles, body temperature, hormone secretion, digestion, alertness, cognitive performance, and immune function. The SCN contains about 20,000 neurons that fire in a rhythmic pattern even without external cues, generating an intrinsic rhythm slightly longer than 24 hours (about 24.2 hours on average). External cues (zeitgebers, particularly light) synchronise this intrinsic rhythm to the exact 24-hour day-night cycle. Without external cues (as in cave experiments), the rhythm free-runs at its intrinsic period, gradually drifting out of sync with the external day. For pilots, the biological clock determines when you're most and least alert, when you sleep best, and how quickly you can adapt to schedule changes. It's the master controller behind circadian rhythm and the reason shift work, jet lag, and irregular schedules are so physiologically disruptive.
exam_tip: Biological clock: SCN in hypothalamus, generates ~24-hour rhythms. Intrinsic period ~24.2 hours, synchronised to 24 hours by light (primary zeitgeber). Controls sleep/wake, temperature, hormones, alertness.
key_facts: Located in the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus
Generates intrinsic rhythm of approximately 24.2 hours
Synchronised to exact 24-hour cycle by external cues, primarily light
Controls rhythms in sleep/wake, body temperature, hormones, alertness, cognition
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms: (b) circadian rhythm.
concept: Circadian rhythm (from Latin 'circa diem' -- about a day) is the approximately 24-hour cycle of physiological and behavioural patterns driven by the biological clock. Body temperature follows a circadian rhythm: lowest around 0400-0600, rising through the morning to peak around 1600-1800, then declining through the evening. Alertness and cognitive performance closely follow the temperature curve--you're sharpest in the late morning and early afternoon, and least alert in the early hours of the morning (the 'window of circadian low,' typically 0200-0600). Cortisol peaks in the early morning (waking you up) and is lowest in the evening. Melatonin rises in the evening (promoting sleep) and drops in the morning. Digestive function, heart rate, blood pressure, and immune function all follow circadian patterns. For pilots, the circadian rhythm explains why night flying is more demanding than day flying (even with adequate prior sleep), why the 0200-0600 window is the most dangerous time for fatigue-related incidents, and why crossing multiple time zones causes jet lag.
exam_tip: Circadian rhythm: ~24-hour cycle. Body temp lowest 0400-0600, peaks 1600-1800. Alertness follows temperature. Circadian low: 0200-0600 (most dangerous for fatigue-related incidents). Melatonin rises evening, cortisol peaks morning.
key_facts: Approximately 24-hour cycle of physiological and behavioural patterns
Body temperature: lowest 0400-0600, peak 1600-1800; alertness follows temperature curve
Circadian low (0200-0600): least alert, most accident-prone period
Cortisol peaks morning (waking); melatonin rises evening (sleep promotion)
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms: (c) circadian dysrhythmia.
concept: Circadian dysrhythmia is the disruption of normal circadian rhythms caused by a mismatch between your internal biological clock and the external environment or your required schedule. The most common form is jet lag: rapid travel across time zones forces your body to function on a schedule that conflicts with your internal clock. Your body temperature, hormone cycles, sleep/wake drive, and alertness patterns are all out of sync with the local time. Other forms include shift-work circadian disruption (working nights, sleeping days) and schedule irregularity (varying start times, mixed day and night operations). Symptoms include: sleep disturbance (insomnia at night, sleepiness during the day), fatigue, digestive problems, reduced concentration and cognitive performance, headache, general malaise, and mood disturbance. Circadian dysrhythmia takes time to resolve: the biological clock adjusts at a rate of about 1-1.5 hours per day for eastward travel and 1-2 hours per day for westward travel. Complete adjustment to a major time zone change can take 5-10 days.
exam_tip: Circadian dysrhythmia: mismatch between internal clock and external schedule. Jet lag, shift work, irregular schedules. Adjustment rate: ~1-1.5 hrs/day eastward, 1-2 hrs/day westward. Full adjustment: 5-10 days for major changes.
key_facts: Disruption from mismatch between internal biological clock and external schedule
Caused by jet lag, shift work, and irregular duty schedules
Symptoms: sleep disturbance, fatigue, digestive problems, cognitive impairment
Clock adjusts ~1-1.5 hrs/day eastward, 1-2 hrs/day westward; full adjustment 5-10 days
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms: (d) desynchronisation.
concept: Desynchronisation refers to the loss of normal alignment between different circadian rhythms within your body. Normally, your body temperature, hormone cycles, sleep/wake patterns, digestive function, and alertness all peak and trough in a coordinated sequence. After a time zone change or shift rotation, these rhythms adjust at different rates--body temperature adjusts relatively slowly, melatonin adjusts moderately, and sleep timing can change quickly (you can force yourself to lie in bed at the 'right' time). The result is internal desynchronisation: your temperature rhythm might still be on NZ time while your sleep pattern has shifted to Australian time, and your digestive system is somewhere in between. This internal misalignment is a major contributor to the malaise of jet lag and shift work--it's not just that one rhythm is off, it's that they're all off by different amounts. Performance is most impaired during the period of maximum internal desynchronisation. Resynchronisation requires consistent exposure to the new time cues (light, meals, social activity) over several days.
exam_tip: Desynchronisation: internal misalignment between different circadian rhythms (temperature, hormones, sleep, digestion) adjusting at different rates. Maximum performance impairment during peak desynchronisation. Resynchronise with consistent time cues.
key_facts: Different circadian rhythms (temperature, hormones, sleep) adjust at different rates
Internal misalignment after time zone or schedule changes
Performance most impaired during peak internal desynchronisation
Resynchronisation requires consistent exposure to new time cues (light, meals, activity)
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms: (e) zeitgeber.
concept: Zeitgeber (German for 'time giver') is an external cue that synchronises your biological clock to the 24-hour day. The primary zeitgeber is light: specialised photoreceptive ganglion cells in the retina detect light levels and signal the SCN to adjust the clock. Bright morning light advances the clock (useful for adjusting to eastward travel), while bright evening light delays it (useful for westward travel). This is why light exposure management is the most powerful tool for managing jet lag and shift work adaptation. Other zeitgebers include: meal timing (eating at regular times reinforces the clock), social interaction and activity patterns, exercise timing, and temperature exposure. Melatonin (both endogenous and supplemental) also acts as a zeitgeber by directly signalling the SCN. For pilots managing circadian disruption, strategic use of zeitgebers accelerates clock adjustment: seeking bright light at the appropriate time, eating meals on the destination schedule, being socially active during the destination's daytime, and taking melatonin at the destination's bedtime.
exam_tip: Zeitgeber: external time cue synchronising the biological clock. Light is the primary zeitgeber. Others: meals, social activity, exercise, temperature, melatonin. Strategic light exposure is the most powerful jet lag management tool.
key_facts: Light is the primary zeitgeber, detected by specialised retinal ganglion cells
Morning light advances the clock; evening light delays it
Other zeitgebers: meal timing, social interaction, exercise, temperature, melatonin
Strategic zeitgeber management accelerates circadian adjustment after time zone changes
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the central human physiological processes underlying circadian rhythm processes.
concept: The circadian system is a hierarchical network with the SCN as the master pacemaker. The SCN receives light information from the retina via the retinohypothalamic tract and uses this to synchronise its intrinsic rhythm. It then coordinates peripheral clocks located in virtually every organ and tissue (liver, heart, lungs, muscles) through neural signals and hormonal outputs. The primary hormonal output is the regulation of melatonin production by the pineal gland: the SCN inhibits melatonin production during daylight hours and releases the inhibition at night, allowing melatonin to rise and promote sleep. Cortisol production by the adrenal glands follows the opposite pattern: high in the morning (cortisol awakening response), declining through the day. Core body temperature is regulated in a circadian pattern by the SCN through the hypothalamus: lowest around 0400-0600, highest around 1600-1800. Growth hormone secretion is linked to deep sleep in the early part of the night. These processes interact to create the coordinated 24-hour cycle that optimises function during the day and promotes recovery during the night.
exam_tip: SCN is master pacemaker; synchronised by light via retinohypothalamic tract. Outputs: melatonin (pineal, rises at night), cortisol (adrenals, peaks morning), body temperature regulation. Peripheral clocks in all organs coordinated by SCN.
key_facts: SCN is master pacemaker, receiving light input via retinohypothalamic tract
Melatonin: pineal gland, inhibited by SCN during day, rises at night to promote sleep
Cortisol: adrenal glands, peaks in morning (awakening response), declines through day
Body temperature: lowest 0400-0600, highest 1600-1800, regulated via hypothalamus
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain how circadian rhythms affect pilot performance.
concept: Circadian rhythms create predictable peaks and troughs in pilot performance across the 24-hour day. Performance is best during the late morning and early afternoon (approximately 0900-1400), coinciding with rising body temperature and cortisol levels. There's a natural dip in the early afternoon (the 'post-lunch dip,' roughly 1300-1500), which is a genuine circadian phenomenon, not just the result of eating lunch. Performance deteriorates through the evening and reaches its lowest point during the circadian low window (0200-0600), when body temperature is lowest, melatonin is highest, and the sleep drive is strongest. During the circadian low, reaction times are 15-30% slower, error rates increase significantly, and the risk of microsleeps is highest. This window accounts for a disproportionate number of fatigue-related aviation incidents. For NZ CPL pilots, this means: night operations during the 0200-0600 window require maximum vigilance, strategic caffeine use, and crew coordination. Early morning starts (0400-0500) mean the first part of the duty day is during the circadian low.
exam_tip: Best performance: 0900-1400. Post-lunch dip: 1300-1500. Circadian low: 0200-0600 (worst performance, most fatigue incidents, 15-30% slower reactions). Early morning starts begin during circadian low.
key_facts: Peak performance approximately 0900-1400 (rising temperature and cortisol)
Post-lunch dip 1300-1500: genuine circadian phenomenon, not just eating
Circadian low 0200-0600: worst performance, highest microsleep and error risk
Reaction times 15-30% slower during circadian low; disproportionate incident rate
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of circadian dysrhythmia and methods of managing these.
concept: Circadian dysrhythmia (jet lag, shift-work disruption) produces measurable performance decrements that need active management. Effects: sleep disturbance (difficulty sleeping at the 'right' time), daytime sleepiness, cognitive impairment (slower processing, worse decision-making), digestive problems, general malaise, and mood disturbance. These effects are worse for eastward travel (which requires advancing the clock, which is harder than delaying it) and for crossing more time zones. Management strategies: pre-trip adjustment (gradually shift your sleep/wake time toward the destination schedule before departure), strategic light exposure at the destination (bright light in the morning to advance the clock, avoiding light in the evening; reverse for westward), melatonin supplementation at the destination bedtime, maintain hydration, avoid alcohol and heavy meals, exercise moderately during local daytime, and accept that full adjustment takes days. For NZ pilots doing Trans-Tasman operations (only 2-3 hours time difference to Australia), the disruption is mild. For longer routes (to Asia, Pacific Islands), more aggressive management is needed.
exam_tip: Management: pre-trip schedule adjustment, strategic light exposure, melatonin at destination bedtime, hydrate, avoid alcohol. Eastward travel harder (advance clock). NZ Trans-Tasman: mild disruption. Longer routes: aggressive management needed.
key_facts: Effects: sleep disturbance, daytime sleepiness, cognitive impairment, digestive problems
Eastward travel harder (clock advance) than westward (clock delay)
Pre-trip adjustment, strategic light exposure, melatonin, hydration, exercise
Full adjustment takes days; NZ-Australia mild (2-3 hrs); longer routes need more management
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the impact of shiftwork on a pilot's performance.
concept: Shift work forces pilots to work during circadian low periods and sleep during circadian high periods, creating a chronic mismatch between the biological clock and the duty schedule. Night shifts are the most challenging: performance during the 0200-0600 window is at its circadian lowest regardless of prior sleep, and daytime sleep after a night shift is shorter and less restorative. Rotating shifts (alternating between day and night duties) are particularly disruptive because the biological clock never fully adjusts to either schedule. Early morning shifts (reporting before 0600) cut into the natural sleep period, reducing total sleep time. The cumulative effect of shift work is chronic fatigue, chronic sleep debt, and increased health risks (cardiovascular disease, metabolic disorders, gastrointestinal problems, and mental health issues). For NZ CPL pilots, irregular schedules are common: dawn freight runs, evening charter returns, next-day turnarounds--all creating the shift-work pattern of circadian disruption.
exam_tip: Night shifts: performance at circadian lowest 0200-0600 regardless of prior sleep. Rotating shifts worst: clock never adjusts. Early starts cut into natural sleep. Chronic effects: fatigue, sleep debt, health risks.
key_facts: Night shift performance at circadian low (0200-0600) regardless of prior sleep quality
Rotating shifts most disruptive: biological clock never fully adjusts
Daytime sleep after night shift: shorter, lighter, less restorative
Chronic shift work: increased cardiovascular, metabolic, GI, and mental health risks
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe how the biological effects of shiftwork can be minimised.
concept: Minimising the biological effects of shift work requires strategies at both individual and organisational levels. Individual: maintain consistent sleep/wake times where possible (even on days off), create a good sleep environment (dark, quiet, cool), use strategic napping before and during night shifts, manage caffeine use (effective early in the shift, avoid late to protect post-shift sleep), maintain regular exercise and healthy eating patterns, limit alcohol use (disrupts sleep), and use bright light strategically (bright light during the first half of a night shift promotes alertness; avoiding bright light before sleep aids sleep onset). Organisational: design rosters that rotate forward (mornings > afternoons > nights rather than the reverse), provide minimum rest periods of at least 12 hours between shifts (longer before night shifts), limit consecutive night shifts, provide appropriate rest facilities, implement fatigue reporting systems, and avoid scheduling critical tasks during the circadian low period where possible. The reality is that shift work can never be made completely harmless--the goal is to minimise the damage and manage the risk.
exam_tip: Individual: consistent sleep times, good sleep environment, strategic napping, managed caffeine, bright light timing. Organisational: forward rotation, minimum 12hr rest, limit consecutive nights, FRMS. Shift work can't be eliminated -- manage the risk.
key_facts: Maintain consistent sleep/wake times; create dark, quiet, cool sleep environment
Strategic napping before/during night shifts; caffeine early, not late in shift
Organisational: forward roster rotation, minimum 12hr rest, limit consecutive nights
Bright light early in night shift for alertness; avoid bright light before sleep
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Identify the principles of good rostering practice.
concept: Good rostering practices minimise fatigue risk while maintaining operational requirements. Key principles: provide adequate rest periods between duties (minimum 10-12 hours, with at least 8 hours of sleep opportunity); limit total duty time per day, week, and month; limit consecutive early starts or night duties; rotate schedules forward (day > evening > night) rather than backward; provide at least 2 consecutive days off per week for recovery; avoid scheduling demanding tasks (check flights, training, unfamiliar operations) during circadian low periods; consider cumulative fatigue--don't just look at individual duty periods in isolation; build in buffer for delays and extensions; account for commuting time when assessing total wakefulness; and apply stricter limits for single-pilot operations (no crew to share the load). In NZ, flight and duty time limitations are prescribed in CAR Part 135 (for air transport) and advisory circulars. Operators should supplement these with FRMS principles that account for the scientific understanding of fatigue, not just regulatory compliance.
exam_tip: Principles: adequate rest (10-12 hrs between duties), limit duty hours, forward rotation, 2+ consecutive days off weekly, avoid demanding tasks during circadian low, account for cumulative fatigue, stricter for single-pilot. NZ: CAR Part 135 limits + FRMS.
key_facts: Adequate rest periods: minimum 10-12 hours with 8+ hours sleep opportunity
Forward roster rotation (day > evening > night); limit consecutive nights
At least 2 consecutive days off per week for recovery
Account for cumulative fatigue, commuting time, and apply stricter limits for single-pilot
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, NZ CARs Part 135, ICAO FRMS guidance

syllabus_desc: Describe methods by which age-related changes in memory and speed of information processing can be moderated by older pilots.
concept: Ageing brings measurable changes in cognitive function: processing speed slows, working memory capacity decreases, multitasking ability declines, and learning new information takes longer. However, these changes can be significantly moderated. Maintained knowledge and experience: older pilots have extensive procedural knowledge, pattern recognition, and judgement that partially compensates for slower processing. Continued practice: 'use it or lose it' applies--regular flying, study, and mental exercise slow cognitive decline. Physical fitness: cardiovascular fitness directly supports brain function; regular exercise improves blood flow to the brain, maintaining cognitive performance. Mental fitness: continued learning, crossword puzzles, reading, and varied mental activities maintain neural plasticity. Standardised procedures: consistent use of checklists, SOPs, and standardised techniques reduces reliance on working memory. Technology aids: flight management systems, autopilot, GPS, and electronic checklists reduce cognitive workload. Health management: controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and diabetes reduces vascular damage to the brain. Social engagement: remaining professionally and socially active maintains cognitive sharpness. The key is honest self-assessment--recognising when compensation strategies are no longer sufficient.
exam_tip: Age-related declines: processing speed, working memory, multitasking. Moderated by: continued practice, physical/mental fitness, standardised procedures, technology aids, health management. Experience compensates partly but honest self-assessment is essential.
key_facts: Processing speed, working memory, and multitasking decline with age
Experience, judgement, and pattern recognition partially compensate
Physical fitness (cardiovascular) directly supports brain function and cognitive performance
Standardised procedures and technology aids reduce reliance on declining working memory
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe what changes would indicate early dementia or age related cognitive impairment in another pilot.
concept: Recognising early cognitive impairment in a colleague is important for safety but requires sensitivity. Warning signs include: increasingly forgetting procedures or checklist items that were previously automatic, difficulty adapting to new procedures or equipment, repeatedly making the same errors, getting lost or confused on familiar routes or in familiar airspace, difficulty with radio communication (missing calls, garbled responses, inability to copy clearances), personality changes (increased irritability, social withdrawal, inappropriate behaviour), declining standards of personal grooming or aircraft care, difficulty with basic calculations (fuel planning, weight and balance), confusion about time, date, or schedule, and a noticeable decline in flight performance that others observe but the pilot doesn't acknowledge. The key differentiator from normal ageing: these changes are progressive, affect daily functioning, represent a decline from the individual's previous performance level, and the pilot may not be aware of them (lack of insight). If you notice these signs in a colleague, the responsible action is to raise the concern--initially with the pilot themselves, then with management or the CAA if necessary. This is about safety, not judgement.
exam_tip: Warning signs: forgetting procedures, difficulty with new learning, repeated errors, getting lost on familiar routes, personality changes, declining standards, lack of insight into own decline. Raise concerns responsibly -- safety over politeness.
key_facts: Forgetting previously automatic procedures; difficulty adapting to new procedures/equipment
Repeated errors, confusion on familiar routes, difficulty with radio communication
Personality changes, declining standards, and lack of insight into own decline
Progressive deterioration distinguishes from normal ageing; raise concerns responsibly for safety
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Identify the human sensors pilots depend on for information acquisition.
concept: Pilots depend on multiple sensory channels to acquire the information needed for safe flight. Vision is dominant, providing approximately 80% of flight-relevant information: reading instruments, scanning for traffic, assessing weather, judging approaches, and interpreting visual navigation references. Hearing provides critical information through radio communications, engine sound (experienced pilots detect subtle engine anomalies by ear), aural alerts and warnings, and wind noise changes. The vestibular system (inner ear) provides motion and orientation information--though in flight this can be misleading. Proprioception (touch, pressure) provides control feel, G-force awareness, and vibration detection. Olfaction (smell) can detect fire, electrical burning, fuel leaks, oil leaks, or cabin contamination. For commercial operations, the pilot must actively manage these sensory channels: prioritising visual scan, listening through cockpit noise, cross-checking vestibular sensations against instruments, and being alert to unexpected smells or sensations. Overloading any single channel (e.g., spending too long heads-down on instruments) means starving other channels of attention.
exam_tip: Sensors: vision (~80%), hearing (comms, engine, alerts), vestibular (motion/orientation -- can mislead), proprioception (control feel, G-force), olfaction (fire, fuel, oil). Must manage and balance attention across channels.
key_facts: Vision provides ~80% of flight information: instruments, traffic, weather, navigation
Hearing: radio comms, engine sound, aural alerts and warnings
Vestibular: motion and orientation (can be misleading in flight)
Proprioception: control feel, G-force; Olfaction: fire, electrical, fuel/oil leaks
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the brain's role in registering sensations, processing sensory information, storing information and controlling actions.
concept: The brain processes information through a series of stages. First, sensory registration: raw data from the senses arrives at the sensory cortices (visual cortex, auditory cortex, etc.) where it's initially registered. Not all incoming information is processed further--most is filtered out as irrelevant (you don't consciously process every instrument reading every second). Second, perception: the brain interprets and organises the registered sensations into meaningful patterns--recognising an instrument reading as 'too high,' identifying a sound as 'ATC calling your callsign,' or detecting that a smell is 'burning electrical.' This stage involves comparison with stored knowledge and expectations. Third, decision-making: based on the perceived information, the brain evaluates options, selects a response, and plans the action using working memory and long-term knowledge. Fourth, response execution: motor commands are sent to muscles to execute the chosen action (moving a control, pressing a button, keying the mic). This entire process takes time--typically 0.5-1.5 seconds for a simple response, longer for complex decisions. Each stage can be a source of error: misperception, misinterpretation, wrong decision, or wrong action.
exam_tip: Four stages: sensory registration > perception (interpretation) > decision-making > response execution. Total processing time: 0.5-1.5 seconds for simple responses, longer for complex. Each stage is a potential error source.
key_facts: Sensory registration: raw data filtered -- most discarded as irrelevant
Perception: brain interprets data into meaningful patterns using stored knowledge
Decision-making: evaluate options, select response, plan action (working memory + LTM)
Response execution: motor commands to muscles; total process 0.5-1.5 seconds minimum
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe a basic model of information processing, including the concepts of: (a) attention.
concept: Attention is the process of focusing cognitive resources on specific information while filtering out other inputs. It's a limited resource--you can't attend to everything at once. In aviation, attention determines what information you process from the vast amount available: which instruments you read, which radio calls you register, what your eyes fixate on during a scan. Attention acts like a spotlight: whatever falls within the beam is processed deeply, while everything outside receives only superficial processing. The key limitation is that the spotlight can only illuminate one area at a time--though you can shift it rapidly. Factors affecting attention: interest (you attend more to what matters to you), novelty (unusual stimuli attract attention automatically), expectation (you attend to what you expect to see--which can cause you to miss the unexpected), workload (high workload narrows attention), and fatigue (reduces the ability to control attention effectively). For commercial pilots, managing attention is a core skill: knowing when to focus narrowly (final approach) and when to scan broadly (cruise scanning for traffic).
exam_tip: Attention is a limited resource (spotlight model). You can't attend to everything simultaneously. Affected by: interest, novelty, expectation, workload, fatigue. Core pilot skill: managing attention allocation.
key_facts: Attention is a limited cognitive resource -- cannot process all inputs simultaneously
Acts like a spotlight: focused area deeply processed, periphery receives superficial processing
Factors: interest, novelty, expectation, workload, and fatigue all affect attention allocation
Managing attention allocation is a core commercial pilot skill
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe a basic model of information processing, including the concepts of: (b) divided attention.
concept: Divided attention is the ability to attend to more than one task or information source simultaneously. In aviation, you're constantly dividing attention: scanning instruments while listening to ATC, flying the approach while briefing the procedure, monitoring the engine while navigating. True simultaneous processing of multiple tasks is extremely limited--what we call 'multi-tasking' is usually rapid time-sharing (switching attention back and forth quickly). The ability to divide attention depends on: the complexity of each task (simple tasks can be time-shared more easily than complex ones), the degree of practice (well-practised tasks require less attention, freeing capacity for other tasks), whether the tasks use different sensory channels (you can listen and look simultaneously more easily than listen to two conversations), and your current capacity (reduced by fatigue, stress, or workload). When task demands exceed your attention capacity, something gets dropped--and the thing that gets dropped is often the thing that wasn't prioritised, which may be the most safety-critical item.
exam_tip: Divided attention: actually rapid time-sharing, not true simultaneous processing. Depends on: task complexity, practice level, different sensory channels, and current capacity. When overloaded, tasks get dropped -- often safety-critical ones.
key_facts: Usually rapid attention switching (time-sharing), not true simultaneous processing
Easier when tasks are simple, well-practised, and use different sensory channels
Capacity reduced by fatigue, stress, and high workload
Overload causes task shedding -- dropped tasks may be safety-critical
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe a basic model of information processing, including the concepts of: (c) selective attention.
concept: Selective attention is the ability to focus on relevant information while filtering out irrelevant distractions. It's essential in the noisy, information-rich cockpit environment where you need to pick out your callsign from multiple radio transmissions, read the relevant instrument from a panel of many, or spot an aircraft against a cluttered visual background. The 'cocktail party effect' is a good example: at a noisy gathering, you can tune into one conversation and filter out others--but you'll still notice if someone says your name across the room. In aviation, selective attention allows you to monitor ATC frequency while focusing on flying, or to concentrate on an approach procedure while remaining aware of a critical engine gauge. The limitation of selective attention is that it works by filtering OUT information--and sometimes the filtered information was important. Selective attention is vulnerable to confirmation bias (attending to information that confirms your expectation while filtering out contradictory data) and to extreme stress (where attention becomes involuntarily selective--fixating on one thing to the exclusion of all else).
exam_tip: Selective attention: focus on relevant, filter out irrelevant. Essential in noisy cockpit. Limitations: filtered information might be important; vulnerable to confirmation bias; stress causes involuntary fixation.
key_facts: Ability to focus on relevant information while filtering out distractions
Essential in the information-rich cockpit environment (multiple radio calls, instruments)
Works by filtering OUT information -- sometimes filtering out important data
Vulnerable to confirmation bias and stress-induced involuntary fixation
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe a basic model of information processing, including the concepts of: (d) attention getting stimulus.
concept: An attention-getting stimulus is any input that captures your attention involuntarily, overriding your current focus.
