These stimuli are typically: novel or unexpected (a fire warning bell you've never heard in flight before), intense (a loud bang, bright flash, strong smell), personally relevant (your callsign on the radio), moving (an aircraft suddenly appearing in your peripheral vision), or biologically significant (the smell of smoke). The aviation industry deliberately designs warning systems to exploit these characteristics: master caution lights are bright and centrally located, fire warnings are loud and distinct, stall warnings use a stick shaker (physical vibration--a different sensory channel). However, too many attention-getting stimuli simultaneously can be overwhelming--a cascade of alerts during an emergency can cause cognitive overload, where the pilot is unable to process and prioritise the information. This is why warning system design includes hierarchy (warning > caution > advisory) and inhibition logic (some alerts suppressed when higher-priority alerts are active). Understanding what captures your attention helps you design your cockpit monitoring to catch what matters.
exam_tip: Attention-getting stimuli: novel, intense, personally relevant, moving, biologically significant. Aviation warnings designed to exploit these. But too many simultaneous alerts cause overload. Warning hierarchy: warning > caution > advisory.
key_facts: Stimuli that involuntarily capture attention: novel, intense, relevant, moving, biologically significant
Aviation warnings designed to use these properties (bright, loud, distinct, vibrating)
Too many simultaneous alerts cause cognitive overload and inability to prioritise
Warning hierarchy (warning > caution > advisory) and inhibition logic prevent cascade overload
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe a basic model of information processing, including the concepts of: (e) sensory threshold.
concept: Sensory threshold is the minimum intensity of a stimulus required for detection. The absolute threshold is the weakest stimulus you can detect at all (e.g., the dimmest light you can see in total darkness). The difference threshold (just noticeable difference, JND) is the smallest change in a stimulus that you can detect (e.g., the smallest altitude change you can read on an altimeter). In aviation, sensory thresholds are important because: instrument displays must be designed to present information above detection thresholds (readable in all lighting conditions), warnings must be loud/bright enough to be detected above cockpit noise/ambient light, and subtle changes in engine sound, vibration, or control feel may be below threshold and go unnoticed. Thresholds are not fixed--they're affected by: alertness (fatigue raises thresholds, making you less sensitive), workload (high workload raises thresholds for non-primary tasks), ambient conditions (background noise raises the hearing threshold), and expectation (you're more sensitive to stimuli you're expecting). For pilots, maintaining awareness that some changes may be below your detection threshold reinforces the importance of instrument crosscheck and systematic monitoring.
exam_tip: Absolute threshold: minimum detectable stimulus. JND: minimum detectable change. Thresholds raised by fatigue, workload, ambient conditions. Instruments and warnings must exceed thresholds. Some changes may be below detection -- crosscheck essential.
key_facts: Absolute threshold: minimum stimulus intensity for detection
Difference threshold (JND): smallest detectable change in a stimulus
Thresholds raised by fatigue, high workload, and ambient conditions (noise, light)
Some flight parameter changes may be below threshold -- systematic crosscheck essential
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe a basic model of information processing, including the concepts of: (f) sensitivity.
concept: Sensitivity refers to how responsive your sensory systems are to stimulation--how well you detect and discriminate stimuli. High sensitivity means you detect weak stimuli and small differences; low sensitivity means you need stronger stimuli to register them. Sensitivity varies across your sensory systems and is affected by multiple factors. Visual sensitivity: highest in the fovea for detail, in the peripheral retina for dim light; reduced by hypoxia, fatigue, and poor dark adaptation. Auditory sensitivity: highest in the 1,000-4,000 Hz range (speech frequencies); reduced by noise-induced hearing loss, age, and ambient noise. Vestibular sensitivity: has specific detection thresholds for angular and linear acceleration (below which movements go undetected). Sensitivity can be enhanced by training--an experienced pilot is more sensitive to subtle changes in engine sound or control feel than a student. It can also be enhanced by alertness and expectation (if you're expecting a change, you're more likely to detect it). The practical implications: maintaining health, rest, and sensory organ protection (hearing protection, eye care, dark adaptation) directly maintains the sensitivity you need for safe flying.
exam_tip: Sensitivity: responsiveness of sensory systems. Varies by condition: hypoxia, fatigue, noise damage all reduce it. Enhanced by training, alertness, and expectation. Maintain health and sensory protection to preserve sensitivity.
key_facts: High sensitivity: detect weak stimuli and small differences; varies by sensory system
Visual: reduced by hypoxia, fatigue, poor dark adaptation. Auditory: reduced by NIHL, age, noise
Enhanced by training (experienced pilots detect subtle changes), alertness, and expectation
Maintain sensitivity through health, rest, and sensory protection (hearing, vision)
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe a basic model of information processing, including the concepts of: (g) adaptation.
concept: Sensory adaptation is the process by which your sensory systems become less responsive to a constant or repeated stimulus over time. You stop noticing things that don't change. Examples in aviation: you stop hearing the constant engine noise after a few minutes (auditory adaptation), you stop feeling the vibration of the seat (tactile adaptation), you stop noticing a persistent but constant smell in the cockpit. Adaptation is generally useful--it prevents sensory overload from unchanging stimuli, freeing your attention for changes and new information. However, it can be dangerous: if you adapt to a slowly developing abnormal condition (a gradual oil smell, a slowly increasing vibration, a very gradual heading drift), you may not detect it because the change is too slow for your adapted sensory system to register. Visual adaptation includes both dark adaptation (increasing sensitivity in darkness) and light adaptation (decreasing sensitivity in bright light). For pilots, understanding adaptation explains why fresh eyes (a relief pilot or second crew member) may notice something the current pilot has adapted to and no longer detects.
exam_tip: Adaptation: sensory systems become less responsive to constant stimuli. Useful (prevents overload) but dangerous (may not detect gradually developing abnormalities). Fresh eyes (relief pilot) may notice what adapted pilot misses.
key_facts: Sensory systems become less responsive to constant/repeated stimuli over time
Useful: prevents overload from unchanging inputs, freeing attention for changes
Dangerous: may not detect slowly developing abnormal conditions
Fresh crew member may notice what adapted pilot has stopped detecting
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe a basic model of information processing, including the concepts of: (h) habituation.
concept: Habituation is a learning process where you stop responding to a stimulus that has been repeatedly presented without consequence. It's different from adaptation (which is sensory) because habituation is a cognitive/behavioural response. In aviation: if a master caution light illuminates frequently for a known, benign reason and you repeatedly cancel it without any adverse consequence, you become habituated to it--you stop treating it as meaningful. This is extremely dangerous because the next time it illuminates, it may be for a genuinely critical reason, but your habituated response is to dismiss and cancel rather than investigate. Similarly, repeated false fire warnings can habituate a crew to ignore the warning, with potentially catastrophic consequences when a real fire occurs. The 'cry wolf' effect is habituation in action. Habituation also explains why routine safety briefings lose their impact on frequent flyers, why complacency develops with repetitive operations, and why checklists that always show 'normal' results stop being read carefully. Combating habituation requires: varying procedures slightly, investigating every alert properly, and maintaining conscious discipline in routine tasks.
exam_tip: Habituation: stop responding to repeated stimuli without consequence (cognitive, not sensory). Dangerous: habituated to frequent alerts, may dismiss genuine emergency. 'Cry wolf' effect. Combat: investigate every alert, vary routines, maintain discipline.
key_facts: Cognitive/behavioural process: stop responding to repeated stimuli without consequence
Different from adaptation (sensory) -- habituation is learned non-response
Dangerous: habituated pilots may dismiss genuine alerts ('cry wolf' effect)
Combat with: investigating every alert, varying routines, maintaining conscious discipline
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the following types of memory: (a) peripheral/sensory memory.
concept: Peripheral or sensory memory is the initial, ultra-brief recording of sensory information. It holds a large amount of raw sensory data for a very short time--iconic memory (visual) lasts about 0.5-1 second, echoic memory (auditory) lasts about 3-4 seconds. This gives your brain a brief buffer to decide what's worth processing further. In the cockpit, sensory memory is what allows you to 'replay' the last few seconds of an ATC instruction you weren't fully attending to--you heard it, it's briefly stored in echoic memory, and you can recall it if you act quickly. Similarly, a brief glance at the instrument panel is held in iconic memory long enough for your brain to detect if anything looks abnormal. However, if you don't transfer the information to working memory (by paying attention to it), it's lost within seconds. The limitations of sensory memory mean that if you're distracted at the moment an ATC call comes in, you've got about 3-4 seconds to 'grab' it from echoic memory before it fades permanently.
exam_tip: Sensory memory: ultra-brief buffer. Iconic (visual): ~0.5-1 second. Echoic (auditory): ~3-4 seconds. Allows brief 'replay' but information is lost if not transferred to working memory within seconds.
key_facts: Ultra-brief recording of raw sensory data (large capacity, very short duration)
Iconic (visual) memory: approximately 0.5-1 second duration
Echoic (auditory) memory: approximately 3-4 seconds duration
Information lost permanently if not transferred to working memory through attention
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the following types of memory: (b) short term/working memory.
concept: Working memory (short-term memory) is your mental workspace--where you hold and manipulate information you're actively using. It has severely limited capacity: approximately 7 plus or minus 2 items (Miller's magic number), and information persists for only about 20-30 seconds without rehearsal. In the cockpit, working memory is what you use to hold an ATC clearance while you read it back, calculate a heading change, or remember the sequence of actions in a procedure. The limited capacity means you can be easily overloaded: a complex ATC instruction with multiple elements (new heading, altitude, speed, squawk code) may exceed your working memory capacity, causing errors or omissions. Working memory is also the bottleneck in multi-tasking--you can only consciously process one thing at a time in working memory, which is why simultaneous demands from ATC, an autopilot disconnect, and an engine warning are so overwhelming. Working memory capacity is reduced by stress, fatigue, hypoxia, and high emotional arousal. Strategies to manage limited working memory: write things down, use checklists, chunk information into meaningful groups, and prioritise what needs active processing.
exam_tip: Working memory: ~7 plus/minus 2 items, ~20-30 seconds without rehearsal. The bottleneck for cockpit multi-tasking. Reduced by stress, fatigue, hypoxia. Manage: write things down, checklists, chunking, prioritise.
key_facts: Capacity: approximately 7 plus or minus 2 items; duration: ~20-30 seconds without rehearsal
The bottleneck in multi-tasking: can only consciously process one thing at a time
Capacity reduced by stress, fatigue, hypoxia, and emotional arousal
Manage with: writing things down, checklists, chunking, prioritisation
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the following types of memory: (c) long term memory.
concept: Long-term memory (LTM) is your permanent storage system with essentially unlimited capacity and no practical time limit. It stores everything from facts and concepts (semantic memory) to personal experiences (episodic memory) to learned skills (procedural memory). In aviation, LTM holds your knowledge base: aerodynamic principles, regulations, procedures, aircraft systems knowledge, meteorology, and navigation. It also stores your flying skills, learned through hundreds of hours of practice. Information transfers from working memory to LTM through encoding processes including: repetition (practice), meaningful association (connecting new information to existing knowledge), elaboration (thinking deeply about the material), and emotional significance (stressful events are strongly encoded). Retrieval from LTM is not always reliable--you can 'know' something but be unable to recall it under pressure (tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon). Retrieval is aided by cues: context, associations, mnemonics. For exam preparation, understanding how LTM encoding works helps you study more effectively: active learning, spaced repetition, and relating concepts to real flying experience all strengthen LTM storage.
exam_tip: LTM: essentially unlimited capacity and duration. Stores facts (semantic), experiences (episodic), and skills (procedural). Encoding strengthened by: repetition, meaningful association, elaboration, emotional significance. Retrieval: aided by cues, context, mnemonics.
key_facts: Unlimited capacity and duration; stores facts, experiences, and skills
Three types: semantic (facts/concepts), episodic (personal experiences), procedural (skills)
Encoding strengthened by repetition, meaningful association, elaboration, and emotion
Retrieval aided by cues and context; can fail under pressure (tip-of-the-tongue)
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the following types of memory: (d) motor/skills memory.
concept: Motor or skills memory (procedural memory) is the type of long-term memory that stores learned motor sequences and procedures. Once a skill is thoroughly learned, it becomes largely automatic--you can perform it without conscious thought, freeing working memory for other tasks. This is the basis of proficiency in flying: a skilled pilot performs basic aircraft handling, radio procedures, and standard checks automatically, leaving cognitive capacity for higher-level tasks like decision-making and situational awareness. Motor memory is remarkably durable: even after extended breaks from flying, basic skills return relatively quickly. However, this automaticity has a downside: errors in automated procedures are hard to detect because the action is performed without conscious monitoring. You might lower the gear out of habit on an aircraft that requires manual gear operation, or miss a checklist item because your hands performed the 'usual' sequence without your brain verifying it. Under extreme stress, there's a tendency to revert to earlier-learned motor patterns (skill regression), which can be problematic if you've recently transitioned to a new aircraft type.
exam_tip: Motor/skills memory: learned procedures become automatic, freeing working memory. Very durable but: errors in automatic procedures hard to detect, and stress can cause skill regression to earlier-learned patterns.
key_facts: Stores learned motor sequences; well-practised skills become automatic
Automaticity frees working memory for higher-level tasks (decision-making, SA)
Downside: errors in automatic procedures difficult to detect
Stress causes skill regression: reverting to earlier-learned (potentially wrong) patterns
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the following types of memory: (e) semantic memory.
concept: Semantic memory stores general knowledge, facts, concepts, and meanings that are not tied to a specific personal experience. Your aviation knowledge base is largely semantic memory: the principles of aerodynamics, the gas laws, NZ CARs, weather patterns, aircraft systems theory, navigation procedures. Semantic memory allows you to know that 'stalling speed increases with bank angle' without needing to remember the specific lesson where you learned it. It's organised in networks of related concepts, which is why studying by connecting ideas to existing knowledge is more effective than rote memorisation. Semantic memory is relatively robust--core factual knowledge resists forgetting better than episodic details. However, it can be incorrect if you encoded wrong information initially (a persistent misconception) or if information has changed since you learned it (updated regulations). For CPL pilots, maintaining current semantic memory requires regular study, recurrent training, and keeping up with regulatory changes and airworthiness directives. KDR refresher work is essentially about refreshing and correcting semantic memory.
exam_tip: Semantic memory: general knowledge and facts (not tied to personal experience). Your aviation knowledge base. Organised in associative networks. Can be incorrect if original encoding was wrong. Needs regular refreshing.
key_facts: Stores general knowledge, facts, and concepts not tied to specific experiences
Your aviation knowledge base: principles, regulations, systems, procedures
Organised in associative networks; learning by connection is more effective than rote
Can contain errors from incorrect initial encoding; needs regular updating and refreshing
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the following types of memory: (f) episodic memory.
concept: Episodic memory stores personal experiences--events that happened to you at a specific time and place. In aviation, episodic memories include: your first solo, that time you got caught in unexpected icing near Kaikoura, the go-around you did at Queenstown in gusty conditions, the emergency you witnessed during training. Episodic memories are rich in context (who was there, what the weather was like, how you felt) and serve as a personal reference library for decision-making. When you encounter a situation similar to a past experience, episodic memory provides a rapid template for how to respond. This is part of what makes experienced pilots better decision-makers--they have a larger library of episodic memories to draw on. However, episodic memory has limitations: it's subject to distortion over time (details change in your memory), it can create unhelpful biases (one bad experience may make you overly cautious in similar situations, or one lucky escape may make you overly confident), and it's inherently limited to YOUR experience rather than the full range of possible scenarios. Incident reports and case studies supplement your personal episodic library with others' experiences.
exam_tip: Episodic memory: personal experiences at specific times and places. Provides decision-making templates from experience. Limitations: distortion over time, bias from individual experiences, limited to personal exposure. Supplement with incident reports.
key_facts: Stores personal experiences with contextual detail (who, where, when, how it felt)
Provides rapid response templates for similar future situations
Subject to distortion over time and can create unhelpful biases
Limited to personal experience; supplement with incident reports and case studies
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations of memory.
concept: Memory is far less reliable than most people believe, and understanding its limitations is critical for safe commercial operations. Working memory limitations: capacity of ~7 items, duration of ~20-30 seconds, and vulnerability to displacement (new information pushes out old). This is why you might forget an ATC instruction while dealing with an approach task. Encoding limitations: not all information entering working memory is transferred to LTM. Stress, distraction, and fatigue all impair encoding. Retrieval limitations: you may know something but be unable to recall it when needed (retrieval failure), especially under pressure. Interference: similar information gets confused (proactive interference--old learning interferes with new; retroactive interference--new learning interferes with old). False memories: your brain can construct memories of events that didn't happen, or merge details from different experiences. Prospective memory failure: forgetting to do something you intended to do (like completing a checklist after being interrupted). This is one of the most common error types in aviation. The practical response to memory limitations: use written records, checklists, and SOPs rather than relying on memory. 'If it's not written down, it didn't happen.'
exam_tip: Key limitations: WM capacity (~7 items), encoding failures (stress/distraction), retrieval failures under pressure, interference, false memories, and prospective memory failure (forgetting intentions after interruption). Solution: write it down, use checklists.
key_facts: Working memory: ~7 items, ~20-30 seconds, vulnerable to displacement
Encoding impaired by stress, distraction, and fatigue
Retrieval fails under pressure; similar information interferes
Prospective memory failure (forgetting intentions after interruption) is a major aviation error type
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the following methods of retaining and retrieving information from memory: (a) chunking.
concept: Chunking is a technique for overcoming working memory's limited capacity by grouping individual items into larger meaningful units. Instead of trying to remember 9 individual digits (e.g., 1-1-8-0-6-5-3-5-0), you chunk them into meaningful groups (118-065-350, or perhaps recognise 118.065 as a frequency and 350 as a heading). Each chunk occupies one 'slot' in working memory, but can contain multiple items. Aviation is full of natural chunks: squawk codes are usually remembered as two 2-digit pairs (4-5, 2-3), radio frequencies as two groups (1-1-8 point 0-6-5), and ATC clearances are chunked by type (heading, altitude, speed). The more meaningful the chunks, the easier they are to remember. Experienced pilots chunk more effectively because they recognise patterns: 'descend FL150, track direct TOPIS, expect RNAV approach runway 03' is three meaningful chunks for an experienced pilot but might be overwhelming for a student. Chunking is a natural process that improves with experience, but you can deliberately apply it to manage complex information.
exam_tip: Chunking: grouping individual items into larger meaningful units to overcome WM capacity limits. Aviation natural chunks: frequencies, squawk codes, ATC clearances. Experienced pilots chunk more effectively through pattern recognition.
key_facts: Groups individual items into meaningful units to overcome ~7-item WM limit
Each chunk occupies one WM slot but can contain multiple items
Aviation natural chunks: frequencies, squawk codes, clearance elements
Effectiveness improves with experience and pattern recognition
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the following methods of retaining and retrieving information from memory: (b) mnemonics.
concept: Mnemonics are memory aids that create artificial associations to help encode and retrieve information. In aviation, mnemonics are everywhere: IMSAFE for fitness to fly, HASELL for aerobatic/stall checks, BUMFICH for pre-landing checks, ANC (Aviate Navigate Communicate) for priority management, DECIDE for decision-making, and countless others. They work by providing a structured retrieval cue: when you think of the mnemonic word or acronym, each letter triggers the associated item. Mnemonics are most effective when: the mnemonic word or phrase is easy to remember, each letter or element has a clear and unambiguous association, they're practised until automatic, and they're relevant to the user's context. Limitations: mnemonics can give a false sense of security (you remember the mnemonic but not necessarily the full meaning behind each element), they can become outdated if procedures change, and they work best for sequential procedures rather than understanding concepts. For CPL preparation, mnemonics are valuable for checklists and procedures, but they supplement rather than replace genuine understanding.
exam_tip: Mnemonics: artificial associations for encoding and retrieval. Aviation examples: IMSAFE, HASELL, BUMFICH, ANC, DECIDE. Most effective when practised until automatic. Limitation: can be recalled without full understanding of each element.
key_facts: Artificial associations creating structured retrieval cues for information
Aviation examples: IMSAFE, HASELL, BUMFICH, ANC, DECIDE, and many more
Most effective when practised until automatic and each element clearly understood
Limitations: false security, can become outdated, supplement rather than replace understanding
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the following methods of retaining and retrieving information from memory: (c) associations (verbal and visual).
concept: Associations link new information to existing knowledge, making it easier to encode and retrieve. Verbal associations connect new information to words, phrases, or verbal descriptions you already know. For example, remembering that 'high to low, look out below' links pressure/temperature changes to altimeter behaviour. Visual associations create mental images: imagining the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve as a 'ski jump' shape helps you remember it's sigmoid. The more vivid, unusual, or personally meaningful the association, the stronger the memory. Connecting abstract concepts to concrete cockpit experiences creates powerful associations: learning about the somatogravic illusion is abstract, but associating it with 'that go-around at Queenstown where I felt like the nose was way up' makes it memorable. Cross-referencing between verbal and visual associations is even more effective: if you can both describe and picture a concept, you've encoded it twice. For CPL study, actively creating associations between textbook concepts and your flying experience dramatically improves retention compared to passive reading.
exam_tip: Associations: link new information to existing knowledge. Verbal (phrases, rhymes) and visual (mental images, diagrams). More vivid and personally relevant = stronger memory. Cross-referencing verbal + visual is most effective.
key_facts: Verbal: connecting new info to known words, phrases, sayings (e.g., 'high to low, look out below')
Visual: creating mental images or diagrams linked to concepts
More vivid, unusual, and personally meaningful associations create stronger memories
Cross-referencing verbal and visual associations provides dual encoding for better retention
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the following methods of retaining and retrieving information from memory: (d) checklists.
concept: Checklists are the most important external memory aid in aviation. They compensate for the fundamental unreliability of human memory by providing a structured, written sequence of items to verify or actions to perform. Checklists address prospective memory failure (forgetting to do intended tasks), working memory limitations (holding complex sequences), and the effects of stress, fatigue, and distraction on memory. There are two types: 'do-then-verify' (read-and-do) lists where you perform each action as you read it, and 'verify' (challenge-and-response) lists where actions are performed from memory then verified against the list. Both types catch errors that memory alone would miss. Checklists are effective because they don't rely on your mental state--whether you're fresh or exhausted, stressed or calm, the list remains the same. They also standardise procedures across crew members and flights. For commercial operations, checklists are legally required and are a core element of SOPs. The key to their effectiveness is disciplined use: always use them, never skip items, and if interrupted during a checklist, go back to the beginning.
exam_tip: Checklists: the most important external memory aid. Compensate for prospective memory failure, WM limits, and stress/fatigue effects. Two types: do-then-verify and verify. If interrupted: go back to the beginning. Disciplined use is essential.
key_facts: Most important external memory aid in aviation; compensates for memory unreliability
Address prospective memory failure, WM limits, and stress/fatigue/distraction effects
Two types: do-then-verify (read-and-do) and verify (challenge-and-response)
If interrupted during a checklist: go back to the beginning; disciplined use essential
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the following methods of retaining and retrieving information from memory: (e) aide memoirs.
concept: Aide memoirs (aide-memoires) are written or printed reference materials that supplement memory for information that doesn't need to be committed to long-term memory but must be available when needed. In the cockpit, these include: approach plates and charts, performance tables, emergency reference cards (QRH -- Quick Reference Handbook), frequency cards, aerodrome information sheets, SOPs, and flight plans. Unlike checklists (which are procedural), aide memoirs contain reference information: speeds, frequencies, procedures for specific situations, aircraft limitations, and system operating parameters. They reduce the cognitive load of trying to remember everything by providing a reliable external source. Electronic flight bags (EFBs) have expanded the aide memoir concept, putting vast amounts of reference information at the pilot's fingertips. The key to effective use of aide memoirs is: knowing what information is available and where to find it (so you can access it quickly when needed), keeping them current (outdated information is worse than no information), and having them accessible (not buried in a bag while you need them during approach).
exam_tip: Aide memoirs: reference materials supplementing memory (charts, QRH, frequency cards, SOPs). Reduce cognitive load. Key: know where to find information, keep it current, keep it accessible. EFBs expand the concept.
key_facts: Written/printed reference materials supplementing memory for non-memorised information
Include: approach plates, QRH, frequency cards, performance tables, SOPs
Reduce cognitive load by providing reliable external reference sources
Must be current, accessible, and the pilot must know where to find needed information
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the concept of mental workload.
concept: Mental workload is the proportion of your total cognitive capacity consumed by the tasks you're performing. It's the information processing demand placed on your brain relative to your available capacity. Low workload (cruise in clear weather, autopilot engaged, no traffic) leaves excess capacity for monitoring, thinking ahead, and handling unexpected events. Moderate workload (approach phase, moderate traffic, some weather) engages most of your capacity while leaving a manageable reserve. High workload (holding, approach in poor weather, equipment problems, ATC demands) may consume your entire capacity or exceed it. When mental workload exceeds capacity, performance degrades: tasks are dropped or performed poorly, errors increase, communication suffers, and situational awareness narrows. Crucially, mental workload is subjective and dynamic--it depends not just on the tasks but on your training, experience, fatigue level, and the tools available. The same approach procedure might be low workload for an experienced pilot in a well-equipped aircraft and overwhelming for a less-experienced pilot in a basic aircraft. Managing mental workload means anticipating high-workload periods and preparing for them.
exam_tip: Mental workload: cognitive demand relative to capacity. When demand exceeds capacity: tasks dropped, errors increase, SA narrows. Workload is subjective (depends on training, experience, fatigue, tools). Manage by anticipation and preparation.
key_facts: Proportion of cognitive capacity consumed by current tasks
Low workload leaves reserve; high workload may exceed capacity
When exceeded: task shedding, increased errors, degraded communication, narrowed SA
Subjective: depends on training, experience, fatigue, and available tools
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the concept of overload.
concept: Overload occurs when the mental workload demanded by your tasks exceeds your available cognitive capacity. There are two types: information overload (too much information arriving simultaneously--multiple ATC instructions, multiple system warnings, multiple crew communications) and task overload (too many actions required simultaneously or in rapid sequence). When overloaded, predictable degradation patterns emerge: you fixate on one task to the exclusion of others, you shed tasks (often unconsciously, and often the wrong tasks), you revert to simpler, well-practised responses even if they're not appropriate, your communication becomes terse or ceases, your scan degrades, and you stop thinking ahead. In severe overload, you may freeze--unable to process anything or take any action. The cockpit environment is designed to manage overload risk: autopilot reduces manual flying workload, checklists guide sequential actions, standard phraseology reduces communication processing, and warning hierarchy prioritises information. As a pilot, you manage overload by: anticipating high-workload periods, preparing in advance, using CRM to distribute tasks, simplifying where possible, and being willing to reject additional demands ('unable, workload').
exam_tip: Overload: demand exceeds capacity. Types: information overload and task overload. Results: fixation, task shedding, reversion to simple responses, communication stops, freeze. Manage: anticipate, prepare, CRM, simplify, 'unable workload.'
key_facts: Occurs when mental workload demands exceed available cognitive capacity
Information overload (too much data) and task overload (too many actions)
Degradation: fixation, task shedding, reversion to simple responses, freeze
Manage: anticipate, prepare, use CRM, simplify, reject additional demands when necessary
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe methods of managing potential overload.
concept: Managing potential overload requires proactive strategies before, during, and after high-workload periods. Before: brief thoroughly so everyone knows the plan, pre-set radios and navigation equipment, review approach procedures early, identify potential complications and prepare responses, distribute tasks in advance (PF/PM roles), and simplify the flight plan where possible (choose a simpler approach if conditions allow). During: prioritise tasks ruthlessly (Aviate, Navigate, Communicate), use automation (autopilot, auto-throttle) to reduce manual workload, delegate tasks to other crew members, defer non-essential tasks ('we'll sort that after landing'), request ATC assistance ('request vectors, workload'), slow things down (request an orbit or hold to buy time), and maintain the instrument scan even when fixated on a problem. After: debrief to identify what worked and what didn't, update procedures if needed. Individual capacity management: maintain proficiency (well-practised tasks consume less capacity), manage fatigue (rested pilots have more capacity), and recognise your personal overload signs (the feeling of 'falling behind the aircraft'). The single most effective overload management tool is CRM: two well-coordinated crew members handle workload far more effectively than one pilot alone.
exam_tip: Before: brief, pre-set, simplify. During: prioritise (ANC), automate, delegate, defer, slow down, request help. After: debrief. Key tools: CRM (distribute workload), automation, and 'aviate, navigate, communicate' priority.
key_facts: Before: brief thoroughly, pre-set equipment, simplify, distribute tasks, prepare for contingencies
During: prioritise (ANC), automate, delegate, defer non-essential, request ATC help, slow down
Individual: maintain proficiency (reduces task demand), manage fatigue (increases capacity)
CRM is the most effective tool: two coordinated crew handle workload far better than one
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe and compare skill, rule and knowledge-based behaviours.
concept: Rasmussen's framework classifies human behaviour into three levels based on cognitive demand. Skill-based behaviour: automatic, practised responses requiring minimal conscious attention--like basic aircraft handling, routine radio calls, or standard checklist responses. These are fast, efficient, and accurate (when conditions match the learned pattern), but errors at this level are slips and lapses (doing the wrong thing automatically). Rule-based behaviour: following learned rules and procedures for recognised situations--'if this, then that.' For example, 'if engine fails below Vyse on a twin, maintain Vyse and clean up.' These are moderately demanding and accurate for situations that match the rules, but errors occur when the wrong rule is applied to the situation or the situation is misidentified. Knowledge-based behaviour: reasoning from first principles for novel situations where no learned rule exists. This is the most cognitively demanding level, the slowest, and the most error-prone. A novel emergency with no standard procedure requires knowledge-based reasoning. Commercial pilot training aims to expand the skill and rule levels through practice and procedures, minimising the need for error-prone knowledge-based processing in emergencies.
exam_tip: Three levels: skill-based (automatic, fast, slips/lapses), rule-based (if-then procedures, moderate demand, wrong rule errors), knowledge-based (reasoning from scratch, slow, most error-prone). Training expands skill/rule to reduce reliance on knowledge-based.
key_facts: Skill-based: automatic, fast, minimal attention; errors are slips and lapses
Rule-based: follows if-then rules for recognised situations; errors from wrong rule selection
Knowledge-based: reasoning from first principles for novel situations; slowest, most error-prone
Training aims to expand skill and rule repertoires, minimising need for knowledge-based processing
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Rasmussen SRK Framework, Campbell & Bagshaw -- HPL

syllabus_desc: Describe the process of acquiring a skill.
concept: Skill acquisition follows a predictable progression through three phases. Cognitive phase: the learner consciously thinks through every step--'push the stick forward, check the airspeed, look outside, add power.' This phase is slow, demanding, and error-prone, but understanding is being built. Working memory is heavily loaded. Associative phase: individual steps begin to link together into smooth sequences. The learner still thinks consciously about the task but it's becoming more fluid. Errors decrease. Working memory demand reduces as portions become semi-automatic. This phase involves extensive practice and feedback. Autonomous phase: the skill becomes automatic, requiring minimal conscious attention. The pilot performs the skill smoothly and accurately while simultaneously handling other tasks. However, further improvement requires deliberate practice--once autonomous, the skill plateaus unless actively refined. For CPL training, understanding this progression helps: don't expect students to multi-task during the cognitive phase, allow sufficient practice for the associative phase, and use deliberate practice techniques (varied scenarios, increasing difficulty) to continue improving beyond the autonomous plateau.
exam_tip: Three phases: cognitive (slow, conscious, demanding), associative (linking steps, becoming fluid), autonomous (automatic, minimal attention). Working memory load decreases with progression. Autonomous skills plateau without deliberate practice.
key_facts: Cognitive phase: slow, conscious processing of every step; high WM load; error-prone
Associative phase: steps link into sequences; becoming more fluid; errors decrease
Autonomous phase: automatic, minimal attention; frees WM for other tasks
Skills plateau at autonomous phase; deliberate practice needed for continued improvement
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe failures of skill, rule and knowledge-based behaviours.
concept: Each level of Rasmussen's framework has characteristic failure modes. Skill-based failures: slips (performing the wrong action in the right sequence--selecting the wrong switch because it's in a similar position to the correct one) and lapses (omitting a step in an automatic sequence--forgetting to set the altimeter during a well-practised descent checklist). These occur during routine, well-practised tasks when attention is diverted. Rule-based failures: applying the wrong rule to a correctly diagnosed situation, or misdiagnosing the situation and applying a rule appropriate to the wrong situation. For example, treating an engine vibration as a simple rough running issue (applying the 'lean the mixture' rule) when it's actually a failing bearing (requiring shutdown). Knowledge-based failures: reaching the wrong conclusion when reasoning from first principles about a novel situation. These are the most dangerous because the pilot may be very confident in their incorrect conclusion. Confirmation bias (seeking evidence that confirms your hypothesis while ignoring contradictory evidence) is the biggest trap at this level. Each failure type requires different defences: skill-based errors need checklists and cross-checks; rule-based errors need correct diagnosis; knowledge-based errors need crew consultation and systematic analysis.
exam_tip: Skill failures: slips (wrong action) and lapses (omitted action). Rule failures: wrong rule applied or wrong situation diagnosed. Knowledge failures: wrong conclusion from reasoning, worsened by confirmation bias. Different defences for each.
key_facts: Skill-based: slips (wrong action) and lapses (omitted step) during routine tasks
Rule-based: wrong rule selection or situation misdiagnosis
Knowledge-based: incorrect reasoning about novel situations; confirmation bias is key trap
Defences: checklists for skill errors, correct diagnosis for rule errors, crew consultation for knowledge errors
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Rasmussen SRK Framework, Reason -- Human Error

syllabus_desc: Explain confirmation bias.
concept: Confirmation bias is the tendency to search for, interpret, and remember information in a way that confirms your existing beliefs or hypothesis, while ignoring or dismissing information that contradicts it. In aviation, this is one of the most dangerous cognitive biases because it can lock you into a wrong decision pathway. Example: you diagnose an engine problem as carburettor icing and apply carb heat. The engine continues to run rough. Confirmation bias makes you think 'the ice must be really bad, it'll clear in a minute' rather than considering the alternative: maybe it's not carb icing at all. You're filtering all the evidence through the lens of your initial diagnosis. Confirmation bias is stronger under: time pressure (you don't have time to reconsider), stress (cognitive flexibility decreases), fatigue (less capacity for critical thinking), and high confidence in your experience ('I've seen this before, I know what it is'). Countering confirmation bias requires: actively seeking contradictory evidence, asking 'what else could this be?', inviting crew input ('do you agree with my assessment?'), using structured decision-making frameworks, and being willing to change your mind when evidence doesn't fit.
exam_tip: Confirmation bias: seeking evidence that confirms your hypothesis while ignoring contradictory data. Stronger under stress, time pressure, fatigue. Counter: actively seek contradictory evidence, ask 'what else could this be?', invite crew input.
key_facts: Tendency to seek, interpret, and remember information confirming existing beliefs
Ignores or dismisses contradictory information, locking into wrong decisions
Stronger under time pressure, stress, fatigue, and high confidence
Counter: seek contradictory evidence, ask 'what else?', crew input, structured decision-making
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of the following on perception: (a) expectation.
concept: Expectation powerfully shapes what you perceive. Your brain doesn't just passively receive sensory information--it actively predicts what it expects to see, hear, or feel, and tends to interpret ambiguous input in line with those predictions. In aviation: if you expect the runway to be straight ahead, you may not notice that you're actually lined up on a taxiway. If you expect a QNH of 1013, you may read 1013 on the altimeter even when it actually reads 1003. If you expect the weather to be fine (because the forecast said so), you may interpret marginal conditions as 'okay' rather than deteriorating. This expectation-driven perception is efficient (it speeds up processing in familiar situations) but dangerous when reality doesn't match expectations. Expectation is closely related to confirmation bias--you expect something, so you see it. The defence is: verify, don't assume. Cross-check critical readings. Use standardised calls that require specific responses. Maintain a questioning attitude: 'does this actually match what I expected?' When something seems slightly off, investigate rather than dismiss it.
exam_tip: Expectation shapes perception: you see what you expect to see. Dangerous when reality doesn't match. Classic errors: misreading instruments to match expectation, not noticing deteriorating conditions. Defence: verify, cross-check, questioning attitude.
key_facts: Brain predicts expected input and interprets ambiguous data to match expectations
Efficient in familiar situations but dangerous when reality doesn't match
Classic errors: misreading instruments, accepting poor conditions, wrong runway alignment
Defence: verify don't assume, cross-check readings, maintain questioning attitude
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of the following on perception: (b) experience.
concept: Experience shapes perception in both positive and negative ways. Positive: experienced pilots recognise patterns faster (an experienced pilot glances at the engine instruments and immediately spots the anomaly that a student would miss), process familiar situations more efficiently, and have richer mental models that help interpret ambiguous information correctly. An experienced NZ bush pilot can read terrain and weather with a sophistication that a newly-qualified CPL simply doesn't have yet. Negative: experience can create perceptual biases. You may not see what you don't expect based on your experience ('I've never had an engine failure, so that rough running must be something minor'). You may apply past patterns to new situations where they don't fit. Over-familiarity with a route or procedure can reduce vigilance--you 'see' the normal picture from memory rather than actually scanning. Experienced pilots operating familiar routes are vulnerable to complacency-driven perceptual failures. The balance is using experience to enhance perception while maintaining the questioning attitude and vigilance of a less-experienced pilot. 'Fresh eyes' approaches--periodically looking at your familiar operation as if for the first time--help counter experience-based perceptual bias.
exam_tip: Experience: positive (faster pattern recognition, richer mental models) and negative (biases, complacency, seeing what you expect from past experience). Counter: maintain questioning attitude, 'fresh eyes' approach, don't assume familiar = safe.
key_facts: Positive: faster pattern recognition, efficient processing of familiar situations
Negative: perceptual biases, complacency, 'seeing' normal from memory instead of scanning
Over-familiarity with routes/procedures reduces vigilance
Counter: maintain questioning attitude, 'fresh eyes' approach, don't equate familiar with safe
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation of mental models.
concept: A mental model is your internal representation of how a system works--your understanding of the aircraft, the weather, the airspace, the traffic picture, or any other aspect of the flight environment. Mental models are constructed from training, experience, and current sensory input, and they allow you to predict what will happen next (if I add power, the nose will pitch up and airspeed will increase). Good mental models enable effective decision-making by allowing you to simulate outcomes mentally before committing to action. They're updated continuously as new information arrives--or at least they should be. Problems arise when: the mental model is incomplete (you don't fully understand the system), incorrect (based on wrong information or assumptions), or outdated (the situation has changed but your model hasn't). An outdated mental model is particularly dangerous: if conditions deteriorate but your model still says 'weather is fine,' you'll make decisions based on a picture that no longer matches reality. Loss of situational awareness is essentially a failure of the mental model. Keeping your mental model current requires continuous information gathering, cross-checking, and willingness to update your picture when new data contradicts it.
exam_tip: Mental model: internal representation of how systems and situations work. Enables prediction and decision-making. Dangerous when: incomplete, incorrect, or outdated. Loss of SA = mental model doesn't match reality. Keep current through continuous information gathering.
key_facts: Internal representation enabling prediction and mental simulation of outcomes
Constructed from training, experience, and current sensory input
Dangerous when incomplete, incorrect, or outdated (doesn't match current reality)
Loss of situational awareness = mental model failure; requires continuous updating
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the special perceptual problems associated with: (a) snow operations.
concept: Snow operations create unique perceptual challenges. Whiteout: when snow-covered terrain and overcast sky blend together, eliminating the horizon and all visual references. This can cause complete spatial disorientation. Sector whiteout: partial loss of horizon in some sectors while others remain clear (discussed under visual illusions). Flat light: overcast conditions over snow reduce shadows and texture cues to near zero, making terrain features (ridges, slopes, drop-offs) invisible. A smooth white surface that's actually a steep slope looks flat. Snow glare: intense reflected sunlight from snow causes disability glare and can cause snow blindness (photokeratitis) with prolonged exposure--essentially sunburn of the cornea, causing pain, tearing, and temporary vision loss. Depth perception is severely degraded over featureless snow--judging height during approach and landing becomes extremely difficult. In NZ, snow operations are relevant for mountain flying (Southern Alps), winter operations at high-elevation aerodromes, and helicopter operations in ski fields. Defences include: instrument references at all times, wearing good quality sunglasses with UV protection, maintaining terrain awareness, using PAPI where available, and not attempting visual approaches in flat-light or whiteout conditions.
exam_tip: Snow: whiteout (no horizon), flat light (terrain invisible), snow glare (photokeratitis), degraded depth perception. NZ: Southern Alps, winter aerodromes, ski fields. Defence: instruments, sunglasses, PAPI, don't go visual in whiteout/flat light.
key_facts: Whiteout: snow + overcast eliminate horizon and all visual references
Flat light: overcast removes shadows and texture, making terrain features invisible
Snow glare: intense UV reflection causing disability glare and potential photokeratitis
Depth perception severely degraded; use instruments, PAPI, and don't go visual in poor conditions
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the special perceptual problems associated with: (b) water operations.
concept: Operations over water present several perceptual challenges. Horizon definition: over calm water, especially in haze, the water surface blends with the sky, eliminating the visible horizon (similar to whiteout over snow). This is particularly dangerous at dusk or dawn when light levels are transitioning. Height judgement: over water, there are few visual cues for judging height accurately. Calm, glassy water is the worst case--the surface becomes a mirror reflecting the sky, and you literally cannot tell where the air ends and the water begins. This has caused numerous CFIT accidents in seaplane operations and low-level coastal flying. Distance estimation: objects on or over water appear further away than they are, because the familiar terrestrial scale cues are absent. Spatial disorientation: flying over water at night with no horizon and scattered lights (ships, oil platforms) that might be confused with stars is a high-risk scenario for spatial illusions. In NZ, water operations include coastal flying around the Hauraki Gulf, Bay of Islands, Marlborough Sounds, and Milford Sound; island services; and search and rescue. For floatplane operations, glassy water landings require specific technique (power-on approach to known water).
exam_tip: Water: horizon blends with sky (especially calm + haze), height judgement impossible over glassy water, distance underestimated. NZ: Hauraki Gulf, Sounds, island services. Glassy water landings require specific technique.
key_facts: Calm water in haze: horizon disappears as surface blends with sky
Glassy water: mirror effect makes it impossible to judge height -- CFIT risk
Objects over water appear further away than actual due to missing scale cues
NZ operations: coastal flying, island services, Sounds, glassy water landing technique required
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the special perceptual problems associated with: (c) agricultural and low flying operations.
concept: Agricultural and low-level flying operations present extreme perceptual demands because of the proximity to the ground, high workload, and limited reaction time. Wire strike hazard: wires are the number-one hazard in agricultural aviation and are extremely difficult to see. They're thin, low-contrast, often don't appear on charts, and may be at unexpected heights and locations. Even after thorough survey, new wires can be erected. Obstacle perception: trees, poles, buildings, and terrain features are encountered at close range with minimal time to react. Speed perception: low-level flight over terrain creates a strong impression of speed through peripheral vision; this can be disorienting and fatiguing. Terrain undulation: at low level, small terrain changes become significant--a ridge that's barely noticeable from 1,000 feet becomes a critical obstacle at 50 feet. Turbulence at low level: mechanical turbulence from terrain, buildings, and trees is more intense and less predictable at low altitude. Fatigue: the intense concentration required for low-level operations is mentally exhausting, leading to attention lapses during long spray sessions. In NZ, agricultural aviation is a significant part of commercial operations, particularly in the Waikato, Canterbury, and Manawatu regions.
exam_tip: Low-level: wire strike is #1 hazard (thin, low-contrast, uncharged). Speed perception intense, terrain changes critical, turbulence unpredictable, extreme mental fatigue from sustained concentration. NZ: significant agricultural aviation sector.
key_facts: Wire strike: number one hazard in agricultural aviation; extremely difficult to see
Obstacles encountered at close range with minimal reaction time
Intense concentration causes rapid mental fatigue and attention lapses
Terrain undulations and mechanical turbulence more critical at low altitude
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe strategies to maintain and enhance situational awareness.
concept: Situational awareness (SA) is knowing what's going on around you and what it means for your flight. Maintaining and enhancing SA requires active, deliberate effort. Strategies include: continuous information gathering (scan instruments, look outside, listen to radio, check weather updates), mental projection (thinking ahead -- 'in 10 minutes I'll be at the ranges, what will the weather be doing there?'), maintaining a current mental model (actively update your picture of traffic, weather, fuel state, and aircraft condition), briefing and debriefing (structured sharing of the plan and what happened), distributing SA tasks in a crew (pilot monitoring maintains big picture while pilot flying focuses on approach), using all available resources (ATC, ATIS, FISCOM, weather radar, TCAS), managing workload to preserve SA capacity (don't let task fixation destroy your big picture), recognising SA loss indicators (confusion, uncertainty, 'that doesn't seem right,' or nobody talking about what's happening), and speaking up when something doesn't add up. For NZ CPL operations, SA is particularly important in the dynamic weather of the South Island mountains and the busy uncontrolled airspace around training aerodromes.
exam_tip: SA strategies: continuous info gathering, thinking ahead (mental projection), current mental model, briefing/debriefing, distributed SA in crew, using all resources, workload management. Recognise SA loss: confusion, uncertainty, 'doesn't feel right.'
key_facts: Continuous information gathering: instruments, outside, radio, weather updates
Mental projection: thinking ahead about what's coming in 10-20 minutes
Distribute SA in crew: PM maintains big picture while PF focuses on immediate tasks
Recognise SA loss indicators: confusion, uncertainty, nobody discussing the situation
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between crew resource management (CRM) and the building of situational awareness amongst flight crew.
concept: CRM and SA are deeply interconnected--CRM provides the tools and processes that build and maintain SA in a crew environment. Effective communication distributes SA: when the PM calls 'approaching 5,000, setting QNH,' both crew members now have that information in their mental models. Shared mental models (both crew members having the same understanding of the situation) are built through: thorough briefing before the flight, structured callouts during the flight, regular cross-checks and information sharing, and debriefing after. CRM assertiveness enables SA recovery: if the PM notices the PF is fixated on troubleshooting and losing altitude awareness, CRM empowers them to speak up ('check altitude'). Workload management through CRM (distributing tasks appropriately between PF and PM) ensures that someone always has capacity for monitoring the big picture. Decision-making as a crew pools different perspectives--two people are more likely to catch a developing problem than one. In single-pilot CPL operations (common in NZ), SA is harder to maintain because all these CRM functions must be self-managed. Using structured procedures, verbalising your actions (talking yourself through the flight), and proactively seeking information from ATC are partial substitutes.
exam_tip: CRM builds SA through: communication (distributes information), shared mental models (common understanding), assertiveness (enables SA recovery), workload management (preserves monitoring capacity). Single-pilot: self-manage with procedures and verbalisation.
key_facts: Communication distributes SA: structured callouts keep both crew informed
Shared mental models built through briefing, callouts, cross-checks, debriefing
CRM assertiveness enables SA recovery when one crew member is fixated
Single-pilot operations: use procedures, verbalisation, and proactive ATC communication
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between skills, knowledge and attitudes.
concept: Skills, knowledge, and attitudes are the three pillars of pilot competence, each distinct but interrelated. Skills are the physical and cognitive abilities to perform tasks: flying the aircraft, navigating, communicating, managing systems. Skills improve with practice and are assessed through practical tests. Knowledge is the factual and conceptual understanding: aerodynamics, regulations, meteorology, human factors, aircraft systems. Knowledge is built through study and assessed through written exams. Attitudes are the mindset and behavioural tendencies that determine how you apply your skills and knowledge: your willingness to follow procedures, your approach to risk, your response to pressure, your openness to feedback, and your commitment to safety. Attitudes are the most critical and the hardest to teach and assess. A pilot with excellent skills and knowledge but poor attitudes (complacency, risk-taking, invulnerability) is more dangerous than a pilot with average skills and good attitudes. The five hazardous attitudes identified in aviation are: anti-authority, impulsivity, invulnerability, macho, and resignation. Training must develop all three: skills through practice, knowledge through study, and attitudes through CRM, mentoring, and organisational culture.
exam_tip: Skills: ability to perform (practice). Knowledge: understanding (study). Attitudes: mindset and behavioural tendencies (hardest to teach). Five hazardous attitudes: anti-authority, impulsivity, invulnerability, macho, resignation. Attitudes are the most critical factor.
key_facts: Skills: physical/cognitive abilities, improved by practice, assessed practically
Knowledge: factual/conceptual understanding, built by study, assessed by examination
Attitudes: mindset determining how skills and knowledge are applied -- most critical factor
Five hazardous attitudes: anti-authority, impulsivity, invulnerability, macho, resignation
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe methods of countering the hazardous attitudes.
concept: Each hazardous attitude has a specific antidote--a corrective thought to counter the dangerous tendency. Anti-authority ('Don't tell me what to do'): antidote is 'Follow the rules--they're usually right.' Rules and procedures exist because people died learning the lessons they encode. Impulsivity ('Do something quickly!'): antidote is 'Not so fast--think first.' The urge to act immediately often leads to the wrong action; taking a moment to think rarely makes things worse. Invulnerability ('It won't happen to me'): antidote is 'It could happen to me.' Accidents happen to experienced, skilled pilots--nobody is immune. Macho ('I can do it'): antidote is 'Taking chances is foolish.' Proving yourself by taking risks impresses nobody when it goes wrong. Resignation ('What's the use?'): antidote is 'I'm not helpless--I can make a difference.' Even in difficult situations, your actions matter. These antidotes work by creating a deliberate cognitive pause: recognising the hazardous attitude in yourself, then consciously applying the corrective thought before acting. Self-awareness is the key--you need to know which hazardous attitudes you're personally prone to. CRM training helps develop this self-awareness.
exam_tip: Antidotes: anti-authority = 'follow the rules'; impulsivity = 'think first'; invulnerability = 'it could happen to me'; macho = 'taking chances is foolish'; resignation = 'I can make a difference.' Key: self-awareness of personal tendencies.
key_facts: Anti-authority: 'follow the rules, they're usually right'
Impulsivity: 'not so fast, think first' -- pause before acting
Invulnerability: 'it could happen to me' -- nobody is immune
Macho: 'taking chances is foolish'; Resignation: 'I can make a difference'
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the error/poor judgement chain.
concept: The error chain (or accident chain) is the concept that accidents rarely result from a single catastrophic failure but rather from a sequence of individually minor errors, poor decisions, and unfavourable conditions that link together. Each link in the chain is a small contribution to the overall risk picture: inadequate pre-flight planning, acceptance of marginal weather, skipping a checklist item, fatigue, time pressure, poor communication. Individually, most of these wouldn't cause an accident. But when they accumulate, they create a path to disaster. The chain concept is powerful because it implies that breaking any single link prevents the accident. A pilot who recognises the chain building--'I'm tired, the weather's marginal, I didn't brief properly, and now ATC is asking for something unusual'--can break the chain by addressing any one factor: delaying for rest, declining the flight, completing the brief, or requesting simpler ATC handling. The earlier you break the chain, the easier it is. The error chain also explains why post-accident investigation identifies multiple contributing factors rather than a single cause.
exam_tip: Error chain: sequence of minor errors/conditions linking to create an accident path. Breaking any single link prevents the accident. Recognise the chain building and break it early. Explains why accidents have multiple contributing factors, not single causes.
key_facts: Accidents result from a chain of individually minor errors, decisions, and conditions
Each link alone wouldn't cause an accident; cumulative combination creates disaster
Breaking any single link prevents the accident -- earlier is easier
Post-accident investigation identifies multiple contributing factors, not single causes
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain clues or red flags that can assist in identifying the error/poor judgement chain.
concept: Red flags that indicate an error chain is building include: departing behind schedule (creates time pressure that degrades decision-making), incomplete preparation (rushed briefing, skipped checks), rationalising marginal conditions ('the weather might improve,' 'we'll probably be okay'), ignoring gut feelings ('something doesn't feel right' is a valid safety input), deviation from SOPs (shortcuts, skipped items), unfamiliar factors accumulating (new aircraft, unfamiliar route, different crew), fatigue or illness (reduced capacity to manage problems), external pressure (passengers waiting, employer expectations, 'get-there-itis'), communication breakdowns (crew not talking, missed ATC calls), unexpected events (equipment anomalies, weather changes, ATC rerouting), and the feeling of 'being behind the aircraft' (events happening faster than you can process). The discipline is to recognise these red flags as they accumulate and take action before the chain completes. A useful mental model: if you can count three or more active red flags, seriously reconsider the flight. Any single red flag warrants heightened awareness.
exam_tip: Red flags: behind schedule, incomplete prep, rationalising marginal conditions, ignoring gut feelings, SOP deviations, unfamiliar factors, fatigue, external pressure, comms breakdown, 'behind the aircraft.' Three or more = seriously reconsider.
key_facts: Behind schedule, incomplete preparation, and rationalising margins are early warning signs
Ignoring gut feelings ('something doesn't feel right') is a critical red flag
External pressure (get-there-itis, passengers, employer) degrades decision-making
Three or more active red flags warrant serious reconsideration of the flight
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Identify risk assessment techniques.
concept: Risk assessment in aviation ranges from formal structured methods to quick cockpit assessments. Formal techniques include: risk matrices (plotting likelihood against consequence to classify risks as low/medium/high/extreme), safety management system (SMS) risk assessments, and HAZID (hazard identification) processes used in operational planning. Practical cockpit techniques include: PAVE (Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures--assessing risk in each category before flight), CARE (Consequences, Alternatives, Reality, External factors), and the '1-2-3' method (what are the 1-2-3 things that could go wrong, and what are the consequences?). Dynamic risk assessment during flight involves: continuous monitoring of changing conditions, recognising when the risk picture changes, and being prepared to modify the plan. The ALARP principle (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) applies: you can't eliminate all risk, but you should reduce it to the lowest practical level through mitigation measures. For NZ CPL operations, pre-flight risk assessment should be systematic, documented, and include consideration of the specific NZ hazards for the planned route (mountain weather, sea crossings, remote terrain).
exam_tip: Formal: risk matrices, SMS assessments. Cockpit: PAVE (Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures), dynamic assessment during flight. ALARP principle: reduce risk as low as reasonably practicable. Systematic, documented assessment for commercial ops.
key_facts: Formal: risk matrices, SMS risk assessments, HAZID processes
Cockpit: PAVE (Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures) checklist
Dynamic risk assessment: continuous monitoring and readiness to modify plan
ALARP principle: reduce risk as low as reasonably practicable through mitigation
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Identify risk levels that compromise safety.
concept: Risk levels in aviation are typically classified using a matrix of likelihood and severity. Likelihood categories: almost certain, likely, possible, unlikely, rare. Severity categories: catastrophic (loss of aircraft/lives), hazardous (serious injury, major damage), major (injury, significant damage), minor (inconvenience, minor damage), negligible (no significant consequence). Risks that compromise safety are those rated as 'high' or 'extreme' on the risk matrix--typically where the combination of likelihood and severity is unacceptable. Examples of unacceptable risk levels: continuing VFR into deteriorating weather with no instrument capability (high likelihood of spatial disorientation, catastrophic consequence), flying with a known mechanical deficiency that affects flight safety, operating single-pilot IFR while significantly fatigued. The key principle is that some risks are always unacceptable regardless of operational pressure: no flight schedule, customer expectation, or commercial consideration justifies accepting a risk level that significantly threatens the safety of the aircraft and its occupants. Risk assessment isn't just about identifying hazards--it's about having the discipline to decline or modify the operation when risk exceeds acceptable levels.
exam_tip: Risk matrix: likelihood x severity. High/extreme ratings are unacceptable. Some risks always unacceptable regardless of pressure (VFR into IMC without instruments, known defects). Discipline to decline when risk exceeds acceptable level.
key_facts: Risk matrix combines likelihood (certain to rare) and severity (catastrophic to negligible)
High and extreme risk ratings are unacceptable and require mitigation or cancellation
No commercial pressure justifies accepting unacceptable risk to safety
Discipline to decline or modify operations when risk exceeds acceptable level is essential
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Outline the general concepts behind decision making.
concept: Decision-making in aviation can follow two broad approaches: analytical (systematic, deliberate) and naturalistic (intuitive, experience-based). Analytical decision-making involves: identifying the problem, gathering information, generating options, evaluating each option against criteria, selecting the best option, implementing it, and reviewing the outcome. It's thorough but slow--suitable for pre-flight planning and non-time-critical decisions. Naturalistic decision-making (recognition-primed decision-making) involves: pattern matching the current situation against past experience, selecting a response from your mental library, mentally simulating the outcome, and if it seems workable, implementing it. This is faster and is how experienced pilots make most in-flight decisions. Both approaches have strengths and weaknesses: analytical is thorough but slow; naturalistic is fast but depends on having relevant experience and can be fooled by novel situations. The best decision-makers use both: naturalistic for familiar, time-critical situations, and analytical for novel or complex situations where intuition might be wrong. Good decision-making also requires: knowing when to decide (not too early, not too late), accepting imperfect information, and being willing to change course.
exam_tip: Two approaches: analytical (systematic, thorough, slow -- good for planning) and naturalistic (intuitive, fast, experience-based -- good for time-critical). Best pilots use both. Good decisions: right timing, accept imperfect info, willing to change.
key_facts: Analytical: systematic evaluation of options -- thorough but slow; suits planning
Naturalistic: pattern matching from experience -- fast but depends on relevant experience
Best decision-makers use both: naturalistic for time-critical, analytical for novel/complex
Good decisions require: right timing, accepting imperfect information, willingness to change
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe methods of enhancing decision making skills.
concept: Decision-making skills improve through deliberate development in several areas. Training: scenario-based training that presents realistic decision-making challenges, CRM training that develops crew decision-making processes, and simulator sessions that expose you to novel situations in a safe environment. Experience building: flying in varied conditions, visiting unfamiliar aerodromes, and volunteering for challenging (but safe) operations builds your pattern recognition library. Structured frameworks: learning and practising decision-making models (DECIDE, FORDEC, SADIE) provides systematic approaches for when intuition isn't sufficient. Debriefing: thorough debriefing of every flight--especially ones where decisions were difficult--builds your experience base faster than simply flying. Studying accidents and incidents: reading accident reports and analysing the decision points gives you vicarious experience. Self-awareness: understanding your personal biases, hazardous attitudes, and stress responses helps you recognise when your decision-making might be compromised. Mentoring: working with experienced pilots who share their decision-making processes. The key is active learning: don't just accumulate hours, accumulate wisdom by reflecting on every flight and every decision.
exam_tip: Enhance DM through: scenario-based training, CRM, simulator sessions, varied experience, structured frameworks (DECIDE), thorough debriefing, studying accidents, self-awareness of biases, mentoring. Active learning, not just hours.
key_facts: Scenario-based training and CRM develop realistic decision-making capability
Structured frameworks (DECIDE, FORDEC, SADIE) provide systematic approaches
Thorough debriefing and accident study build experience faster than flying hours alone
Self-awareness of biases, attitudes, and stress responses identifies compromised DM
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the application of decision-making models used in aviation including: (a) DECIDE.
concept: DECIDE is a structured decision-making model: D-Detect the change (something in the environment has changed or is different from expected), E-Estimate the significance (how does this change affect the safety and outcome of the flight?), C-Choose a course of action (identify options and select the best one), I-Identify solutions (determine the specific actions needed to implement your choice), D-Do the necessary action (execute the plan), E-Evaluate the effects (monitor the outcome--did your action work? Do you need to decide again?). Example: you're flying from Christchurch to Queenstown and notice (Detect) the cloud base is lower than forecast. You Estimate that the Lindis Pass route will be below minimums. You Choose to divert via the coastal route. You Identify the specific actions: new heading, ATC notification, fuel check for the longer route. You Do it: turn, call ATC, check fuel. You Evaluate: is the coastal route still clear? Is fuel sufficient? DECIDE provides a structured framework that prevents impulsive decisions and ensures you monitor outcomes.
