exam_tip: Harassment degrades flight safety by causing distraction, stress and communication breakdown. Must be addressed through clear policies, protected reporting and genuine consequences.
key_facts: Any unwanted behaviour causing intimidation, degradation or offence
Direct flight safety impact: distraction, stress, communication breakdown
Addressed through policies, reporting channels, investigation and consequences
NZ legal framework: Human Rights Act and Employment Relations Act
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe what is meant by stereotypes and stereotypical behaviour within organisations and give examples of where such behaviour may have a negative impact on safety.
concept: Stereotyping is when you make assumptions about someone based on their group membership rather than their individual qualities. 'She's too young to be a captain.' 'He's ex-military so he'll be rigid about procedures.' 'Pilots from that country don't challenge authority.' In aviation, stereotypes are dangerous because they override observation with assumption. If you assume a crew member is competent (or incompetent) based on their background rather than their actual performance, you'll either over-rely on them or dismiss their input -- both of which compromise safety. Stereotypical behaviour within organisations can manifest as gender bias in training opportunities, age-based assumptions about adaptability, or nationality-based assumptions about communication ability.
exam_tip: Stereotypes replace individual assessment with group assumptions. In aviation, this causes over-reliance or dismissal of crew members based on demographics rather than actual competence.
key_facts: Assumptions based on group membership rather than individual qualities
Override observation with prejudgement in crew assessments
Can affect training opportunities, role assignments and communication
Safety impact: over-reliance on or dismissal of crew members based on demographics
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the rationale for mandatory reporting of incidents as required by CAR Part 12.
concept: Mandatory reporting under NZ CAR Part 12 exists because voluntary reporting alone doesn't capture the full safety picture. Some events are too serious to leave to individual discretion -- they need to be reported so the regulator and the industry can identify trends, address systemic issues, and prevent accidents. Part 12 requires reporting of accidents, serious incidents, and specific defined occurrences. The rationale is fundamentally about learning: every incident carries information that could prevent a future accident. Without mandatory reporting, organisations might suppress unfavourable data, individuals might not recognise the significance of what happened, and the industry would lose the pattern-recognition capability that comes from aggregating data across multiple operators.
exam_tip: CAR Part 12 mandatory reporting ensures serious events are captured regardless of individual discretion. Rationale: trend identification, systemic issue resolution, and industry-wide learning.
key_facts: Required for accidents, serious incidents and defined occurrences
Prevents suppression of unfavourable safety data
Enables trend identification across multiple operators
Supports systemic issue resolution and industry-wide learning
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the relevance of internal hazard reporting.
concept: Internal hazard reporting catches the near-misses, the close calls, and the 'I noticed something that doesn't seem right' observations that mandatory reporting doesn't cover. It's the canary in the coal mine. For every accident, there are hundreds of incidents, and for every incident, thousands of hazard observations. If you only wait for accidents to learn, you're learning too late and too expensively. Internal reporting also captures operational context that formal reports often miss -- the workarounds people use, the procedures that don't quite work in practice, the environmental conditions that create recurring problems. In NZ, even small operators benefit from encouraging crew to report hazards informally, whether through a structured hazard register or simply an open-door conversation with the chief pilot.
exam_tip: Internal hazard reporting captures near-misses and observations below the mandatory reporting threshold -- the 'canary in the coal mine' for safety management.
key_facts: Captures near-misses and observations below mandatory reporting thresholds
For every accident: hundreds of incidents, thousands of hazard observations
Captures operational context formal reports miss
Even small NZ operators benefit from structured hazard reporting
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between normal error, at risk behaviour and high culpability behaviour.
concept: The Just Culture model draws three clear lines. Normal error is an honest mistake made by a competent person following reasonable procedures -- misreading an altimeter during high workload, for example. This deserves support and system improvement, not punishment. At-risk behaviour is when someone consciously cuts a corner or takes a shortcut, usually because they've done it before without consequences and the risk doesn't feel real -- like skipping a checklist item because 'I always remember that one.' This deserves coaching and system review to understand why the shortcut seemed reasonable. High culpability behaviour is wilful disregard for safety -- flying under the influence, deliberately violating known safety rules, or reckless conduct. This deserves disciplinary action. Getting these distinctions right is what makes a just culture work.
exam_tip: Normal error = honest mistake (support). At-risk behaviour = conscious shortcut (coach). High culpability = wilful disregard (discipline). Getting the classification right is the foundation of just culture.
key_facts: Normal error: honest mistake by competent person -- support and improve the system
At-risk behaviour: conscious shortcut without perceived risk -- coach and review system
High culpability: wilful disregard for safety -- disciplinary action
Correct classification is the foundation of a functioning just culture
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between negligent and reckless behaviour.
concept: Negligent behaviour is when a pilot fails to exercise the standard of care that a reasonable, competent pilot would exercise in the same circumstances. It's a failure to meet the expected standard, but without deliberate intent to cause harm. Forgetting to check NOTAMs before a flight is negligent -- you should have done it, but you didn't intend to create a dangerous situation. Reckless behaviour goes further -- it's when you consciously disregard a known and substantial risk. Flying VFR into IMC when you can see the cloud ahead and press on anyway is reckless. The distinction matters legally and in a just culture context: negligence may warrant retraining and monitoring, while recklessness typically warrants serious disciplinary or regulatory action.
exam_tip: Negligence = failure to meet a reasonable standard of care (unintentional). Recklessness = conscious disregard of a known substantial risk (intentional). Recklessness warrants more severe consequences.
key_facts: Negligence: failure to exercise reasonable care, without deliberate intent
Recklessness: conscious disregard of a known and substantial risk
Negligence may warrant retraining; recklessness warrants serious action
The distinction is critical for legal proceedings and just culture application
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Identify the attributes of at risk behaviour.
concept: At-risk behaviour has some telltale attributes. First, it involves a conscious choice -- the person knows there's a safer way but chooses the shortcut. Second, the risk is not perceived as significant -- either because they've done it before without consequences or because the potential downside seems remote. Third, it's often normalised within the group -- 'everyone does it.' Fourth, it usually provides some perceived benefit: saving time, reducing workload, or avoiding hassle. Classic examples in aviation: not following the full taxi checklist because 'we only taxied 50 metres,' skipping the walkaround on the second flight of the day, or accepting a clearance you're not entirely comfortable with because you don't want to inconvenience ATC.
exam_tip: At-risk behaviour attributes: conscious choice, unrecognised risk, normalised within the group, provides perceived benefit. Often expressed as 'everyone does it' or 'it's never been a problem before.'
key_facts: Conscious choice -- the person knows there is a safer alternative
Risk not perceived as significant due to prior incident-free experience
Often normalised within the group ('everyone does it')
Provides perceived benefit: saves time, reduces workload, avoids hassle
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the concepts of risk creep and risk tolerance and their application within an aviation organisation.
concept: Risk creep is the gradual, often invisible, increase in risk tolerance over time. It happens when small deviations from standard become normal, and the baseline shifts without anyone noticing. Each individual deviation seems minor -- 'we'll just skip that one check today' -- but over months and years, the cumulative effect is an organisation operating far outside its intended safety margins. Risk tolerance is the level of risk an organisation or individual is willing to accept. A healthy organisation sets explicit risk tolerances and monitors for creep. An unhealthy one lets tolerance drift upward, driven by commercial pressure, complacency, or simply the absence of incidents reinforcing the belief that current practices are safe. In NZ, several accident investigations have identified risk creep as a contributing factor.
exam_tip: Risk creep = gradual, invisible increase in accepted risk. Risk tolerance = the level of risk an organisation accepts. Both must be actively monitored -- absence of incidents doesn't mean absence of risk.
key_facts: Risk creep: gradual increase in accepted risk, often unnoticed
Each small deviation normalises slightly higher risk
Risk tolerance: the level of risk an organisation is willing to accept
Absence of incidents reinforces false belief that creeping risk is safe
Active monitoring required to detect and correct risk creep
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the role of punitive sanction.
concept: Punitive sanction -- punishment -- has a role in aviation safety, but a narrower one than many people think. In a just culture, punishment is reserved for wilful violations, reckless behaviour, and cases where someone deliberately disregards known safety requirements. For honest errors and at-risk behaviours, punishment is counterproductive: it drives reporting underground, makes people hide mistakes, and destroys the information flow that the safety system depends on. However, having no consequences for genuinely culpable behaviour is equally damaging -- it signals that rules don't matter and erodes trust in the system. The right balance: support and educate for errors, coach for at-risk behaviour, and apply fair but firm sanctions for wilful violations.
exam_tip: Punitive sanction is reserved for wilful violations and reckless behaviour. Punishing honest errors destroys reporting culture. The key is proportionate response based on just culture classification.
key_facts: Reserved for wilful violations and reckless behaviour only
Punishing honest errors drives reporting underground
No consequences for culpable behaviour erodes trust in the system
Proportionate: support errors, coach at-risk behaviour, sanction wilful violations
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in flight deck design: comfort.
concept: Comfort in flight deck design isn't about luxury -- it's about sustained performance. An uncomfortable pilot is a distracted pilot. If your seat doesn't support your back properly over a four-hour sector, you'll be shifting around, losing focus, and arriving at your destination with reduced capacity for the approach and landing. Comfort encompasses seat design, cushioning, ventilation, temperature control, and noise levels. It also includes less obvious factors like vibration damping and the ability to relax muscles during cruise. NZ operators flying long sectors over water to the Pacific Islands or down the length of the South Island know this well -- what's tolerable for 30 minutes becomes a significant human factors issue over several hours.
exam_tip: Flight deck comfort affects sustained performance over long sectors. Discomfort causes distraction and fatigue -- it's a safety issue, not a luxury.
key_facts: Discomfort causes distraction and accelerates fatigue
Includes seat design, ventilation, temperature, noise and vibration
What's tolerable short-term becomes a significant issue over long sectors
Directly affects pilot performance during critical arrival phase
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in flight deck design: posture.
concept: Posture in the cockpit matters more than most pilots realise. Poor posture leads to fatigue, back pain, and reduced ability to perform physical tasks like controlling the aircraft in turbulence. But there's a design dimension too: if the seat and controls aren't positioned to allow a neutral, sustainable posture, the pilot has to work harder just to maintain position -- and that's energy not available for flying. Good flight deck design allows pilots of different body sizes to achieve a proper eye position (for instrument reading and outside visual reference) while maintaining comfortable control access. In many GA aircraft, the ergonomic compromises are significant -- which is why adjustable seats and rudder pedals are so important.
exam_tip: Proper posture reduces fatigue and enables sustained control inputs. Design must accommodate different body sizes while maintaining correct eye position for instruments and outside reference.
key_facts: Poor posture accelerates fatigue and reduces physical performance
Design must allow neutral posture while maintaining correct eye position
Must accommodate different body sizes through adjustable seats and pedals
GA aircraft often have significant ergonomic compromises
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in flight deck design: lighting levels.
concept: Lighting in the cockpit is a balancing act. You need enough light to read instruments, charts and checklists, but not so much that it destroys your night vision or creates glare on screens. Poor lighting design forces pilots into awkward positions to read things, increases eye fatigue, and can cause critical information to be missed. At night, the transition between looking at a lit instrument panel and peering out the window into darkness requires careful lighting management. Red and white lighting options serve different purposes -- red preserves dark adaptation better, but white provides better colour discrimination for charts. Modern glass cockpits add the challenge of screen brightness management, where a bright PFD can make the outside world invisible.
exam_tip: Cockpit lighting must balance readability with night vision preservation. Poor lighting increases eye fatigue and missed information. Screen brightness in glass cockpits requires careful management.
key_facts: Must balance instrument readability with night vision preservation
Red lighting preserves dark adaptation; white provides better colour discrimination
Poor lighting causes eye fatigue and missed information
Glass cockpit screen brightness can overpower outside visual reference at night
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between biometrics, biomechanics and anthropometry.
concept: These three terms sound similar but address different aspects of human physical characteristics. Biometrics is the measurement and statistical analysis of people's unique physical and behavioural characteristics -- fingerprints, retinal patterns, voice recognition. Biomechanics studies how the body moves, the forces involved, and the mechanical efficiency of different postures and movements. Anthropometry is the measurement of body dimensions -- arm length, seated height, leg reach, hand size. In flight deck design, anthropometry determines whether a pilot can reach the controls, biomechanics determines whether they can operate them effectively, and biometrics might be used for cockpit access security. Of the three, anthropometry has the most direct impact on flight deck layout.
exam_tip: Biometrics = unique physical identifiers. Biomechanics = body movement and forces. Anthropometry = body dimensions. Anthropometry has the most direct impact on flight deck design.
key_facts: Biometrics: measurement of unique physical/behavioural characteristics
Biomechanics: study of body movement, forces and mechanical efficiency
Anthropometry: measurement of body dimensions (arm length, reach, height)
Anthropometry most directly affects flight deck layout and control placement
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe applications of biomechanics in the design of flight decks.
concept: Biomechanics in flight deck design is about making sure the human body can interact with aircraft controls efficiently and without injury. Consider the force required to operate rudder pedals during crosswind landings -- it needs to be manageable for the smallest qualified pilot but provide enough feedback for the largest. Control column forces must allow precise inputs without causing arm fatigue over long flights. Switch placement must account for natural hand and finger movements. Even the direction controls move matters -- pulling back to climb feels intuitive because it aligns with how our bodies work. Poorly applied biomechanics leads to control reversals (where the pilot's instinctive movement is wrong), excessive fatigue, and repetitive strain injuries.
exam_tip: Biomechanics ensures controls require appropriate force, move in intuitive directions, and can be operated without fatigue or injury by the full range of qualified pilots.
key_facts: Control forces must be manageable for all qualified pilot sizes
Control movements must align with intuitive body responses
Poor biomechanics causes control reversals, fatigue and strain injuries
Applies to rudder pedals, control column, switches and knobs
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the relevance of anthropometry in the design of flight decks.
concept: Anthropometry -- the science of body measurements -- drives flight deck design more than any other ergonomic discipline. The cockpit needs to fit pilots ranging from a small-framed person to a large-framed person, and they all need to reach the controls, see the instruments, and maintain proper eye position for the external visual picture. Designers typically use the 5th to 95th percentile range, which means 90% of the population is accommodated, but 10% may struggle. Adjustable seats, rudder pedals, and sometimes even instrument panels help bridge the gap. In NZ, the pilot population is diverse, and GA aircraft with fixed cockpit geometry can create real challenges for pilots at the extremes of the anthropometric range.
exam_tip: Anthropometry determines whether pilots of different body sizes can reach controls, see instruments and maintain correct eye position. Design uses 5th-95th percentile range.
key_facts: Drives cockpit layout based on body dimension measurements
Design accommodates 5th to 95th percentile of pilot population
Adjustable seats and pedals bridge the gap for different body sizes
Critical for reach, visibility and maintaining correct eye datum position
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of a poorly designed cockpit on pilot performance.
concept: A poorly designed cockpit forces the pilot to fight the machine instead of flying it. The effects cascade: controls that are hard to reach increase workload and distract from monitoring. Instruments placed outside the normal scan pattern get missed. Poorly positioned switches lead to inadvertent selections. Uncomfortable seating accelerates fatigue. Poor lighting causes eye strain and missed information. Non-intuitive control logic creates error traps -- if pushing a button up means 'decrease,' that's a design-induced error waiting to happen. Over time, these design deficiencies normalise: pilots develop workarounds and compensating habits, which themselves become error sources. The net effect is a pilot spending cognitive resources on cockpit management that should be available for situational awareness and decision-making.
exam_tip: Poor cockpit design increases workload, creates error traps, accelerates fatigue and diverts cognitive resources from situational awareness to cockpit management.
key_facts: Hard-to-reach controls increase workload and distract from monitoring
Poorly placed instruments get missed in the scan
Non-intuitive control logic creates design-induced error traps
Pilots develop workarounds that themselves become error sources
Cognitive resources diverted from SA to cockpit management
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of eye datum or eye design position.
concept: The eye datum (or eye design position) is the specific point in space where the designer intends the pilot's eyes to be. Everything in the cockpit is designed around this point -- instrument readability, external visibility over the nose, alignment of visual approach aids like PAPI/VASI, and the relationship between the glareshield and the outside world. If your eyes aren't at the design eye position, instruments may be obscured by the glareshield, the outside visual picture changes, and approach aids give misleading guidance. This is why proper seat adjustment is so important -- it's not about comfort alone, it's about putting your eyes where the designer intended them to be. Many NZ training aircraft have reference marks on the windscreen or cockpit structure to help pilots find the correct eye position.
exam_tip: Eye datum is the designed eye position around which all cockpit visibility is optimised. Incorrect eye position affects instrument readability, outside visibility and visual approach aid interpretation.
key_facts: The specific point in space where the designer intends the pilot's eyes to be
All cockpit visibility designed around this position
Affects instrument readability, glareshield clearance and outside visibility
Incorrect position causes PAPI/VASI misinterpretation
Proper seat adjustment is critical to achieving correct eye datum
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the problems associated with windshield design and visibility.
concept: The windshield is your window to the outside world, and its design creates more problems than you'd expect. Curved windshields distort the visual picture -- objects may appear closer or further away, higher or lower than they actually are. Scratches, crazing, and contamination scatter light, creating glare in sunny conditions and reducing visibility in rain. Multiple panes can create double images, especially at night. The frame structure creates blind spots -- and in some aircraft, these blind spots are large enough to hide an approaching aircraft during a lookout scan. Rain on the windshield degrades visibility dramatically, and not all aircraft have effective wipers or rain repellent. Bird strike resistance requirements add thickness and layers, which can increase distortion. These are all known design compromises that pilots need to understand and account for.
exam_tip: Windshield problems: distortion from curvature, glare from scratches/crazing, blind spots from frames, rain degradation, double images from multiple panes. Pilots must account for these limitations.
key_facts: Curved surfaces distort visual picture -- objects appear displaced
Scratches and crazing create glare and scatter light
Frame structure creates blind spots that can hide approaching traffic
Rain dramatically degrades visibility; wipers may be inadequate
Multiple panes can create double images at night
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of advanced cockpit automation, including: failure to monitor.
concept: Automation is brilliant at flying the aircraft precisely -- and that's exactly the problem. When the autopilot is doing all the work, the human tendency is to stop watching. Monitoring a system that almost never fails is one of the hardest tasks humans face, because our brains are wired for novelty and change, not for watching something stay the same hour after hour. This monitoring failure means that when the automation does something unexpected -- a mode change, a deviation, a degradation -- the crew may not notice for a dangerously long time. In the worst cases, the crew's mental model of what the automation is doing diverges from what it's actually doing, and by the time they notice, the recovery time is critically short.
exam_tip: Humans are poor at monitoring systems that rarely fail. Automation monitoring degrades over time, leading to delayed detection of automation anomalies and divergent mental models.
key_facts: Human brains are wired for novelty, not sustained monitoring of stable systems
Monitoring effectiveness degrades over long periods of normal operation
Crew mental model can diverge from actual automation state
Delayed detection of anomalies reduces recovery time
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of advanced cockpit automation, including: boredom and complacency.
concept: Boredom and complacency are the twin byproducts of automation that does its job well. Boredom sets in during long cruise sectors where the automation handles everything and the crew has nothing actively demanding their attention. Complacency develops over time -- after hundreds of flights where the automation performed flawlessly, the crew's vigilance naturally drops. 'It always works fine' becomes the unspoken assumption. The combination is particularly dangerous because complacent crew members don't just monitor less -- they also become slower to recognise and respond when things do go wrong. Their skills for manual handling may have degraded, and their awareness of the automation's current mode and target may be vague. It's a paradox: the better the automation works, the more vulnerable the human becomes.
exam_tip: Better automation performance creates worse monitoring. Boredom reduces alertness; complacency reduces vigilance and degrades manual skills. The automation paradox: reliability breeds vulnerability.
key_facts: Boredom from lack of active demands during automated flight
Complacency from repeated flawless automation performance
Degraded recognition of and response to automation anomalies
Manual handling skills atrophy from lack of practice
The automation paradox: better reliability creates worse human performance
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of advanced cockpit automation, including: loss of proficiency.
concept: Use it or lose it -- that old saying applies perfectly to flying skills in an automated cockpit. When the autopilot flies 95% of every flight, the pilot's manual flying skills inevitably erode. Hand-flying an ILS to minimums, managing a raw-data approach, recovering from unusual attitudes -- these all require practice that automated flight doesn't provide. The loss isn't just motor skills; it's the cognitive skills too. Mental arithmetic for descent planning, mental picture of the aircraft's energy state, instinctive recognition of deviations from the desired flight path. Some operators mandate regular hand-flying practice for exactly this reason. In NZ training, the transition from PPL (where you hand-fly everything) to CPL/ATPL environments (where automation does much of the work) is where this proficiency erosion can begin.
exam_tip: Automation erodes both manual flying skills and cognitive skills (mental arithmetic, energy management, deviation recognition). Regular hand-flying practice is essential to maintain proficiency.
key_facts: Manual flying skills erode when automation handles most flying
Cognitive skills also degrade: mental arithmetic, energy management
Loss of instinctive recognition of flight path deviations
Some operators mandate regular hand-flying practice
Proficiency loss is most dangerous during automation failures requiring manual takeover
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of advanced cockpit automation, including: job satisfaction.
concept: Here's an aspect of automation that doesn't get enough attention: what happens to a pilot's job satisfaction when the machine does most of the flying? Many pilots became pilots because they love to fly -- and 'fly' means hands on controls, managing the aircraft, making decisions. When automation reduces the pilot's role to button-pushing, mode selection, and monitoring, some of that satisfaction evaporates. Reduced job satisfaction isn't just a morale issue -- it has safety implications. A disengaged pilot monitors less effectively, takes less pride in their work, and may become less committed to maintaining currency and skills. Conversely, pilots who find ways to stay engaged with the automation -- understanding it deeply, managing it skillfully -- tend to maintain higher satisfaction and performance.
exam_tip: Automation can reduce job satisfaction by removing the active flying role. Disengaged pilots monitor less effectively and maintain skills less diligently -- a direct safety concern.
key_facts: Automation reduces the active flying role that attracts many to the profession
Reduced satisfaction leads to reduced engagement and monitoring effectiveness
Disengaged pilots maintain currency and skills less diligently
Deep understanding and skilful management of automation can maintain engagement
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of advanced cockpit automation, including: crew coordination.
concept: Automation changes how crews coordinate in ways that aren't always obvious. When both pilots are hand-flying or closely managing the aircraft, there's a natural flow of communication -- callouts, cross-checks, verbal confirmations. With automation, the workload drops and so does the communication. Crew members may retreat into their own tasks (or their own thoughts), reducing the shared situational awareness that keeps operations safe. Mode changes made by one crew member may not be communicated to or understood by the other. The PF-PM (pilot flying/pilot monitoring) roles can become blurred when neither person is actively flying. Effective crew coordination with automation requires deliberate verbalisation of automation inputs, shared monitoring strategies, and clear role delineation even when the autopilot is engaged.
exam_tip: Automation reduces natural communication flow between crew. Effective coordination requires deliberate verbalisation of inputs, shared monitoring strategies and clear role delineation.
key_facts: Reduced workload leads to reduced communication between crew
Mode changes may not be communicated or understood by both pilots
PF/PM roles can blur when neither is actively flying
Requires deliberate verbalisation and shared monitoring strategies
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of advanced cockpit automation, including: problems associated with equipment failure.
concept: When automation fails, the workload doesn't just return to normal -- it spikes. The crew now has to take over the tasks the automation was handling, diagnose what failed, figure out what the automation was doing when it failed, and manage the aircraft state that the failure may have left them in. If the crew has been relying on automation for a long period, they may need time to rebuild their situational awareness, reacquire manual flying proficiency, and adjust to the increased workload -- all under time pressure. Partial failures are even more insidious than total ones, because the automation may continue to function in some respects while giving incorrect information or commands in others. The crew has to identify which parts of the automation they can still trust.
exam_tip: Automation failure causes workload spike, not just return to baseline. Partial failures are particularly dangerous -- crew must determine which automation outputs remain trustworthy.
key_facts: Workload spikes beyond normal manual flying levels during automation failure
Crew must simultaneously take over, diagnose and manage aircraft state
Partial failures are more insidious -- some functions work, others don't
SA must be rebuilt after prolonged reliance on automation
Time pressure compounds all these challenges
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe elements of coping behaviour associated with automatic cockpits.
concept: Pilots develop coping behaviours to deal with the demands (and the monotony) of automated cockpits. Some are helpful: developing a structured scanning routine for the automation displays, creating personal crosscheck habits, or deliberately hand-flying during non-critical phases to maintain skills. Others are not: reading material unrelated to the flight, becoming absorbed in tablet devices, engaging in extended non-operational conversation, or developing an over-reliance on the automation that leaves you unprepared for failure. Some pilots cope with the monitoring challenge by deliberately increasing their interaction with the automation -- making small adjustments, checking different display modes, reviewing the flight plan. The key is that coping strategies should maintain engagement and readiness, not further disconnect the pilot from the operation.
exam_tip: Helpful coping: structured scanning, personal crosschecks, deliberate hand-flying practice. Harmful coping: non-operational distractions, over-reliance, disengagement from monitoring.
key_facts: Helpful: structured scanning, personal crosschecks, hand-flying practice
Harmful: non-operational reading/devices, over-reliance, mental disengagement
Increased automation interaction maintains engagement
Coping strategies should maintain readiness, not increase disconnection
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in control design: shape/recognition by touch.
concept: Shape coding -- making controls identifiable by touch alone -- is one of the most elegant solutions in flight deck design. The classic example is landing gear and flap levers: the gear lever is shaped like a wheel, the flap lever like a flap. You can identify them without looking, which means you can keep your eyes outside or on the instruments during critical phases. This matters most when workload is high and you're reaching for a control by feel. If controls feel the same, you risk selecting the wrong one -- and the consequences can be severe. Think about the difference between accidentally selecting the flap lever versus the gear lever during a go-around. Shape coding eliminates that ambiguity at the tactile level.
exam_tip: Shape/touch coding allows control identification without visual reference -- gear lever shaped like a wheel, flap lever like a flap. Prevents wrong-control selection during high-workload phases.
key_facts: Controls identifiable by touch alone through distinctive shapes
Classic example: gear lever (wheel shape), flap lever (flap shape)
Allows eyes to remain on instruments or outside during control selection
Prevents critical wrong-control errors during high-workload phases
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in control design: location.
concept: Where a control is located in the cockpit directly affects how quickly and accurately a pilot can operate it. Frequently used controls need to be within easy reach from the normal flying position. Emergency controls need to be accessible quickly and without fumbling. Controls used together should be grouped together. Critical controls should be separated from each other so you can't accidentally select the wrong one. Location also establishes expectation -- pilots expect throttle controls to be in the centre console or overhead, trim controls near the control column, and electrical switches on a panel. When a control is in an unexpected location, pilots default to where they expect it to be, especially under stress. That's when location design errors become dangerous.
exam_tip: Control location determines access speed and accuracy. Frequently used = easy reach. Emergency = quick access. Related controls grouped. Critical controls separated. Unexpected locations cause errors under stress.
key_facts: Frequently used controls within easy reach of normal flying position
Emergency controls quickly accessible without fumbling
Related controls grouped; critical controls separated
Pilots default to expected locations under stress -- non-standard placement is dangerous
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in control design: layout and the uniformity of spatial arrangement.
concept: Layout uniformity means that similar controls are arranged in the same pattern across different aircraft types. If the engine instruments always read left-to-right matching the engine order, if switches always flip up for 'on,' if circuit breaker panels follow the same logical grouping, then a pilot transitioning between aircraft types carries their muscle memory and scan patterns with them. When layout isn't uniform, error rates skyrocket during type transitions. This is why standardisation matters in aviation -- ICAO and national regulators push for common layouts precisely because pilots regularly operate different aircraft types. In NZ GA, you might fly a Cessna 172 in the morning and a Piper Archer in the afternoon, and non-standard layouts between the two become a genuine error trap.
exam_tip: Uniform layout allows muscle memory and scan patterns to transfer between aircraft types. Non-standard layouts cause errors during type transitions, especially in NZ GA where pilots fly multiple types.
key_facts: Consistent spatial arrangement allows skill transfer between aircraft types
Muscle memory and scan patterns rely on layout uniformity
Non-standard layouts cause errors during type transitions
ICAO and regulators push for common cockpit layouts for this reason
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in control design: direction of movement.
concept: The direction a control moves should match what the pilot intuitively expects. Pull back to go up. Push throttle forward to increase power. Rotate a knob clockwise to increase. These population stereotypes are deeply ingrained, and when a control moves opposite to expectation, errors are inevitable -- especially under time pressure or stress when pilots revert to instinct. The classic example is the reversal error: in some older aircraft, the fuel selector rotates in the opposite direction to what's intuitive, leading to pilots selecting the wrong tank. Direction of movement also applies to automation interfaces -- a knob that increases a value when turned left violates the clockwise-to-increase convention and will generate errors. Good design follows established conventions; bad design creates error traps.
exam_tip: Controls must move in the direction the pilot intuitively expects (population stereotypes). Pull back = up, forward = more power, clockwise = increase. Reversals cause errors under stress.
key_facts: Must follow population stereotypes: pull back = up, forward = more
Clockwise rotation should mean increase
Reversal errors occur when controls move opposite to expectation
Under stress, pilots revert to instinctive movements regardless of actual design
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in control design: visibility.
concept: A control you can't see is a control you're more likely to misidentify or mis-operate. Visibility in control design means the pilot can see the control's position, its label, and any associated status indication from the normal seated position. This is particularly important for controls that have multiple discrete positions -- a three-position switch where you can't see whether it's in the middle or top position is an error trap. Visibility also applies to the feedback the control provides -- can you visually confirm the control has moved to the intended position? At night, backlighting and illuminated labels become critical. Controls hidden behind other equipment, obscured by the control column, or placed in shadowed areas of the panel are design flaws that increase error probability.
exam_tip: Control visibility means pilots can see position, labels and status indication from normal seated position. Controls hidden, obscured or in shadows increase error probability. Night lighting is critical.
key_facts: Pilot must see control position, label and status from normal seated position
Multi-position switches need clear visual indication of current state
Night operations require backlighting and illuminated labels
Hidden or obscured controls increase misidentification and error probability
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in the design of instrumentation, displays and alerts: size.
concept: Size matters in instrument design -- literally. An instrument that's too small to read at arm's length from the normal seated position is going to generate errors, especially under turbulence, at night, or when the pilot is fatigued. But bigger isn't always better either: oversized instruments consume panel space that could be used for other critical displays. The key is that the display must be large enough for its most critical information to be readable at a glance, without requiring the pilot to lean forward or squint. This is particularly relevant with modern glass cockpits, where a wealth of information is packed onto screens -- if the symbology or text is too small, critical flight data can be missed in the clutter.
exam_tip: Instrument size must allow critical information to be read at a glance from normal position. Too small = errors; too large = wasted space. Glass cockpits risk information clutter on limited screen area.
key_facts: Must be readable at a glance from normal seated position
Too small causes errors, especially under turbulence, at night, or when fatigued
Glass cockpits pack dense information -- symbology/text size is critical
Size must balance readability against available panel space
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in the design of instrumentation, displays and alerts: position.
concept: Where an instrument sits on the panel determines how often and how accurately it gets scanned. Primary flight instruments need to be directly in front of the pilot, in the primary field of vision, requiring minimal eye movement from the outside world. Secondary instruments can be further out. Engine instruments are typically grouped centrally where both crew members can see them. The problem arises when critical information is placed in a position that falls outside the pilot's normal scan -- it simply gets missed. In NZ GA aircraft, the limited panel space sometimes forces compromises, putting important instruments in peripheral locations. Understanding which instruments are in sub-optimal positions in your aircraft helps you build a deliberate scan to compensate.
exam_tip: Primary flight instruments must be in the direct line of sight. Information outside the normal scan pattern gets missed. Know which instruments in your aircraft are in sub-optimal positions.
key_facts: Primary flight instruments directly in front, minimal eye movement required
Information outside normal scan pattern is frequently missed
Engine instruments typically grouped centrally for both crew
GA aircraft may force compromises -- know your aircraft's limitations
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in the design of instrumentation, displays and alerts: layout.
concept: Instrument layout follows the principle that related information should be grouped together and arranged in a logical pattern. The classic 'basic T' arrangement puts the attitude indicator at the centre top, airspeed indicator to the left, altimeter to the right, and heading indicator below -- because these four primary instruments form the core of the instrument scan. Engine instruments are grouped so all gauges for one engine are together, and normal operating ranges are at the same clock position across all gauges (so a quick glance shows if anything is out of line). Poor layout forces the pilot to hunt for information, increases scan time, and makes it harder to detect abnormal indications. Glass cockpits offer layout flexibility, which is both an advantage (customisation) and a risk (non-standard presentations).
exam_tip: Logical layout groups related information and arranges it for efficient scanning. The 'basic T' arrangement is the standard for primary flight instruments. Glass cockpits offer flexibility but risk non-standard layouts.
key_facts: Related information grouped together in logical patterns
Basic T: AI centre, ASI left, ALT right, HI below
Engine gauges aligned so normal ranges are at same clock position
Poor layout increases scan time and delays abnormality detection
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in the design of instrumentation, displays and alerts: visibility.
concept: An instrument you can't see clearly is an instrument you'll misread. Visibility in display design covers several factors: can the pilot see the instrument from their normal position without leaning or craning? Is the display free from glare, reflections and shadows? Can it be read in all lighting conditions -- bright sunlight, night flying, and everything in between? Are the markings and colours distinct enough to distinguish between different indications? Parallax error is a visibility issue too: analogue instruments read differently depending on viewing angle, which matters when the pilot monitoring reads the pilot flying's instruments from an offset position. Modern screens face the sunlight readability challenge -- many LCD displays wash out in direct sunlight, making critical flight data unreadable at exactly the wrong moment.
exam_tip: Display visibility must work in all lighting conditions. Issues: glare, reflections, parallax error, sunlight washout of LCD screens. If you can't read it clearly, you'll misread it.
key_facts: Must be visible from normal position without leaning or craning
Free from glare, reflections and shadows in all lighting conditions
Parallax error affects analogue instruments viewed from offset positions
LCD screens can wash out in direct sunlight, making data unreadable
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in the design of instrumentation, displays and alerts: legibility.
concept: Legibility is about whether the markings, numbers and text on a display can actually be read quickly and accurately. Font size, font style, line thickness, contrast against the background, and spacing between characters all affect legibility. A crowded display with thin lines and small text might contain all the information you need, but if you can't read it quickly, it's useless in a cockpit environment. Legibility degrades under vibration (the numbers blur), under fatigue (visual acuity drops), and at the edges of the visual field (resolution decreases). Good design provides adequate size, clear contrast, and appropriate font choices. Poor legibility is particularly dangerous on approach plates and charts where misreading a number can put you on the wrong altitude or heading.
exam_tip: Legibility depends on font size, contrast, spacing and line thickness. Degrades under vibration, fatigue and peripheral viewing. Misreading numbers on charts is a direct safety hazard.
key_facts: Determined by font size, style, contrast, spacing and line thickness
Degrades under vibration, fatigue and peripheral viewing
Crowded displays with thin lines and small text generate misreading errors
Particularly critical on approach plates where misread numbers have severe consequences
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in the design of instrumentation, displays and alerts: scale.
concept: Scale design determines how precisely a pilot can read a value from an instrument. A scale that covers too wide a range on a small dial means each graduation represents a large value -- making precise readings difficult. A scale that covers too narrow a range may not show the full picture. Linear scales, circular scales, and digital readouts each have advantages: circular scales are good for showing trends and rates of change (you can see the needle moving), while digital readouts are precise for specific values but poor for showing trends. The choice of scale range and graduation also matters: an altimeter with 20-foot graduations is more precise than one with 50-foot graduations, but may be harder to read quickly. Good scale design balances precision with readability.
exam_tip: Scale design balances range coverage with reading precision. Circular scales show trends well; digital readouts give precise values but poor trend information. Graduation size affects readability.
key_facts: Wide range on small dial = poor precision; narrow range = incomplete picture
Circular scales good for trends and rate of change
Digital readouts precise but poor for trend recognition
Graduation size must balance precision with quick readability
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in the design of instrumentation, displays and alerts: use of colour.
concept: Colour coding is one of the most powerful tools in instrument design -- when used well, it allows instant recognition of normal, caution and warning states without reading any text. Green for normal range, yellow/amber for caution, red for danger -- this convention is universal in aviation and leverages our deep-seated colour associations. But colour has limitations: about 8% of males have some degree of colour vision deficiency, so colour should never be the only coding method. Colour also loses effectiveness in certain lighting conditions -- red markings disappear under red cockpit lighting. Too many colours create confusion rather than clarity. Good design uses colour consistently, sparingly, and always in combination with another coding method (shape, position, or text).
exam_tip: Colour coding enables instant recognition of states (green = normal, amber = caution, red = danger). Never the sole coding method due to colour vision deficiency. Must be consistent and not overused.
key_facts: Green = normal, amber/yellow = caution, red = danger (universal convention)
~8% of males have colour vision deficiency -- never use colour as sole code
Red markings can disappear under red cockpit lighting
Must be consistent, sparingly used, and paired with another coding method
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in the design of instrumentation, displays and alerts: illumination.
concept: Illumination of instruments and displays needs to work across the full range of lighting conditions a pilot faces -- from brilliant tropical sunlight at FL350 to a pitch-black night approach into a rural NZ strip with no runway lighting. Internal illumination must be adjustable: too bright at night and it destroys dark adaptation, too dim and critical information is unreadable. Flood lighting, integral lighting, and post lighting each have different characteristics. Modern glass cockpits add screen backlighting to the equation, which needs independent control from panel lighting. The transition between looking at lit instruments and peering into darkness outside is one of the most challenging visual tasks in aviation, and good illumination design minimises the adaptation demand this transition creates.
exam_tip: Illumination must be adjustable across all conditions -- sunlight to pitch black. Too bright at night destroys dark adaptation; too dim makes information unreadable. Independent control for different light sources.
key_facts: Must work from bright sunlight to pitch-black night conditions
Too bright at night destroys dark adaptation; too dim = unreadable
Different lighting types: flood, integral, post, screen backlight
Each light source needs independent brightness control
Minimising adaptation demand between instruments and outside view is critical
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe potential errors in the interpretation of three pointer altimeters.
concept: The three-pointer altimeter is one of aviation's most notorious error traps. It displays altitude using three needles of different sizes: a long needle for hundreds of feet, a medium needle for thousands, and a small needle for tens of thousands. The problem is that under time pressure or during rapid altitude changes, pilots misread which pointer is which, leading to altitude errors of exactly 10,000 feet. You think you're at 10,500 feet when you're actually at 500 feet -- or vice versa. The design violates a basic human factors principle: the most important information (the 10,000-foot pointer) is displayed on the smallest, least visible needle. Numerous incidents and accidents have been attributed to three-pointer altimeter misreading. Modern drum-and-counter altimeters largely solve this problem, but three-pointer instruments still exist in older NZ GA aircraft.
exam_tip: Three-pointer altimeters cause 10,000-foot misreading errors because the most critical information is on the smallest needle. Particularly dangerous during rapid altitude changes and high workload.
key_facts: Three needles: long = hundreds, medium = thousands, small = tens of thousands
Most critical information (10,000s) on the smallest, least visible needle
Leads to altitude errors of exactly 10,000 feet
Numerous accidents attributed to this design flaw
Drum-and-counter altimeters largely solve the problem
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe potential errors in the interpretation of the artificial horizon.
concept: The artificial horizon (attitude indicator) can be misinterpreted in several ways. The most dangerous is reversal error -- interpreting a bank as being in the opposite direction, which happens when the pilot perceives the aircraft symbol as moving against a fixed horizon rather than the horizon moving behind a fixed aircraft. This is more likely during unusual attitudes, spatial disorientation, or when a pilot is transitioning from an aircraft with a different AI presentation. Some older designs use a moving-aircraft/fixed-horizon display while others use a fixed-aircraft/moving-horizon display, and switching between the two creates interpretation risk. Other errors include misreading pitch attitude when the horizon line is obscured by the miniature aircraft symbol, and confusion about whether a small brown area at the top means a slight nose-up or a massive bank.
exam_tip: AI errors: reversal error (misreading bank direction), confusion between moving-aircraft and moving-horizon presentations, and pitch misreading during unusual attitudes.
key_facts: Reversal error: perceiving bank in the opposite direction
More likely during unusual attitudes and spatial disorientation
Different AI presentations (moving-aircraft vs moving-horizon) create transition risk
Pitch attitude can be misread when horizon line is obscured
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe problems associated with the presentation and misinterpretation of alerts.
concept: Alerting systems are only useful if the crew can interpret and respond to them correctly. Problems arise in several areas. Alert proliferation -- too many alerts firing simultaneously during a complex failure, overwhelming the crew with information when they can least process it. Nuisance alerts -- warnings that trigger inappropriately and train crews to ignore them (the 'cry wolf' effect). Ambiguous alerts -- indications that could mean multiple things, forcing the crew to diagnose before they can act. Poorly prioritised alerts -- when minor cautions compete for attention with critical warnings. Alert fatigue -- the cumulative effect of too many alerts over time, reducing the crew's response to all of them. Good alert design is prioritised, clear, actionable, and minimises false positives.
exam_tip: Alert problems: proliferation (too many at once), nuisance alerts (cry wolf effect), ambiguity, poor prioritisation, and alert fatigue. Good design is clear, prioritised, actionable and minimises false positives.
key_facts: Alert proliferation overwhelms crew during complex failures
Nuisance alerts create 'cry wolf' effect -- crews learn to ignore them
Ambiguous alerts force diagnosis before action
Poor prioritisation lets minor cautions compete with critical warnings
Alert fatigue reduces response effectiveness over time
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe how colour coding conventions are used in aviation instruments, displays and navigation charts.
concept: Colour coding in aviation follows well-established conventions that pilots learn to read instinctively. On instruments: green arcs indicate normal operating range, yellow arcs indicate caution ranges, red lines mark never-exceed or minimum limits, and white arcs indicate specific operating ranges (like flap operating speed). On navigation charts: blue indicates water features, green indicates lower terrain, brown/yellow indicates higher terrain, and magenta is used for tracks and bearings. On approach plates: similar colour conventions apply to terrain depiction and obstacle marking. On glass cockpit displays: colour conventions for flight path, terrain, traffic and weather alerts follow standardised schemes. The power of consistent colour coding is that it enables pre-attentive processing -- you spot the red before you consciously read the number.
exam_tip: Instrument arcs: green = normal, yellow = caution, red = limits, white = specific ranges. Charts: blue = water, green = low terrain, brown = high terrain, magenta = tracks. Consistent coding enables pre-attentive recognition.
key_facts: Instruments: green = normal, yellow = caution, red = limits, white = specific ranges
Charts: blue = water, green = low terrain, brown/yellow = high terrain, magenta = tracks
Glass cockpits follow standardised colour schemes for flight data, terrain, traffic
Consistent colour coding enables pre-attentive processing -- spot anomalies before reading values
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the elements of an effective checklist.
concept: An effective checklist is short, clear and actually usable in the cockpit environment. The elements that make it work: logical sequencing that follows the natural flow of the task, clear and unambiguous language, items grouped by phase of flight, a manageable number of items per section (research suggests no more than 7-9 items per grouping), and a clear challenge-and-response format for multi-crew operations. The layout matters: adequate font size, good contrast, minimal clutter, and critical items highlighted. It should be possible to complete the checklist without losing your place, even if interrupted. Importantly, an effective checklist distinguishes between 'do' lists (used to perform actions in sequence) and 'confirm' lists (used to verify actions already completed from memory).
exam_tip: Effective checklists: logical sequence, clear language, grouped by phase, 7-9 items per group, challenge-and-response format, adequate font size. Distinguish 'do' lists from 'confirm' lists.
key_facts: Logical sequencing following natural task flow
Clear, unambiguous language with challenge-and-response format
Grouped by phase, no more than 7-9 items per grouping
Adequate font size, good contrast, minimal clutter
Two types: 'do' lists (perform actions) and 'confirm' lists (verify completed actions)
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Identify the phases of flight that a checklist plays an important role.
concept: Checklists play a role in every phase of flight, but they're particularly critical at transition points where the aircraft configuration or operation changes. Pre-flight: verifying the aircraft is airworthy and properly configured. Before start: ensuring systems are set for engine start. After start: confirming all systems are responding normally. Before taxi: navigation and communication setup. Before takeoff: final configuration check -- this is the last line of defence before you're committed. Climb, cruise, descent: configuration changes verified. Before landing: gear, flaps, speeds confirmed. After landing: securing the aircraft appropriately. Shutdown: final systems secured. Each phase transition is a potential error point, and the checklist exists to catch what your busy brain might miss.
exam_tip: Checklists are critical at every phase transition: pre-flight, before/after start, taxi, takeoff, climb, cruise, descent, before/after landing, shutdown. Each transition is a potential error point.
key_facts: Critical at phase transitions where configuration or operation changes
Pre-flight, before/after start, taxi, takeoff, climb, cruise, descent
Before landing is particularly critical -- last defence before commitment
After landing and shutdown complete the cycle
Each transition is a potential error point the checklist guards against
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the reasons for checklist complacency.
concept: Checklist complacency creeps in because checklists are repetitive by nature -- and humans are wired to stop paying attention to things that never change. After 500 flights where every item on the before-takeoff checklist was exactly where it should be, the 501st check becomes a mechanical exercise rather than a genuine verification. Pilots develop a rhythm of challenge-and-response that sounds right but doesn't involve actually looking at what's being checked. Time pressure amplifies this -- when you're running late, the checklist feels like an obstacle rather than a safety tool. Familiarity with the aircraft makes it worse: 'I know this aircraft, I don't need to actually check that.' The most dangerous form is when a crew develops a shared complacency, where both members are going through the motions without either one genuinely verifying.
exam_tip: Checklist complacency arises from repetition, time pressure, familiarity and routine. The most dangerous form is shared crew complacency where neither member genuinely verifies.
key_facts: Repetition makes checks mechanical rather than genuine verification
Time pressure makes checklists feel like obstacles
Aircraft familiarity creates false confidence ('I know this aircraft')
Shared crew complacency: both going through motions, neither verifying
Challenge-and-response rhythm can mask lack of actual checking
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe the possible ramifications of checklist complacency.
concept: The ramifications of checklist complacency can range from embarrassing to fatal. At the mild end: taking off with the fuel selector on the wrong tank, departure with the transponder still on standby, or taxiing with the pitot heat off in icing conditions. At the severe end: gear-up landings because the before-landing check was a formality, takeoff with flight controls locked because nobody actually verified 'controls free and correct,' or configuration errors that lead to loss of control. Several major accidents have been directly attributed to checklist items being called 'complete' without being checked. The insidious thing about complacency is that it builds slowly through successful outcomes -- every time nothing goes wrong despite a superficial check, the behaviour is reinforced.
exam_tip: Ramifications range from minor (wrong tank, transponder off) to fatal (gear-up landing, locked controls, configuration errors). Complacency is reinforced by successful outcomes despite superficial checks.
key_facts: Mild: wrong fuel tank, transponder on standby, pitot heat off
Severe: gear-up landing, locked flight controls, configuration errors
Several major accidents attributed to items called complete without checking
Complacency reinforced by successful outcomes despite superficial checking
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of colour, font and type size for written checklists.
concept: The physical design of a written checklist directly affects how reliably it gets used. Colour helps distinguish between different phases (blue for taxi, green for takeoff, amber for emergency) and highlights critical items. Font choice matters: sans-serif fonts are generally more legible in cockpit conditions, and clear distinction between similar characters (0 vs O, 1 vs l, 5 vs S) reduces misreading. Type size must be large enough to read under vibration, in dim lighting, and when fatigued -- which typically means larger than you'd use in an office document. Contrast between text and background is just as important as size. Poor checklist design leads to items being skipped because they're hard to read, phases being confused because they all look the same, and critical items being missed because they don't stand out visually.
exam_tip: Checklist design: colour distinguishes phases and highlights critical items. Sans-serif fonts improve legibility. Type size must work under vibration, dim light and fatigue. Poor design leads to skipped items.
key_facts: Colour codes different phases and highlights critical items
Sans-serif fonts more legible in cockpit conditions
Type size must account for vibration, dim lighting and fatigue
Good contrast between text and background is essential
Poor design causes skipped items, phase confusion and missed critical checks
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe problems associated with the design and use of checklists and manuals.
concept: Even well-intentioned checklists and manuals can create problems if they're poorly designed. Common issues include: checklists that are too long (causing fatigue and item-skipping), language that's ambiguous or inconsistent, items that don't match the actual cockpit layout or procedure sequence, emergency checklists that are too complex to follow under stress, and manuals that are so voluminous that finding critical information in flight is impractical. Another common problem is a disconnect between the checklist and actual line operations -- procedures written by people who haven't flown the aircraft in years may not reflect cockpit reality. In NZ GA, operators sometimes use manufacturer checklists that haven't been adapted for local conditions, or create their own checklists without applying human factors principles to the design.
exam_tip: Checklist problems: too long, ambiguous language, doesn't match cockpit layout, emergency procedures too complex, manuals too voluminous. Disconnect between written procedure and line operations is common.
key_facts: Too-long checklists cause fatigue and item-skipping
Ambiguous language leads to inconsistent execution
Procedures may not match actual cockpit layout or sequence
Emergency checklists may be too complex for high-stress use
Disconnect between written procedures and actual line operations
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe problems associated with the design and use of maps and charts.
concept: Maps and charts pack enormous amounts of information into a small space, and that creates human factors challenges. Clutter is the primary enemy -- when every piece of information competes for attention, critical items get lost. Scale misinterpretation leads to distance and position errors. Symbol confusion arises when too many different symbols are used or when symbols are too similar to each other. Orientation errors occur when the chart isn't aligned with the aircraft's heading. Folding a large chart in a small cockpit while trying to fly is a physical challenge that can distract from primary flight duties. Currency is another issue -- an outdated chart with incorrect frequencies, altitudes or airspace boundaries is dangerous. With electronic charts on tablets, new problems emerge: screen size limitations, glare, battery dependency, and the temptation to zoom in so far you lose the big picture.
exam_tip: Chart problems: clutter, scale misinterpretation, symbol confusion, orientation errors, physical handling in cockpit, currency. Electronic charts add screen size, glare and battery concerns.
key_facts: Clutter: too much information competing for attention
Scale misinterpretation leads to distance and position errors
Symbol confusion when too many or too similar symbols used
Orientation errors when chart not aligned with aircraft heading
Electronic charts: screen size, glare, battery dependency, loss of big picture when zoomed
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, Hawkins -- Human Factors in Flight, Campbell & Bagshaw -- Human Performance & Limitations
